CONTRACT DESCRIPTION:                         Courtroom Technology and Misc A/V Systems 
CONTRACT #                                                 16069 
CONTRACT PERIOD:                                    July 12, 2019 through July 11, 2020 
 
 
 
  
Dear Joe: 
 
Lake County’s current contract with Conference Technologies, Inc. expires July 11, 2020.  Based on performance and 
continuing need, the County has extended this contract for the following one (1) year period of July 12, 2020 through July 11, 
2021.  All the terms and conditions of the contract shall apply. 
 
It is the responsibility of Conference Technologies, Inc. to maintain a valid Certificate of Insurance (COI) in compliance with the 
terms and conditions of the contract. Upon acceptance of this renewal, contractors should work with their broker to obtain a 
new COI evidencing coverage for the renewal period of the contract. A valid company contact email address along with the new 
certificate(s) should be submitted to purchasing@lakecountyil.gov. Any hard copies of said Notices and Certificates of Insurance 
shall be provided to: Lake County Purchasing Division, 18 N. County St. – 9th Fl., Waukegan, IL 60085‐4350. 
 
Effective January 2019 the Lake County Board implemented a Vendor Disclosure Statement Policy, which require vendors to 
disclose any familial relationships between a Lake County elected official, department director, deputy director, manager and  
owners, principals or officers of the vendor’s company as well as campaign contributions to County elected officials.  A copy of 
the Vendor Disclosure  Statement is attached to this renewal notice.  It is the responsibility of all vendors to disclose this 
information and provide an updated form at renewal.  Please notify Lake County Purchasing Division by submitting an updated 
Vendor Disclosure Statement to purchasing@lakecountyil.gov. 
 
Please accept this letter as formal notice to renew the agreement. If you have any concerns, please contact us no later than 30 
days prior to the expiration date to purchasing@lakecountyil.gov or fax to (847) 984‐5889. 
 
Thank you for your continued interest in doing business with Lake County. 
 
Sincerely, 
 
 
RuthAnne K. Hall 
Purchasing Agent

Vendors wishing to contract with Lake County for goods and services in an amount greater than $30,000 shall submit this form in 
advance of award. Vendors shall disclose: 
‐ 
A familial relationship between a Lake County elected official, department director, deputy director and 
manager and owners, principals, or officers of the vendor’s company. Familial relationship is defined as 
a spouse (including civil partner), child, stepchild, parent, stepparent, grandparent, in-laws (including 
parent, grandparent, sibling, or child), relatives and non-relatives living in the same residence, and 
offspring born to any aforementioned person. 
‐ 
All political campaign contributions made by the vendor or an owner, principal, officer, manager, lobbyist, 
agent, consultant, counsel, subcontractor or corporate entity under the control of the vendor to any county 
board member, county board chair, or countywide elected official as well as contributions to any political 
action committees within the last five years.

Dear James: 
 
You are hereby awarded Contract #18018 for the Lake County Hyland OnBase Software Maintenance. All of the terms 
and conditions of the Bid shall apply.   
 
Contractor shall provide Lake County with Certificates of Insurance evidencing the above‐required insurance, prior to 
commencement of this Agreement and thereafter with certificates evidencing renewals or replacements of said policies 
of insurance at least thirty (30) days prior to the expiration of cancellation of any such policies.  Failure to Comply:  In the 
event the proposer fails to obtain or maintain any insurance coverage’s required under this agreement, Lake County may 
purchase such insurance coverage and charge the expense to Contractor.  Any hard copies of said Notices and Certificates 
of Insurance shall be provided to:  Lake County Purchasing Division, 18 No. County St. – 9th Fl., Waukegan, IL 60085‐4350. 
 
This is not an order. Vendors shall make deliveries only as directed by purchase order. Invoices must reference purchase 
order number to ensure prompt payment.  
 
Thank you for your interest in doing business with Lake County. 
 
Sincerely, 
 
RuthAnne K. Hall 
Purchasing Agent

Dear James: 
 
Lake County’s current contract with Imagesoft, Inc. expires February 29, 2020.  Based on performance and 
continuing need, the County has extended this contract for the following one (1) year period of March 1, 2020 
through February 28, 2021.  All the terms and conditions of the contract shall apply. 
 
It is the responsibility of Imagesoft, Inc. to maintain a valid Certificate of Insurance in compliance with the terms and 
conditions of the contract. Certificates evidencing renewals or replacements of said policies of insurance at least 
thirty (30) days prior to the expiration of cancellation of any such policies. In the event the proposer fails to obtain 
or maintain any insurance coverages required under this agreement, Lake County may purchase such insurance 
coverage and charge the expense to Contractor. 
 
Lake County electronically monitors compliance of Certificate of Insurance requirements. Upon acceptance of this 
renewal, contractors should work with their broker to obtain a new Certificate of Insurance evidencing coverage for 
the renewal period of the contract. A valid company contact email address along with the new certificate(s) should 
be submitted to purchasing@lakecountyil.gov. Any hard copies of said Notices and Certificates of Insurance shall be 
provided to: Lake County Purchasing Division, 18 N. County St. – 9th Fl., Waukegan, IL 60085-4350. 
 
Please accept this letter as formal notice to renew the agreement. If you have any concerns, please contact us no 
later than 30 days prior to the expiration date to purchasing@lakecountyil.gov or fax to (847) 984-5889. 
 
Thank you for your continued interest in doing business with Lake County. 
 
Sincerely, 
 
 
RuthAnne K. Hall 
Purchasing Agent

FIRST AMENDMENT TO THE STATEMENT OF WORK NO. 1  
FOR CLERK DIGITIZATION, LAKE COUNTY CONTRACT #19028 
 
This First Amendment (the “Amendment”), dated this 22nd day of October, 2021 (the “Amendment Effective 
Date”), to Statement of Work No. 1 dated July 16, 2020 (“SOW”) which SOW is incorporated into and made part 
of the Agreement #19028, dated July 16, 2020 (“Agreement”) is entered into by and between Clerk of the Circuit 
Court of the Nineteenth Judicial Circuit (“the Clerk” or “Customer”) and HOV SERVICES INC., an Exela 
Technologies company (“Exela” or “Vendor”). 
WHEREAS, the parties desire to make amendments to SOW as described herein. 
NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the premises and other good and valuable consideration received by each 
party from the other, the sufficiency of which is hereby acknowledged, the parties hereby agree to amend the SOW 
as follows: 
A. 
Adding a one-time additional project for Offsite Record Digitization Services (Physical case file 
conversion services) on Exela’s premises as follows: 
1. The parties agree on a project plan for this one-time project as follows: 
i. The project, starting with point (A)(2), below, will begin upon the effective date of this 
Amendment. 
ii. After Exela delivers the first group of 250 boxes to the Clerk, the Clerk will attempt, using 
all reasonable efforts, to prepare the boxes for return to Exela within four (4) to six (6) 
weeks. The parties shall schedule all regular deliveries/returns for Thursdays, as has been 
their previous practice. If the Clerk will not be able to meet a deadline for preparing a 
group of boxes, the Clerk will provide Exela notice by the Monday before a scheduled 
pickup that an additional time is required.  
iii. The estimated timeframe for completing this one-time project shall be six (6) months. 
Contacts: primary contacts for the completion of this project shall be: 
Exela: Kevin Bormet. 
Clerk: Rebecca Cook/Richard Worth. 
2. Exela will audit all boxes containing case files stored at the Exela Rantoul facility beginning as of 
the Amendment Effective Date to identify all boxes that contain Multi Volume Case Files.  “Multi 
Volume Case Files” shall be defined as case numbers that were originally found in multiple folders 
(volumes).   
3. Exela will return boxes containing Multi Volume Case Files to the Clerk.  Exela will also return 
to the Clerk any boxes where a case number was split between boxes according to the manifest 
and/or markings on the outside of the boxes. Boxes not containing Multi Volume Case Files or 
cases split between boxes will be placed back into storage. 
4. Exela will transport the boxes it identifies as returnable to the Clerk back to the Clerk’s Office in 
groups of 250 boxes with the first group occurring within fourteen (14) business days of the 
Amendment effective date. 
5. When the Clerk’s employees complete their reconstitution of the files in a set of 250 boxes, Exela 
will deliver the next 250 boxes and will transport the previous set back to the Exela facility for 
processing at no additional cost to the Clerk.  This process will continue until all Multi Volume 
Case Files have been returned to the Clerk, reconstitute, and then returned to and rescanned by 
Exela. 
6. The Clerk will reconstitute the files and documents within each of the 250 boxes (without binding 
the documents together), and then return the boxes to Exela in groups of 250 boxes.  In each box 
will be a list of cases (made up of multiple folders) that need to be rescanned. A sample of that list 
is attached to this Amendment as Exhibit 1.  The Clerk will place the cases to be rescanned in the 

--- PAGE BREAK ---

Confidential – Not for disclosure 
Page 2 of 3 
front of each box, and a target will be placed behind the cases to be rescanned to signify that the 
cases behind the target do not need to be rescanned in this process.  The Clerk will place a 
completed case patch page at the beginning of each volume folder or brown folder. Exela will scan 
the cases to be rescanned in the exact order they are received from the Clerk while maintaining 
the integrity of the individual volumes that make up a single case. 
7. Exela will rescan the Multi Volume Case documents within the 250 box groups and generate 
new output pdf files.  The output pdfs will reflect both (a) the same physical sequence of 
documents as received within the boxes from the Clerk and (b) the same individual volume 
count. By way of example: 
 
20 CH 1234 
(A 3-volume file 
with two brown 
accordion folders)

(Newest 
case docs)



Order of scanning: Vol. 3 
Vol. 2 
Vol. 1 
1st Brown

NOTE: The volumes within a case file contain fewer than 999 pages, such that a volume should never need to be 
broken up into multiple pdf files. For other scans (“other” meaning folders that are not denoted as volumes), the 
pdf output files should indicate in DocDNA which output file is first (e.g., the first 999 pages), then which 
second (the second 999 pages), etc. (If volumes within a case file do need to be divided into multiple pdfs 
because of page limitations [>999 pages], then those volumes also need to be identifiable as first [e.g., the first 
999 pages], then second [the second 999 pages], etc.) 
8. Exela will remove the previous scans from DocDNA, upload the updated pdf files to DocDNA in 
way that will identify which pdf corresponds to which volume of a case file (or brown folder, as 
applicable), and then return the boxes to storage. 
9. Exela will perform the following quality assurance process to ascertain that files are being scanned 
correctly, uploaded to DocDNA, and displaying correctly in DocDNA: 
i. Review all thumbnail views of the images looking for skews, cut-offs, double feeds, etc.  
Note, this is not considered 100% image review quality assurance. 
ii. Verify each patch reads and separates the cases. 
iii. Verify each patch matches the case number. 
iv. Verify that all scans are imported into DocDNA.  
10. Exela will perform an audit of 100% of the case numbers within DocDNA using the list provided 
by the Clerk (Exhibit 1) to verify that (a) each case number has the correct number of volumes 
reflected as PDFs within DocDNA, (b) the first page of every volume is the right case number, 
and (c) any old files for each case are removed. Exela will use Exhibit 1 to note completion of that 
audit and return Exhibit 1 with the audit confirmation to the Clerk via an electronic scanned copy.  
For two (2) percent of the case numbers within DocDNA using the list provided by the Clerk, 
Exela will verify that the first images in each PDF represent the scan of the first document in the 
applicable paper volume, and that the last image in each PDF represents a scan of the last 
document in the applicable paper volume. 
11. The Clerk will validate each group of 250 boxes for conformity with the output requirements 
above, and will send Exela a list of case files that do not conform. For case files that either Exela 
or the Clerk identify as not conforming to the output requirements above, then Exela shall rescan 
the materials without charge and, to document the rescans and that they were without charge, 
include in its invoice for offsite services a count of the images that required rescanning.

This Amendment #02 (“Amendment”) takes effect as of the date signed by all parties listed in this 
preamble (“Amendment Effective Date”), and amends and revises that certain Agreement 21019 For 
Inmate Phone, Tablet, Bail, Kiosk & Video Visitation Services, dated May 20, 2021 as amended from 
time to time (the “Agreement”), by and between Global Tel*Link Corporation d/b/a ViaPath Technologies 
with an address of 3120 Fairview Park Drive, Suite 300, Falls Church, Virginia 22042 (the “Contractor”), 
and Lake County, Illinois Sheriff’s Office with an address of 18 N. County 9th Floor Waukegan, Illinois 
60085 (the “County”) (Contractor and County collectively, the “Parties” and each a “Party”). All capitalized 
terms not defined herein shall have the definitions set forth in the Agreement. 
 
WHEREAS, as set forth in Amendment 1, the terms and provisions of this Agreement were 
previously amended for the period from August 2, 2025, in order to address changes mandated by the 
Federal Communications Commission’s (“FCC”) Final Report and Order, Order on Reconsideration, 
Clarification and Waiver, and Further Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (the “FCC Order”) to implement the 
provisions of the Martha Wright-Reed Just and Reasonable Communications Act of 2022 (“MWR Act”) 
governing incarcerated people’s communications services (“IPCS”). 
WHEREAS, by this Amendment the Parties intend to update the Agreement in consideration of 
the FCC Waiver Order issued on June 30, 2025. 
 
NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the promises and covenants set forth in this 
Amendment, and for good and valuable consideration, the sufficiency of which is acknowledged by the 
Parties’ signatures, the Parties agree as follows: 
1. Section 5. Agreement Fees, Rates and Commissions, of this Agreement is hereby deleted in its 
entirety and replaced with the following: 
The following rates, charges, commissions and other provisions are amended and/or deleted as 
follows effective on as of the effective date of this Amendment. The Parties acknowledge and agree 
that the terms set forth in the FCC Order Letter were applicable for the period from August 2, 2025, 
through the Amendment Effective Date. 
A. Rates and Charges for Inmate Telephone Services.  The per-minute-of-use call rate 
shall not exceed the maximum rates authorized by the state’s telecommunication 
regulatory authority (“PUC”) and the Federal Communications Commission (“FCC”). 
i. Interstate ITS calls, whether made using a Collect, Prepaid Debit, or 
AdvancePay™ format:  $0.07 per minute of use. 
ii. Intrastate ITS calls, whether made using a Collect, Prepaid Debit, or 
AdvancePay™ format:  $0.07 per minute of use. 
iii. International ITS calls, whether made using a Prepaid Debit or AdvancePay™ 
format: The Interstate ITS rate set forth above, plus the applicable call termination 
rate for the international destination of the call as published on the Contractor’s 
website, which may be updated every 3 months in accordance with the FCC Order.  
These rates can be found at:  https://www.gtl.net/legal-and-privacy/federal-tariffs-
and-price-lists/. 
iv. No per call, per connection, or flat-rate calling charges shall apply to international, 
interstate and intrastate ITS per minute of use calls. 
v. The ITS rates set forth above are exclusive of taxes and other amounts collected 
by the Contractor on behalf of, or paid to, third parties, including but not limited

vi. Remuneration shall be forty percent (40%) of the Gross Revenue billed or prepaid

of

limited to, the following: cracked or smashed screens, missing or removed internal

immediately to County staff, submit a request for replacement, and cooperate fully 
with efforts to retrieve the Tablet. Replacement will be subject to Contractor review, 
and inmates shall be responsible for the cost of a replacement Tablet.

Other Inmate Violation or Misuse. Inmates shall be responsible for the replacement 
costs of Tablets damaged due to misuse or violation of any Terms of Use, including

replacement will be mutually agreed upon and memorialized in writing during the project 
management phase of implementation by Contractor and County via a Standard Operating 
Procedure (“SOP”).  
i. 
Tablet Replacement Request. Inmates and/or County must submit a Tablet replacement 
request (“Tablet Replacement Request”) for review and approval of the Contractor. 
The Tablet Replacement Request should include a description of the issue with the 
Tablet and a description of how and when the Tablet was damaged, lost, or 
stolen. Contractor will review the Tablet Replacement Request, inspect the Tablet, and 
determine if additional information is needed to process the request.

Replacement Cost Invoicing/Billing. The Parties agree that the replacement cost for a 
Tablet is $255.00. If it is determined that an inmate is responsible for the replacement 
cost, Contractor will invoice the County for the replacement cost. Contractor will 
invoice the County monthly for such replacement Tablets. County will assess the 
inmate for the replacement costs, obtain reimbursement from the inmate, or remit 
payment from the Inmate Welfare fund. 
iii. 
Priority of Tablet Replacement. Unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the Parties, 
Contractor will prioritize replacement of Tablets due to normal wear and tear as well 
as defect and malfunctioning before all other.

for LAKE COUNTY JAIL (LCJ) 
 
This AGREEMENT is entered into by and between Lake County (“County”) and Global Tel*Link (GTL) (“Contractor”), 
3120 Fairview Park Drive, Suite 300, Falls Church, VA 22042 
 
 
RECITALS 
 
WHEREAS, Lake County is seeking a Contractor to provide Inmate Phone, Tablets, Bail, Kiosk and Video Visitation for 
Lake County, IL as noted in the Contractor proposal dated February 9, 2021, (“Services”); and 
 
WHEREAS, Contractor has the professional expertise and credentials to provide these Services and has agreed to 
assume responsibility for this Agreement. 
 
NOW, THEREFORE, Lake County and Contractor agree as follows: 
 
SECTION 1.  AGREEMENT DOCUMENTS 
The Agreement Documents that constitute the entire Agreement between Lake County and Contractor are in order of 
precedence: 
 
A. This Agreement and all exhibits thereto;  
B. Terms and Conditions identified in RFP 21019 – Inmate Phone, Tablets, Bail, Kiosk and Video Visitation as Exhibit 
A; 
C. Contractor’s RFP response to RFP 21019 proposal dated February 9, 2021 and all exhibits thereto identified 
within as Exhibit B;   
D. Implementation Plan identified within as Exhibit C; and 
E. Master Service Agreement identified within as Exhibit D. 
 
SECTION 2.  SCOPE OF WORK 
Lake County Jail sought proposals for a Contractor to provide an inmate phone, tablet, bail, kiosk, video visitation 
system, and services at the Lake County Jail including an on-site administrator to monitor the day-to-day operations. 
The Contractor provided a proposal dated February 9, 2021 (Exhibit B) to provide a “turn-key” fully operational system 
(including, but not limited to, all related hardware, controls, managed hosting and reporting).  The Contractor has 
outlined sixty (60) days from start to finish the Implementation Plan that can be found in Exhibit C. 
 
SECTION 3.   DURATION 
This Agreement shall be in effect for a two (2) year period from the date of award. Lake County reserves the right 
to renew this contract for three (3) additional one (1) year periods, subject to acceptable performance by the 
Contractor and upon appropriation of sufficient funds. At the end of any contract term, Lake County reserves the 
right to extend this contract for a period of up to sixty (60) days for the purpose of getting a new contract in place. 
At the end of any contract year, this agreement is subject to the appropriation of sufficient funds. 
 
SECTION 4.  INITIAL INVESTMENT 
The system shall be installed by Contractor at no cost to Lake County and as such, the parties recognize that Contractor 
has invested or shall invest significant up-front resources of infrastructure and equipment to establish and maintain a 
fully operable turnkey electronic telephone and video communications network at the Lake County Jail Facility.   
Such investment shall include Contractor to furnish and install:

SECTION 6.  MAINTENANCE OF EQUIPMENT 
During the Term of this Agreement, Contractor shall provide maintenance for all hardware, firmware and software and 
agrees to repair and maintain its equipment in good operating condition and shall exclusively maintain its equipment 
in such condition by furnishing all necessary parts and labor reasonably necessary for the successful operation of the 
equipment.  Contractor shall have access to its equipment to adjust, remove, disconnect, repair, replace, maintain or 
alter the equipment as required to provide a complete and functional operating system.   Lake County shall permit 
employees or contractors of Contractor reasonable and safe access in order to provide such service, repair, and 
maintenance on equipment.  Contractor equipment shall remain the sole property of Contractor. Upon termination of 
this Agreement, Contractor shall have the right to enter the premises to remove the equipment.  Lake County shall 
notify Contractor of any misuse, destruction, damage, or vandalism to the equipment as soon as practicable. Lake 
County shall exercise reasonable care to prevent damage or destruction of Contractor equipment.  Maintenance, 
service and upgrades shall be provided by Contractor at no cost to Lake County. 
 
SECTION 7.  CHANGE ORDERS 
In the event changes to the Scope of the project and/or additional work become necessary or desirable to the parties, 
the parties shall follow the procedures set forth in this Section. A Change shall be effective only when documented by 
a written, dated agreement executed by both parties which expressly references this Agreement (a “Change Order”). 
The Change Order shall set forth in detail: (i) the Change requested, (ii) the reason for the proposed Change; (iii) the 
cost of the Change; and (iv) the impact of the Change on time for completion of the project.   
 
In the event either party desires a Change, the Project Manager for such party shall submit to the other party’s Project 
Manager a proposed Change Order. If the receiving party does not accept the Change Order in writing within ten (10) 
days, the receiving party shall be deemed to have rejected the Change Order. If the parties cannot reach agreement on 
a proposed Change, Contractor shall nevertheless continue to render performance under this Agreement in accordance 
with its (unchanged) terms and conditions.  
 
Changes that involve or increase in the amounts payable by the County may require execution by the County Purchasing 
Agent.  Some increases may also require approval by the County Board.  In those cases where the County Purchasing 
Agent’s signature is required, or County Board approval is needed, the Change Order shall not be deemed rejected by 
County after ten (10) days provided the Project Manager has indicated in writing within the ten (10) day period of his 
intent to present the Change Order for appropriate signature or approval.

INMATE PHONE, TABLET, BAIL, AND VIDEO VISITATION FOR THE LAKE COUNTY SHERIFF  
GENERAL INFORMATION 
January 2021  
INTENT 
Lake County is requesting proposals to provide an inmate phone, tablet, bail, kiosk, video visitation system, and services 
at the Lake County Jail including an on-site administrator to monitor the day-to-day operations. Lake County is only 
interested in receiving proposals that provide a “turn-key” fully operational system (including, but not limited to, all 
related hardware, controls, managed hosting and reporting). The Lake County Sheriff’s Office’s top priority in awarding 
this RFP is ensuring that all services offered are at a fair and reasonable cost for inmates, their family, and friends. We 
welcome all proposals and if a vendor is unable to provide a specific service outlined in our RFP (i.e., specific 
investigative software features or on-site admin) we are open to alternative solutions and want to hear what your 
company has to offer. 
 
BACKGROUND 
Lake County is located in northeast Illinois, between the Chicago and Milwaukee metropolitan areas. Lake County is home 
to about 736,000 residents. Lake County is committed to open government and transparency, and the County board’s 
conservative fiscal policies have allowed the County to maintain fiscal stability and achieve AAA bond rating from Standard 
& Poor’s and Moody’s. Lake County government, one of the top ten employers in the County with approximately 2700 
employees, provides the best possible services to its diverse residents. From addressing traffic congestion and balancing 
growth to promoting healthy and safe communities, every Lake County employee specializes in providing customer service 
to the residents of Lake County. The County's departments and elected officials, including 21 elected board members, 
work daily to increase the quality, timeliness, and cost effectiveness of the programs they provide. 
 
The Lake County Sheriff’s Jail Division incarcerates adults (18 years of age and older) accused of crimes and awaiting trial, as 
well as those convicted and sentenced to less than one year in jail (county time) and those sentenced to the Illinois 
Department of Corrections and awaiting transfer. The Lake County Jail was the first county facility in Illinois and the 16th in 
the nation to be built and operated under the principles of Direct Supervision. The jail consists of housing units referred to as 
‘pods’ in which Jail Officers and detainees intermingle without the full complement of traditional barriers. A pod typically 
holds between 24 and 60 detainees. The Lake County Jail received and has maintained accreditation through the Prison Rape 
Elimination Act (PREA) since 1989, National Commission on Correctional Health Care (NCCHC) since November 1987, and 
through the American Correctional Association since August 2006. 
 
Typically, 80% to 90% of the inmates are incarcerated on a pretrial basis. Individuals sentenced to longer than one year 
are transferred to the custody of the Illinois Department of Corrections. In 2019, 7,264detainees were booked with an 
average length of stay of 30 days. However, inmates can be sentenced to the Jail facility for up to one year. 
 
The current facility was opened in March 1989 and has a total rated capacity of 778 beds in the jail and 288 beds in a 
connected Community Based Corrections Center. The current operational capacity of the jail is 740 beds and the 
Community Based Center currently has 168 beds. 
 
Over the past year, the Adult Jail’s daily population has fluctuated from a low of 466 to more than 672 due to the COVID-
19 pandemic and seasonal variation. The average daily population of the Community Based Program is currently 101 
residents (8 females / 93 males), the majority of which come from the jail upon being sentenced. 
 
The Lake County Jail currently uses Central Square for its Jail Management Software (JMS) and Stellar Services for its 
commissary accounts. It is required that the selected system shall integrate with both existing and all future software at no 
cost to the County. In addition, the Lake County Sheriff’s Office is currently interviewing other potential Record Management 
Service Providers as a 9-1-1 consolidation project. Any changes to the JMS, CAD, RMS system or commissary software must be 
integrated at no cost to the County. 
 
 

--- PAGE BREAK ---

 
 
18 
 
The Lake County Jail is a secure facility with restricted access. The Proposer, their sub-Proposer, and employees shall strictly 
observe and comply with all rules, regulations, policies, procedures, post orders and general orders established at each 
facility. The Proposer shall report violations by their sub-Proposers and/or employees to Lake County in writing, within 24 
hours and provide written notice of Proposer action taken. 
 
The Lake County Jail currently has 88 inmate phones and 36 video visitation terminals. All visitors have the option to come 
into the facility to schedule a visit or set up a visit online. The approved visitor list is compiled from names the inmates 
provide to staff, of who they want visits from, and those names are entered into the Jail Management Systems visitor 
module. Sheriff’s policy is to require 24 hours to set up a visit onsite; the public can schedule the visit for the next day or 
any day in the future. On the day of the visit all public must present a valid form of identification. 
 
Parking is not provided at the Adult Correctional Facility. The Proposer, any subcontractor, and employees will need to 
purchase a parking permit from the City of Waukegan.  All parking information can be found on the City of Waukegan’s 
website.

Video Visitation 
The Lake County Jail has the technological capability for both on-site and remote video visitation. Onsite visitations consist of 
the visitor signing up online and an Officer approves or denies the requested date and time. If approved, the visitors report to 
intake and visit the inmates through the video system while inmates are in the booths inside the pods. Remote visitations are 
done with the visitor at home while the inmate is in their pod booth. All visitors sign up online and are approved or denied by 
the Officers.   
 
Remote visits cost $5.00 and there is no limit on how many remote visits can be scheduled. All visitors are allowed one free 
on-site visit per week at no cost. Since COVID, the Jail has eliminated all on-site visiting and have in place given one free remote 
visit per week.

• 
 Behavior Modification Application 
• 
 Eligibility Status 
• 
Dashboards and real time tablet analysis 
• 
Services to allow officers to formulate notifications that can be pushed to all inmate tablets 
• 
Locate tablet features 
• 
Activation / Deactivation of tablets by: 
o Inmate 
o Groups of inmates 
o Pod 
• 
Activation / Deactivation of any or all applications by: 
o Inmate 
o Groups of inmates 
o Pod 
23. Vendor shall provide tablets which have been wiped of any residual operating systems. In your RFP response, please 
detail the process on how the tablets are wiped. 
24. Vendor Tablets must provide live monitoring capabilities. 
25. Vendor must provide tracking of tablet revenues for commissions verification. 
26. Vendor shall refund any inmate who is unable to access their purchased or rented content within three (3) business 
days. The preferred method of refunding is placing it on the inmate’s books versus their phone account. If this 
method results in additional steps required by the Jail, please disclose the additional steps in your RFP response.  
 
Public Kiosk for Inmate Telephone System  
The County currently has one kiosk available for the public to access in the Community Based Correction Center (CBCC) and 
one kiosk in jail reception that allows friends and family to deposit funds in person should they so choose. A third kiosk is 
located at booking for inmates to deposit their money. It is our intent to keep kiosks in all three locations described above. 
 
1. Lobby Kiosk Specifications 
a. Lobby kiosks must have a touch screen, a receipt printer, two (2) bill acceptors, and it should be built in a 
steel case to prevent vandalism. The kiosks must be self-contained and only require LCSO to provide 
power and network connectivity. 
b. Lobby kiosks must be designed in such a fashion that LCSO personnel can change receipt paper without 
the intervention of the Proposer. This is a mandatory item so that in the event the receipt paper runs 
out, the facility does not need to wait on Proposer’s response. Proposer must furnish LCSO with extra 
receipt paper at no cost. 
c. Lobby kiosks must be ADA compliant. 
d. At a minimum, lobby kiosks must be bi-lingual and allow the user to select either English or Spanish. 
Additional languages are encouraged. Please list all other languages. 
e. The successful Proposer, or their sub-contracted armor car service, will be responsible to collecting ALL 
cash from the lobby kiosks twice a week on non-consecutive days. Jail Command must agree upon 
which two days of the week the cash will be collected.  
f. 
All selections on lobby kiosks must be performed using touch screen technology. NO keyboard or mouse 
will be permitted. 
g. The Lobby Kiosk must allow the user to place money on an inmate’s commissary using either cash or 
credit card through an interface to the LCSO jail management system. 
h. The Lobby Kiosk must be allowed for posting of cash bail. 

--- PAGE BREAK ---

 
 
29 
 
i. 
The Lobby Kiosk must allow the user to enter a full or partial inmate name. 
j. 
During the deposit process, the lobby kiosk must present the user with notification of the usage fees and 
must require the user to acknowledge these fees.  
k. Once an inmate has been selected, the lobby kiosk must present the user with a maximum amount that 
they may deposit. The lobby kiosk will not permit deposits beyond the maximum allowable amount. 
l. 
During the deposit process the lobby kiosk must require the user to select their payment method (cash, 
credit cards, etc.) 
m. If the user elects to use cash, the lobby kiosk must count the cash as the user places the bills in the 
acceptor.  
n. The lobby kiosk must show the end user the amount they have deposited, any surcharges, and the amount 
to be applied to the inmate’s commissary account or the user’s pre-paid telephone account.  
o. LCSO requires that any cash deposited for inmate’s bail be surcharge free. There shall be no exception to 
this. 
p. The successful proposer must be responsible for all counterfeit bills that may be accepted through the 
lobby kiosk. 
q. If the user elects to use a credit card, the lobby kiosk must allow the user to enter the amount that they 
wish to have charged to the card.  
r. The successful proposer must be responsible for all credit card charge backs but will be allowed to 
attempt to recoup from the inmate’s commissary or pre-paid inmate telephone account. 
s. All transactions accepted at the lobby kiosk must be receipted upon completion. 
t. All receipts must contain a contact number in the event there is an issue with the money deposited into 
the inmate’s commissary account, pre-paid phone account, or bond account. 
u. Successful proposer shall be completely responsible for solving any issues that users have with 
transactions that occur with a lobby kiosk. LCSO does not want to have any interaction with the public on 
transactional issues. 
 
2. Booking Kiosk - When an inmate is being booked into the jail, LCSO directs the inmate to deposit all coins and cash 
into the booking kiosk. 
a. At a minimum, booking kiosk must be bi-lingual and allow the user to select either English or 
Spanish. Additional languages are encouraged. Please list all other languages. 
b. Booking kiosk must accept coins, cash and credit cards. There shall be no exceptions to this.  
c. 
Booking kiosk must have a touch screen, a receipt printer, two (2) bill acceptors, coin acceptor and it 
should be built in a steel case to prevent vandalism.  
d. The kiosks must be self-contained and only require LCSO to provide power and network connectivity.  
e. All selections on the booking kiosk must be performed using touch screen technology. NO keyboard or 
mouse will be permitted. 
f. 
All transactions accepted at the booking kiosk must be receipted upon completion. 
g. Booking kiosk must be designed in such a fashion that LCSO personnel can change receipt paper 
without the intervention of the Proposer. This is a mandatory item so that in the event the receipt 
paper runs out, the facility does not need to wait on the Proposer’s response. Proposer must furnish 
LCSO with all receipt paper at no charge. 
h. The successful proposer must be responsible for all counterfeit bills that may be accepted through the 
booking kiosk. 
i. 
The successful Proposer, or their sub-contracted armor car service, will be responsible to collecting ALL cash 
and coins from the booking kiosk twice a week on non-consecutive days. Jail Command must agree upon 
which two days of the week the cash will be collected. Our booking kiosk is in a secure location and weapons 
will not be allowed to be brought into the facility by the Proposer or armored car service. 
j. 
LCSO requires that any cash deposited for inmate’s bail be surcharge free. There shall be no exception to this. 
 
3. Kiosk Key Terms and Conditions

30 
 
a. System Responsibility- The successful proposer is responsible for a complete installation and preventive 
maintenance of all kiosks and shall provide a complete and tested system. The proposer must provide 
any required interface equipment at no cost to the LCSO. It is to be understood that a complete 
operating system is required in all cases. 
b. Consumer Fees - The LCSO realizes that the successful proposer will charge transaction fees. The County 
believes in providing fair and affordable services to inmates, friends, and family of inmates. The Vendor 
must disclose all possible consumer fees. This includes Vendor fees and third-party fees. The County may 
reject proposals if all potential fees are not fully disclosed. This would include, but is not limited to, any 
and all deposit fees, transfer fees, refund fees, account maintenance fees, billing fees, credit card fees, 
paper bill fees, monthly access fees, etc. All fees must be in writing and detailed whether for a lobby 
kiosk, the booking kiosk, or for the internet banking solution. 
c. The proposer must have sufficient repair personnel to be able to respond to multiple repair events at 
the same time. 
d. The proposer should provide a free-of-charge repair service number for a live, trained person to receive 
trouble reports twenty-four (24) hours a day, seven (7) days a week, three hundred sixty-five (365) days 
a year (366 days in a Leap Year). 
e. Out-of-hours and weekend repair service will be limited to essential repairs only. However, the proposer 
must respond to those repairs when required in under four (4) hours.  
f. The proposer must inform the LCSO as soon as possible of any occurrence of an unusual nature that may 
result in prolonged or serious service interruption. The proposer shall perform any work requiring 
prolonged or serious service interruption at a time, which will cause minimum disruption to the users in 
agreement with the designated representative, and a time mutually agreed upon. 
g. All money and card transactions must be guaranteed by the successful proposer. The LCSO shall not be 
responsible for fraudulent money or card transactions.  
 
Internet Banking Solution 
1. The successful proposer must also provide an Internet Banking Solution for receiving money for deposit on an 
inmate’s commissary account through credit card transactions. 
2. The successful proposal must describe their solution to provide Debit Release Cards that allows the transfer of 
remaining trust and phone account balances to a debit card upon the inmate’s release. 
3. The Internet Banking Solution must allow the user to enter the amount they wish to have charged to the card.  
4. The Internet Banking Solution must show the end user the amount they have deposited, the surcharge, and the 
amount to be applied to the inmate’s commissary account. 
5. Successful proposer must be solely responsible for solving any issues that users have with transactions that occur with 
through the Internet Banking Solution. LCSO does not want to interact with the public on transactional issues. 
6. All money received for deposit to the inmate’s commissary account or bail must be placed in the LCSO JMS through 
an interface. Please specify the frequency of the updates and whether your system is real-time or not. If there is a 
delay, please disclose the length of delay. 
7. The posting of money to the LCSO JMS must be done electronically and not require personnel from LCSO to hand 
enter each separate transaction. 
8. All money received both at the lobby kiosk, through the Internet Banking Solution for inmate commissary, and for bail 
must be ACH transferred to the LCSO by the next business day after the transaction occurs. The LCSO does NOT want 
to wait until the money has been collected from the Lobby Kiosk. 
9. When the ACH transfer occurs, the successful Proposer must notify LCSO staff of the amount to be transferred along 
with inmate’s name, identification number, and the amount they have received. This email will be used to reconcile 
against the LCSO JMS. 
10. All bank deposits and transactions must match. If the Proposer does batch deposits, they must provide the County a 
breakdown of which transactions were included in the batch by providing at a minimum the inmate’s name, date, and 
amount. 
11. All money and card transactions must be guaranteed by the successful proposer. The LCSO shall not be responsible 
for fraudulent money or card transactions.

12. For each bail transaction completed, the Proposer will charge a $21.00 transaction and processing fee on behalf of

On-site Representative 
Lake County currently has an onsite representative, employed by our phone, tablet, and kiosk vendor that is responsible 
for the daily operations of phones, tablets, and video visitation units. The onsite representative works Monday – Friday 
for eight (8) hours a day and is available for emergency calls when there are major equipment and/or network outages. 
The main responsibilities of our onsite representative are repairing damaged and/or inoperable equipment, answering 
and resolving grievances pertaining to the phones, tablets, and video visitation, and approving video visitation accounts. 
it is expected that if the on-site representative position is vacant for an extended amount of time (anything longer than 
3 days), that it be filled immediately, even if on a temporary basis, until the position is permanently filled. This includes 
an employee that has been terminated, resigned, or on an extended leave, including but not limited to medical, 
vacation, personal, and maternity/paternity leave. No vacancy will be allowed, and Lake County staff will not resume the 
daily responsibilities of the vendor. 
 
It is Lake County’s intent to maintain this position. Please provide a detailed position description of the proposed onsite 
representative, qualifications, the employee’s immediate supervisor’s contact information, eligible benefits, and training 
history. Although it is Lake County’s intent to keep this position, we are open to hearing other solutions if a vendor is 
unable to provide an on-site representative.  
 
Commission 
1. The commission offered to the County shall be based on total gross revenues, with no deductions for fraud, bad debt, 
uncollectible, or unbillable calls.  No deduction shall be made for any cost of providing the services, software, or 
equipment described in this RFP.    
2. Commissions shall be paid monthly and shall be accompanied by a commission and usage summary report which shall 
include, at a minimum, the following information: 
• 
Date of report 
• 
Time period being reported 
• 
Total number of calls 
• 
Total number minutes 
• 
Total tablet purchases/rentals by category (i.e. music, movie, game, etc.) 
• 
Total video visitation sessions 
• 
Total video visitation minutes 
• 
Total gross revenue (as defined above) 
3. Commission payments shall arrive no later than forty-five (45) days following the calendar month for which 
commissions are being paid. 
4. All Commissions are deposited into the Inmate Welfare Fund.  
5. The County currently does not receive commission on bail payments.

EVALUATION CRITERIA 
January 2021  
 
The County will conduct a comprehensive, fair, and impartial evaluation of proposals received in response to this procurement 
effort.  All proposals will be evaluated by how well the proposal satisfies the described/stated needs, rather than how exactly 
the proposal matches the strictest interpretation of the terminology and design concepts stated herein.  Newly emerging 
technologies, additional features, and the ability of the proposed solutions to adapt will be a consideration. 
 
Evaluation Organization 
An Evaluation Committee will be established to score and evaluate the submitted proposals. 
The Evaluation Committee may include members from Lake County’s departments who have experience with the associated 
services. The Committee will be responsible for the proposal evaluation (including corporate reference checks). 
 
Evaluation of the Proposals 
Lake County will evaluate the Proposers response and the extent to which it meets the requirements delineated in this RFP. 
All proposals submitted in response to this RFP will be scored based on the evaluation factors identified below. 
 
Evaluation Factors: 
• 
Demonstrated understanding and ability to meet requirements outlined in a comprehensive RFP 
response. 
• 
Service and Support 
• 
Demonstrates ability to provide reliable service for all central call, tablet, bail, and video 
processing with ongoing ability to provide any necessary customer support to address 
operations in a 24/7 365-day facility. 
• 
Proposed Account Support Team 
• 
Maintenance Policies and Procedures 
• 
Reference Feedback 
• 
Pricing and Financial Offering – Evaluation will not be focused on the highest proposed commission. 
Emphasis should be on ensuring that rates and fees are fair and reasonable to the inmates and their 
family. 
• 
Fees charged to inmates and depositors 
• 
Commission rate based on total gross revenue  
• 
Rate charged to the party who accepts the charges  
• 
Proposer’s ability to offer direct billing collect call billing through a LEC, prepaid and debit-
based calling 
• 
Hardware, Technology, and Software Offering 
• 
Ability to meet the minimum requirements outlined in our RFP 
• 
Investigative Software 
• 
Value Added Services 
• 
Proposed Project Plan  
• 
Detailed scheduling plan with timelines 
• 
Comprehensive project plan outlining responsibilities and correlating action items of each party 
with suggested timelines that line up the proposed scheduling plan 
• 
Implementation with minimal interruption of services 
 
Short List 
The evaluation factors will be used to assist the evaluation committee in determining a short list. Proposers will be notified by 
the County if they have been selected for the short list. Please note, Lake County reserves the right to not short list any and 
all Proposers if it is not in the best interest of the County.

Interview

visits to any locations serviced by Proposers.  Site visits may be scheduled or unscheduled as determined by the County.  If 
applicable, the County shall contact Proposers to arrange an interview. Information provided as part of the interview may be 
used by Evaluation Committee to re-evaluate and re-rank Proposers. 
 
Additional Investigations 
The County reserves the right to make such additional investigations as it deems necessary to establish the competence 
and financial stability of any firm submitting a proposal including contacting other clients, whether listed as a reference of 
not, to assess the performance of the vendor and the level of satisfaction of other entities with the project management 
services solution proposed to Lake County. 
 
Best and Final Offer 
The County reserves the right to request a Best and Final Offer (BAFO) if additional information or modified terms are 
necessary for the Evaluation Committee to complete its evaluation and ranking. A BAFO will not be used solely to reduce 
pricing. If a BAFO is requested, all short-listed proposers, or if the short list process is not used, all qualified Proposers will 
be provided an opportunity to submit a modified Response. Only one BAFO request will be issued by the County. The 
information received from the BAFO will be used by the Evaluation Committee to re-evaluate and re-rank the Proposers. 
 
Intent to Negotiate 
The County reserves the right to invite the most qualified Proposer to negotiate final terms and conditions, finalize scope 
clarification and confirm final pricing and payment terms.  The information received from the negotiation shall be found 
in the final contract document.  If the parties are unable to negotiate a satisfactory contract the negotiations will be 
terminated.  The County reserves the right to either begin negotiations with the qualified Proposer that is next preferred 
or non-award the request for proposal.

The awarded vendor will have full responsibility for project implementation. It is expected that the vendor 
shall provide the most detailed price breakdown. Items, which are not produced or provided directly, shall be 
marked as such. Prices shall be inclusive of any and all costs associated with full implementation, which may 
include cost of manufacture, packing, handling loading/unloading, removal of debris, transportation, inside 
delivery, installation, training, maintenance, documentation, insurance, and any other item that could be 
interpreted as the overall cost to effect a turnkey procurement. All commission proposed shall be based on 
total gross revenue with no deductions from fraud, bad debt, uncollectable, or unbillable calls. 
 
Any taxes are to be charged to end user customers based on per call, visit, etc. and not at the time of deposit. 
Please provide a detailed explanation on how taxes will be charged to customers/end users.

Kiosk Pricing Structures 
The kiosks in the jail are currently provided by the phone, tablet, and video visitation provider and are used for ordering 
commissary, funding inmate deposits (commissary and phone), and bail. We would like each Proposer to provide a detailed 
pricing structure for the four different structures below. If your company offers other combinations that are not listed below, 
please include details for those structures as well. We are open to reviewing all proposed structures and suggestions. 
 
• 
Proposer Structure - using Proposer’s kiosks exclusively for all inmate deposits (phone and commissary), bail, 
and ordering commissary. 
• 
Commissary Vendor Structure - using our commissary vendor’s kiosks exclusively for all inmate deposits 
(phone and commissary), bail, and ordering commissary. 
• 
Hybrid Structure 1 - using Proposer’s kiosk for inmate phone deposits and using our current commissary 
vendor’s kiosks for bail, commissary deposits, and ordering commissary. 
• 
Hybrid Structure 2 – using Proposer’s kiosk for inmate phone deposits and bail and using our commissary 
vendor’s kiosk for commissary deposits and ordering commissary.

All miscellaneous fees

SUSTAINABLITY STATEMENT                                                                                                                  January 2021 
 
The County of Lake is committed to green and sustainable practices and good environmental 
stewardship. Consequently, we are asking Proposers to provide a Statement of Sustainability to 
ensure our Proposers are also incorporating sustainability into their firms’ practices. 
 
INSTRUCTIONS 
On the following Sustainability Statement form, provide a clear description of your firm’s 
sustainable practices, policies, or procedures. These practices may include, but may not be limited 
to, the following categories and examples: 
Waste Minimization within your office or facilities, such as a recycling programs, double-sided 
copying, electronic internal communications (i.e. memos), use of recycled-content materials and 
reusable cups, limiting printing, electronic document management, instituting green purchasing 
policies, using green cleaning supplies and practices, or reducing packaging in materials you procure 
or supply. 
 
Energy Efficiency within your office, facilities, or firm, such as lighting retrofits, photo-sensor 
switches for lighting, effective use of daytime lighting, using Energy Star rated appliances or 
equipment, using an alternative fuel or having efficient fleet policies, an anti-idling policy, or indoor 
temperature management (i.e. turning the thermostat up in the summer and down in the winter). 
 
Water Efficiency within the office, facilities, or firm, such as faucet or fixture retrofits, switching 
from individual bottled water to office water coolers or drinking fountains, and installing drought-
tolerant landscaping. 
 
Staff encouraged to adopt sustainable practices and supported by your firm through public transit 
benefits, bicycle accommodations, telecommuting options, support for green seminar attendance, 
becoming US Green Building Council LEED accredited, or creating an internal “green team.” 
 
Education of your staff about green practices, education of your business peers about your green 
accomplishments, education of your community by your sustainability, or notice of any 
environmental awards your firm has achieved.

     Vendor Certification Definitions 
• 
Minority-owned business (MBE) 
A business concern which is at least 51% owned by one or more minority persons, or in the case of a corporation, at least 
51% of the stock in which is owned by one or more minority persons; and the management and daily business operations of 
which are controlled by one or more of the minority individuals who own it. 
• 
Woman-owned business (WBE) 
A business which is at least 51% owned by one or more women, or, in the case of a corporation, at least 51% of the stock in 
which is owned by one or more women; and the management and daily business operations of which are controlled by one 
or more of the women who own it. 
• 
Veteran-owned Business Enterprise (VBE)  
A small business (i) that is at least 51 percent owned, controlled and managed by one or more Eligible Veterans or in the case 
of a corporation, at least 51 percent or more of the stock of which is owned, controlled and managed by one or more Eligible 
Veterans.  
• 
Eligible Veteran means a person who (i) has been either a member of the armed forces of the United States or, while a 
citizen of the United States, was a member of the armed forces of allies of the United States in time of hostilities with a 
foreign country and (ii) has served under one or more of the following conditions: (a) the veteran served a total of at 
least 6 months; (b) the veteran served for the duration of hostilities regardless of the length of the engagement; (c) the 
veteran was discharged on the basis of hardship; or (d) the veteran was released from active duty because of a service 
connected disability and was discharged under honorable conditions.  
• 
Armed forces of the United States means the United States Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, Coast Guard or service 
in active duty as defined under 38 U.S.C. Section 101. Service in the Merchant Marine that constitutes active duty under 
Section 401 of federal Public Act 95-202 shall also be considered service in the armed forces for purposes of this 
Division.  
• 
Persons with Disabilities Owned Business Enterprise (PDBE)  
A small business (i) that is at least 51 percent owned. controlled and managed by one or more Persons with a Disability; or in 
the case of a corporation, at least 51 percent or more of the stock of which is owned, controlled, and managed by one or 
more Persons with a Disability.  
• 
Disability or Disabled means, with respect to an individual, a physical or mental impairment that substantially limits one 
or more of the major life activities of the individual, a record of physical or mental impairment that substantially limits 
one or more of the major life activities of the individual, or being regarded as an individual with a physical or mental 
impairment that substantially limits one or more of the major life activities of the individual.  
• 
Service-Disabled Veteran-owned Business Enterprise (SDVBE)  
A small business (i) that is at least 51 percent owned, controlled, and managed by one or more qualified service disabled 
veterans or in the case of a corporation, at least 51 percent or more of the stock of which is owned, controlled and managed 
by one or more Service Disabled Veterans.  
• 
Service-Disabled Veteran means an Eligible Veteran who has been found to have 10 percent or more service-connected 
disability by the United States Department of Veterans Affairs or the United States Department of Defense.  
• 
Service-connected disability means a disability incurred in the line of duty in the active military, naval or air service as 
described in 38 U.S.C. 101(16).  
• 
BEP – Business Enterprise Program  
Business Enterprise Program (BEP) BEP assists businesses owned by minorities, women and people with disabilities gain 
access to the State of Illinois procurement process. BEP certification with the State of Illinois can also open the door to 
opportunities with other public and private entities which are looking for diverse suppliers. 
• 
Small Disadvantaged Businesses (SDB) 
A Small Disadvantaged Business (SDB) is a small business owned and controlled by socially and economically disadvantaged 
individuals as defined by Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) 19.001 
• 
Veteran-Owned Small Business (VOSB) 
A Veteran-Owned Small Business (VOSB) is a small business that is at least 51 percent owned by one or more veterans; or, if a 
publicly owned business, at least 51 percent of the stock is owned by one or more veterans.  Also, one or more veterans 
control management and daily business operations of the firm. 
• 
Local business 
Lake County launched a Buy Local. Build Local. Work Local initiative in 2013 to increase the outreach and procurement 
opportunities for businesses located within Lake County, including women‐owned businesses and minority‐owned business 
enterprises (L/W/MBE). The overarching objective is to maximize participation from these businesses in the County’s 
procurement process, in accordance with applicable law.

Communications Solutions – Enabling Connections without Compromising Control 
Connect inmates to their visitors and keep your finger on the information pulse. From secure telephones and in-pod

visitors onsite and remotely. GTL Visitation Solutions make it easy to schedule and administer on-premises and 
remote video visitations. GTL’s Video Visitation system manages over 13M essential visits per year. 
Investigative and Intelligence Solutions – Finding Answers that Prevent and Solve Crimes 
Data Mine and interpret valuable information and intelligence data from multiple sources, including commissary 
orders and telephone calls. GTL puts it all together in clear, easy-to-interpret reports, graphs, and charts. Connections 
become clear for real-time, actionable intelligence. Designed with input from leading investigators, our field-tested 
technology reveals previously hidden activities, identifies incriminating calls, and helps solve and prevent crimes. Our 
Data IQ software can import data from any source without additional development, or external law enforcement 
agencies. Simply put: “If you can THINK it, we can LINK it.” Designed with input from leading investigators, field-
tested technology reveals previously hidden activities, identifies incriminating calls and helps investigators solve and 
proactively prevent crimes.

Enterprise Management Solutions span the entire spectrum of inmate/facility interaction for correctional facilities of 
all sizes. Facilities across the country already experience enhanced levels of efficiency and greater control over the 
inmate population using GTL’s technology. From collecting and tracking inmate funds to one-stop access to inmate 
data, LCSO can rely on our tested and proven management  solutions.

The Lake County Sheriff’s Office is currently soliciting sealed proposals to 
provide local, long-distance, and international telephone service, inmate and 
correctional officer tablets, banking, kiosks, video visitation service, a recording 
and monitoring system, and all related equipment for the inmates at the Lake 
County Jail. 
 
Lake County is interested in one complete “turn-key” fully operational system 
and service, which will provide local, inter- lata, intra-lata, inter-state and video 
visitation service for which a Cost Recovery Fee will be paid to Lake County for 
all calls processed by coinless telephones, tablets, and video visitation 
equipment. The method of providing service will include a single primary vendor 
with end-to-end network and equipment responsibilities. 
 
Lake County Jail currently offers tablets to the inmates and selected Correctional 
Officers on a 1:1 basis. It is the intent of the Lake County Sheriff that the awarded 
proposer will be able to provide a turn-key tablet solution that is Wi-Fi enabled.

Training Process for LCSO Staff 
The GTL training team uses onsite and web-based training to meet the diverse needs of individuals 
working in the corrections environment. GTL trainers use demonstrations, question-and-answer 
sessions, hands-on activity, and process reviews to help correctional staff quickly master the features 
and functionality of the system while learning by doing. 
1. On-Site Training – Upon installation, and typically before Go-Live, the GTL training team 
will provide in-depth training on all aspects of the system. During on-site training, the GTL 
trainer will lead a review of system features and functionality and actively encourage each 
participant to ask questions and perform hands-on activity in the system where 
applicable. The GTL trainer will provide a training syllabus that outlines the topics to be 
covered and provide adequate training materials such as user guides, quick reference 
guides, and quick-tip cards. On-site training will be available throughout the life of the 
contract as new features and functionalities are added, upgrades and expansions become 
available, or anytime upon request of LCSO. 
2. Web-Based Training – In conjunction with initial on-site training, our certified trainers  
can lead web-based training, allowing users to view the system via an online meeting and 
interact with the trainers and other participants on a toll-free conference bridge. This type 
of training will continue to be available throughout the life of the contract just as on-site 
training is. In fact, many facilities find that on-going web-based training is more beneficial 
after initial on-site training, because it allows GTL to train users at a time and place that is 
convenient for them. This ensures nearly every work schedule can be accommodated. 
3. Training Documentation – All materials presented during training sessions will be left 
with LCSO staff for references, along with full system user guides. In addition, GTL will 
provide LCSO with an online portal that allows users to access all documentation, from 
training agendas to how-to guides. GTL will also provide “Quick Tip” posters to be 
mounted near facility workstations for quick refreshers on common system features.

Video Relay Service (VRS) 
Lake County can ensure your hearing-impaired population 
will have the same calling privileges and security restrictions 
as all other inmates. GTL partners with Purple 
Communications, one of the largest and most respected 
 
 
 
Meet ADA communication guidelines for 
the hearing-impaired 
VRS providers in the United States. The GTL VRS App facilitates real-time video conversation directly 
between parties or through English and Spanish speaking American Sign Language (ASL) interpreters, 
who would also be on the telephone with the called party. 
LCSO can use GTL VRS app to replace or complement standard phone or TTY/TDD calls. We will mount 
Flex Link kiosks where you designate. For those who can’t use the wall-mounted kiosks, mobile Flex 
Link kiosk carts are also available. LCSO Inmates can use the same tablets they use for entertainment, 
education, and other applications. To prevent panning, the tablets must be docked in charging stations 
for the camera to work. Calls can be monitored and recorded for future analysis. 
This is an IP-based communication solution where the inmate communicates in sign language with a 
video translator via our Flex Link kiosk and the GTL VRS application. While the same hardware platform 
can be used for phone calls and video visitation, VRS is a separate application. 
GTL VRS fully-integrates with the GTL ITS platform. Calls can be live-monitored and recorded, including 
audio and video. VRS calls are subject to the same protocols, privileges, controls, recording, reviewing, 
and security protocols as traditional calls. This includes PIN and PAN protocols, restricted numbers, 
and call length. Like all call records, VRS Call Data Records are created, stored, and ready for analysis 
on our inmate telephone platform. 
 
Restricted Access 
Since VRS interpreter services are funded by the FCC, VRS calls are provided at no cost to users. 
Therefore, it’s imperative only eligible inmates have access to this powerful communication tool. Access 
is granted solely by Lake County and attached to the inmate’s PIN. Upon login, only inmates with 
proper PIN permission will see the GTL VRS icon on their touch screen. 
 
Call Settings 
Once placed, the call is routed through our partner, Purple Communications. They supply the video- 
call platform and ASL interpreters. Purple is a leading provider of video relay services to the hearing- 
impaired community and has served their communication needs for over 35 years. 
The VRS call type is determined based on the phone number dialed. If the call is to another registered 
VRS user, a direct connection is established, and no interpreter is engaged. This is per FCC requirements 
as VRS providers must connect two VRS users directly to one another via video, 

--- PAGE BREAK ---

© 2021 GTL 
Lake County RFP No. 21019 | 48 
 
 
eliminating the need for an interpreter since the two VRS users can communicate directly via sign 
language. If the call is to any other telephone number not registered as a VRS phone number, an 
English or Spanish speaking ASL interpreter, dependent on the inmate’s language selection, joins the 
call to translate the conversation between sign language and spoken words. 
 
Monitoring, Recording, and Analysis 
Because GTL VRS is integrated into the GTL ITS, VRS calls can be live-monitored or recorded for later 
analysis, delivering necessary reporting and investigative features. VRS Call Data Recordings include 
audio and video, and like all calls, records are stored on our inmate telephone platform. 
VRS data is included in call detail reports, giving your investigative staff a more inclusive view of what’s 
happening inside LCSO’s facilities. Like traditional telephone calls, personal calls are recorded while 
privileged calls are not.

© 2021 GTL 
Lake County RFP No. 21019 | 49 
 
 
How It Works—Placing a Call 
Using the GTL VRS app is simple and intuitive, allowing Lake County’s hearing-impaired population to 
fully communicate with their attorneys, friends, and family. 
 
 
To place a call using GTL VRS, an inmate logs into the Flex Link kiosk using their PIN. Once logged in, 
the GTL VRS app icon appears on the touchscreen. The icon will only appear if Lake County has granted 
the inmate access. 
After tapping the icon to initiate a call, the inmate selects the interpreter language, English or Spanish. 
As an extra security measure, the inmate must reenter the PIN followed by the telephone number they 
wish to dial. GTL ITS performs all standard checks to ensure the number is not banned and is on the 
approved list. 
The call is connected either to a video interpreter or directly to the called party as described earlier. An 
onscreen ASL video and text message informs the inmate the VRS call may be monitored and 
recorded. Depending on the type of call, the receiving party will either hear a recording via telephone 
or see the ASL video and text message if they are also a VRS user. 
To prevent fraud, if the inmate does not begin communicating via sign language, the call is terminated. 
If there is a call time limit, a visual indicator will announce that the call will end soon as a countdown 
begins. The inmate may also end the call at any time directly through the VRS app.

8. The Inmate phone system shall process all inmate calls on an outgoing

GTL Response: GTL has read, understands and will comply. 
 
The GTL ITS permits outgoing calls only. GTL employs several methods to ensure there will be no

• 
GTL uses IPVPN circuits to connect each facility to the data center, eliminating the need for 
POTS (plain ordinary telephone service) lines connected to our system. It is not possible to 
make an inbound call to our system through an IPVPN circuit. 
• 
Trunks in the ITS are engineered for one-way outbound calling only. 
• 
The inmate telephones have no ringer or other means of signaling an incoming call.

9. System shall provide the ability to detect three-way calls, and other 
fraudulent dialing patterns. Corrections personnel should be provided with 
the ability to flag and/or terminate such calls. 
 
GTL Response: GTL has read, understands and will comply. 
 
Called Party IQ® - Inmate-to-Inmate bridged call detection 
GTL’s Called Party IQ® leverages our leading position in the inmate telephone industry to add an 
additional layer of detection of three-way calling involving multiple inmates (including inmates not in 
the LCSO facilities bridged by an outside party. Called Party IQ brings state-of-the-art investigation 
capabilities to LCSO for detection of inmate to inmate telephone calls via a centralized multi-way call 
detection technology. The Called Party IQ process is a Live Call Event tool used to analyze call records 
and recordings when two or more inmate calls are made simultaneously to one destination number. 
Unlike Called Party IQ, similar products of 3rd party applications are post call production based  forcing 
investigators to wait for results. Because GTL services over 50% of the inmate population in  the United 
States, all calls processed by that population are required to validate through our centralized Universal 
Call Validation Software. This validation step affords LCSO the ability to identify, in real-time, any call 
that is already in progress to the same phone number or number being dialed by their resident/inmate. 
Should an event occur, the validation environment sends a token to the 1st Dialing Platform and to the 
2nd Dialing Platform so that both Call Detail Records are flagged. 
Subscribers can view the name of the other agency from where the 2nd call was placed. 
GTL’s system is designed with investigative intelligence in mind. Many GTL customers are located in 
relative proximity to each other, and many share data of these bridged calls and additional data each 
County, State or Federal facility would like to share. Because of our systems design, you can share data 
with authorized users from other agencies. Just like the level of access you can provide your staff 

--- PAGE BREAK ---

© 2021 GTL 
Lake County RFP No. 21019 | 62 
 
 
you can provide the same security with outside agencies, so you both can share data that will assist in 
investigations in the entire region. GTL provides GTL ITS services to most of the surrounding 
communities of LCSO. 
GTL’s ITS is designed with patented technology to isolate any attempt by the called party to bridge a 
call in-progress to a third party, commonly referred to as “three-way calling” or “conferencing.” GTL 
recommends allowing all calls to continue, even when the system detects a three-way-calling 
attempt. We encourage this to capture the most actionable intelligence possible and to avoid any 
false positives. 
 
GTL 3-Way Call Prevention 
Although GTL recommends all calls be allowed to continue when a detection occurs to capture the 
most actionable intelligence possible and to avoid any false positives, the system can be configured 
to do one of the following upon detection of call forwarding or three-way calling: 
• 
Disconnect the call immediately. 
• 
Disconnect the call after providing an explanatory message that a prohibited remote call 
forwarding/3-way call attempt has been detected (during which the parties cannot 
communicate). 
• 
Play a message that a prohibited call forwarding/3-way call attempt has been detected. 
Understanding the importance to facility security of preventing inmates communicating with non- 
approved parties (including other inmates), GTL has over twenty (20) U.S. patents specifically related to 
the detection of thee-way call attempts. GTL’s call three-way call detection method is multi-layered and 
does not rely solely on audible clicks or other sounds but utilizes a variety of factors to detect 3- way 
call attempts. 
The system constantly analyzes the audio stream, looking for audio events with sufficient amplitude 
and duration to indicate that a three-way call may be occurring. When triggered, the three-way call 
analysis software breaks the audio stream into 10ms slices before and after the event for detailed 
analysis to determine if it is a three-way call. 
Triggers include detection of a tone or noise (such as call progress tones) in the middle of a 
conversation with a specific degree of amplitude and duration that indicates that the tone is not part of 
the conversation. 
This three-way call detection method does not listen for frequencies which can be problematic but 
rather audio events or aberrations of sufficient amplitude and duration that are not part of normal 
conversation. 

--- PAGE BREAK ---

© 2021 GTL 
Lake County RFP No. 21019 | 63 
 
 
The call will be flagged in the call detail record and made available for review in a report designed for 
this specific purpose (“Three-Way/Energy Detect”). 
 
 
GTL Technology – Cell Phone Three-Way Detection 
As technology has evolved for wireless networks (and traditional public telephone networks), the 
detection of when a digital signal is interrupted to add another digital signal is increasingly more difficult 
than when calls were carried by analog-only landline networks. The cleanliness of the signal does not 
become disrupted and wireless carriers are less interested in signaling regarding the addition of another 
line since they no longer charge for the feature of three-way calling and cell phone plans no longer 
demand to know what “minutes” are being consumed. GTL is now employing patented technology to 
leverage the signaling employed by wireless and VoIP networks to identify when a party that has been 
called by an inmate from our telephone platform attempts to employ three-way call joining to that 
inmate call. Although not every carrier that GTL partners with provides the access to signaling required 
to employ this technology, the technology is 100% efficient at detecting these three-way attempts when 
the carrier does support it.

2. The proposed system shall prevent the inmate from receiving a second dial 
tone, or “chain-dialing”. 
 
GTL Response: GTL has read, understands and will comply. 
 
GTL’s ITS will allow completion of only one dialed number per individual attempt. Any attempt to dial 
extra digits during a call attempt will be blocked. The inmate must hang up (terminating the first call), 
re-enter his/her PIN and redial to place another call, thereby eliminating the potential for chain dialing 
or other phone fraud. 
 
 
3. The proposed system shall prevent extra digits dialed by the inmate after 
the party has accepted the call. In your RFP response, please describe the 
process and how this is averted. 
 
GTL Response: GTL has read, understands and will comply. 
 
The Extra Dialed Digit filter defines the maximum DTMF tones that will be 
accepted in the called number sequence; generally, this will be set for NANP 
10 digits. If an inmate attempts to dial extra digits (beyond the filter specified) 
after the called party has accepted the call, the ITS will detect the DTMF 
tones and terminate the call. Alternatively, the system can allow the call to 
continue for purposes of obtaining actionable intelligence. In either case, the 
call will be flagged in the database.
