SUSTAINABILITY STATEMENT 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
OCTOBER 2023 
 
 
 
The County of Lake has a responsibility to balance fiscal, environmental, and social considerations into its operational 
decision‐making process.  The County’s commitment to green and sustainable practices and good environmental 
stewardship was memorialized by the Lake County Board in the County’s 2013 Strategic Plan where sustainability is 
listed as a value and a goal.  This was further strengthened in September 2020 by adopting a Net Zero Emissions goal 
through Joint Resolution.  Therefore, we shall promote environmentally preferable purchasing, whenever practical, by 
procuring goods or services that lessen the destructive effects on the environment and the health and well‐being of all 
citizens.   
Consideration of the practices adopted by our contracted firms is key to magnifying the impact of the County’s 
sustainability measures. Proposers are requested to provide a Statement of Sustainability demonstrating the methods 
they have incorporated into their firms.  Sustainability may be one of the scoring criteria included the evaluation rubric 
for the award of this contract.   
INSTRUCTIONS 
Please provide a narrative outlining any policies or practices implemented by your firm to reduce your carbon footprint.  
Your response should include, but need not be limited to: 
 
A copy of your firm’s sustainability policy, awards, and accolades.   
 
Practices such as waste minimization, energy/water efficiency, methods instituted to reduce pollution, green 
products utilized, staff education, community involvement and volunteerism.    
o Specifically include the percentage of your firm’s energy that comes from renewable sources and 
percentage of your fleet that is non‐emitting. 
 
Sustainable approaches your firm may have for this specific project.   
 
Cost variances to incorporate a more sustainable approach to this project and any calculated life cycle costs.

     Vendor Certification Definitions 
 
Minority‐owned business (MBE) 
A business concern which is at least 51% owned by one or more minority persons, or in the case of a corporation, at least 51% of 
the stock in which is owned by one or more minority persons; and the management and daily business operations of which are 
controlled by one or more of the minority individuals who own it. 
 
Woman‐owned business (WBE) 
A business which is at least 51% owned by one or more women, or, in the case of a corporation, at least 51% of the stock in which 
is owned by one or more women; and the management and daily business operations of which are controlled by one or more of 
the women who own it. 
 
Veteran‐owned Business Enterprise (VBE)  
A small business (i) that is at least 51 percent owned, controlled and managed by one or more Eligible Veterans or in the case of 
a corporation, at least 51 percent or more of the stock of which is owned, controlled and managed by one or more Eligible 
Veterans.  
 
Eligible Veteran means a person who (i) has been either a member of the armed forces of the United States or, while a citizen 
of the United States, was a member of the armed forces of allies of the United States in time of hostilities with a foreign 
country and (ii) has served under one or more of the following conditions: (a) the veteran served a total of at least 6 months; 
(b)  the  veteran  served  for  the  duration  of  hostilities  regardless  of  the  length  of  the  engagement;  (c)  the  veteran  was 
discharged on the basis of hardship; or (d) the veteran was released from active duty because of a service connected disability 
and was discharged under honorable conditions.  
 
Armed Forces of the United States means the United States Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, Coast Guard, or service in 
active duty as defined under 38 U.S.C. Section 101. Service in the Merchant Marine that constitutes active duty under Section 
401 of federal Public Act 95‐202 shall also be considered service in the armed forces for purposes of this Division.  
 
Persons with Disabilities Owned Business Enterprise (PDBE)  
A small business (i) that is at least 51 percent owned. controlled and managed by one or more Persons with a Disability; or in the 
case of a corporation, at least 51 percent or more of the stock of which is owned, controlled, and managed by one or more Persons 
with a Disability.  
 
Disability or Disabled means, with respect to an individual, a physical or mental impairment that substantially limits one or 
more of the major life activities of the individual, a record of physical or mental impairment that substantially limits one or 
more of the major life activities of the individual, or being regarded as an individual with a physical or mental impairment 
that substantially limits one or more of the major life activities of the individual.  
 
Service‐Disabled Veteran‐owned Business Enterprise (SDVBE)  
A small business (i) that is at least 51 percent owned, controlled, and managed by one or more qualified service‐disabled veterans 
or in the case of a corporation, at least 51 percent or more of the stock of which is owned, controlled and managed by one or 
more Service Disabled Veterans.  
 
Service‐Disabled Veteran means an Eligible Veteran who has been found to have 10 percent or more service‐connected 
disability by the United States Department of Veterans Affairs or the United States Department of Defense.  
 
Service‐connected disability means  a  disability  incurred  in  the  line  of duty  in  the active  military, naval  or  air  service  as 
described in 38 U.S.C. 101(16).  
 
BEP – Business Enterprise Program  
Business Enterprise Program (BEP) BEP assists businesses owned by minorities, women, and people with disabilities gain access 
to the State of Illinois procurement process. BEP certification with the State of Illinois can also open the door to opportunities 
with other public and private entities which are looking for diverse suppliers. 
 
Small Disadvantaged Businesses (SDB) 
A Small Disadvantaged Business (SDB) is a small business owned  and controlled by socially and economically disadvantaged 
individuals as defined by Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) 19.001 
 
Veteran‐Owned Small Business (VOSB) 
A Veteran‐Owned Small Business (VOSB) is a small business that is at least 51 percent owned by one or more veterans; or, if a 
publicly owned business, at least 51 percent of the stock is owned by one or more veterans.  Also, one or more veterans control 
management and daily business operations of the firm. 
 
Local business 
Lake County launched a Buy Local. Build Local. Work Local initiative in 2013 to increase the outreach and procurement 
opportunities for businesses located within Lake County, including women‐owned businesses and minority‐owned business 
enterprises (L/W/MBE). The overarching objective is to maximize participation from these businesses in the County’s procurement 
process, in accordance with applicable law.

SOI Topics: Facilities Master Planning, Design of Facility Improvements, Construction Oversight 
Aux Sable Creek Basin and Westside WWTP Phosphorus Removal and Expansion 
Executive Summary
Reference: Allison Swisher, PE, Director of Public Utilities | 815.724.4222 | aswisher@jolietcity.org 
Start/End Dates: 2/2016-4/2019 | Eng. Fees: $1.2M | Team Personnel: Eric Cockerill, Nathan Cassity, Andrew Dow, Mark 
Rokita, Josh Rivera, Kim Gahagan 
 
Project Highlights 
▪ Optimized process improvements by modeling  
▪ Complex construction staging to tie new station 
into existing facility 
▪ Permit negotiations 
▪ Split flow to different clarifiers 
In anticipation of new permit limits, Joliet retained 
Donohue to provide planning services for 
implementing biological nutrient removal at all three 
Joliet treatment facilities. As a result of modeling 
the activated sludge system with BioWin, the 
evaluation concluded that the existing oxidation 
ditch at the Aux Sable Creek Basin WWTP would 
achieve improved treatment with increased flows to 
the WWTP. Through pump stations in the collection 
system, the City is able to divert flows from the 
Westside WWTP to the Aux Sable Creek Basin 
WWTP. 
The City of Joliet elected to maximize the capacity 
of the Aux Sable Creek Basin WWTP in order to 
reduce flows currently treated at the Westside 
WWTP. Increasing the capacity of the WWTP 
required renegotiating the discharge permit. 
Donohue assisted the City in working with IEPA and 
negotiating with the environmental groups for the 
new discharge permit. 
At Aux Sable WWTP, Donohue designed a new 
biological selector that is built upstream of the 
existing oxidation ditch. This selector structure allows 
the plant to operate in three different biological 
phosphorus removal arrangements, depending on 
specific operating conditions. This will allow the 
facility to maintain biological phosphorus removal 
(and avoid chemical use) through a broader range 
of operating conditions than if a different, less 
robust approach was taken. With the addition of 
two new final clarifiers, the selectors also provide 
additional biological treatment capacity to meet 
IEPA requirements for the plant expansion. In order 
to provide backup treatment capability in the event 
of biological upset, chemical addition facilities were 
added. By providing phosphorus removal 
biologically, the City is avoiding up to $220,000 in 
chemical costs each year compared to using only 
chemical treatment to meet the same goals.  
At the Westside WWTP, different site constraints 
and influent characteristics guided a decision to 
implement only 
chemical phosphorus 
removal facilities to 
meet the effluent limit. 
However, through the 
negotiations with IEPA 
and the environmental 
groups, Joliet will 
have the ability to 
trade phosphorus 
credits with its third treatment facility (Eastside 
WWTP) when phosphorus removal is achieved there 
in order to reduce its reliance on chemical addition. 
Due to the constrained site, implementing chemical 
phosphorus removal at the Westside WWTP saved 
the City $17 million in initial capital costs compared 
to a biological option. Once trading is implemented, 
the City is expected to avoid $50,000 in chemical 
costs (conservatively). 
In addition, the planning and design of the project 
included the ability to trade total phosphorus 
performance between two of its facilities to both 
meet the effluent limitations and lower operating 
costs once improvements are implemented at the 
Eastside WWTP. This is the first trading 
arrangement approved by the State of Illinois and 
paves the way for future similar arrangements at 
other facilities in Illinois. 
This project won an ACEC Engineering Excellence 
Special Achievement award and was selected for 
the Judges’ Choice award.  
 
“It has been our pleasure to work with Donohue on 
this project, and we are pleased to recommend 
their professional engineering services.” 
 – Allison Swisher, PE, City of Joliet,  
Director of Public Utilities

Disinfection Improvements Study 
Kenosha Water Utility, Wisconsin 
Reference: Curt Czarnecki, General Manager | 262.653.4306| cczarnecki@kenosha.org 
Start/End Dates: 3/2020-6/2020 | Eng. Fees: $1.2M | Team Personnel: Nathan Cassity, Andrew Dow 
 
Project Highlights 
▪ Treatment and hydraulic capacity analysis 
De scription of
Co mpany
The Kenosha Water Utility’s (KWU) wastewater 
treatment plant (WWTP) has a rated average day 
flow capacity of 25 mgd and a peak flow capacity 
of over 100 mgd. The disinfection system was 
originally designed for a peak flow of 85 mgd and 
a year-round fecal coliform limit of 400 count/ 
100 ml monthly geometric mean. In 2020, Kenosha, 
along with other facilities throughout Wisconsin, 
were notified that the next permit renewal would 
include an E. coli limit of 126 count/100 mL monthly 
geometric mean and no more than 10% of samples 
exceeding 410 count/100 mL in a month. The new 
disinfection limits are more stringent. 
Kenosha retained Donohue to evaluate the 
disinfection system chemical dosing capacity and 
hydraulic capacity. Kenosha was also concerned 
about gas safety and included gas safety 
improvements in the study.  
The disinfection study evaluated the existing dosing 
capacity and operating data including the chlorine 
dose, chlorine residual, detention time, contact time 
(CT), and 
effluent E. 
coli. Based 
upon existing 
operating 
data, the 
minimum CT 
value 
required to 
meet the E. 
coli limits was 
determined to be 20. Using the minimum CT and the 
existing chlorine demand, Donohue sized a chlorine 
feed system for the new E. coli limits and 112 mgd 
plant capacity. Donohue recommended increasing 
the feed capacity to 1,500 lbs/day. Kenosha 
implemented the recommended improvements. 
 
The chlorine contact tank hydraulic capacity was 
also evaluated. Due to hydraulic restrictions, the 
secondary treatment process becomes flooded 
during peak flow events. Donohue developed a 
hydraulic model, worked with Kenosha to obtain 
field data during wet weather, and calibrated and 
validated the model. Donohue recommended 
improvements to the gates and channels between 
the chlorine contact tanks to increase capacity to 
112 mgd. A new chlorine contact tank is also 
recommended to increase the detention time and 
CT. Kenosha will implement the improvements in the 
near future. 
The safety concerns with the gaseous systems include 
pressurized piping from tanks to vacuum regulators 
and inadequate ventilation systems to maintain 
acceptable room temperatures. Donohue 
recommended implementation of vacuum regulators 
directly on the tanks with vacuum piping to the gas 
feeders to reduce the risk of pressurized gas 
escaping into the building. Donohue also 
recommended additional heating and air circulation 
to improve ventilation and room temperatures. 
Kenosha implemented the recommended safety 
improvements. 
 
“Their process of analysis and thoroughness was 
extremely beneficial...They listened to our needs 
and requests and provided us with the information 
required to make the tough decisions. Their detailed 
design, analysis and engineering made for a very 
successful installation.” – Melissa Arnot, PE, Kenosha 
Water Utility, Assistant General Manager

SOI Topics: Facilities Master Planning, Risk Assessments 
2050 Facilities Plan Using Asset Management Approach 
Milwaukee Metropolitan Sewerage District, Milwaukee, Wisconsin 
Reference: Greg Hottinger, Asset Management Manager, Milwaukee MSD | 414.225.2059 | ghottinger@mmsd.com 
Start/End Dates: 1/2015-2/2021 | Eng. Fees: $600,000 | Team Personnel: Craig Heisel, Bill Marten 
 
Project Highlights 
▪ Project had three timeline horizons: near term, 
20 years, and 35 years 
▪ Focus of the alternatives analysis was on 
biosolids and energy components, which had the 
highest risk rankings and highest financial 
impacts 
The Milwaukee 
Metropolitan 
Sewerage 
District (MMSD) 
retained a 
team of 
consultants to 
develop a 
comprehensive 
facilities plan 
to meet 
MMSD’s needs through the year 2050 using an 
asset management approach. Donohue was a key 
member of the Facilities Plan team and worked 
closely with the District, their O&M contractor, and 
other consultants throughout the progressive phases 
of the Facilities Plan. 
The major equipment and facilities for each of the 
District’s systems (collection system, Jones Island [JI] 
and South Shore [SS] Water Reclamation Facilities 
[WRF], and biosolids systems) were reviewed for 
asset condition, capacity, life expectancy, and 
performance. Evaluations included visual inspections 
of the facilities with key O&M staff and O&M staff 
interviews. 
The results of the inspections, interviews, and 
database analysis were then used to conduct a risk 
assessment for prioritizing the capital program and 
budgets. 
The plan established Level of Service goals and 
standardized consequences for not meeting the 
goals. The goals include permit compliance, energy, 
safety, fiscal responsibility, and others. Facilities, 
systems, unit processes, and equipment were 
evaluated for their likelihood of failure and the 
resulting consequences. Weighted Risk Scores 
(consequence of failure x likelihood of failure) were 
developed from this analysis. 
The facilities, systems, and unit processes that were 
identified as high risk received an alternatives 
analysis to identify the most advantageous 
alternative to mitigate the risks, including the cost-
benefit. In addition, Donohue worked 
collaboratively with the O&M staff to identify 
equipment or systems that posed a significant risk to 
operations, performance, or capacity due to a 
single point failure. 
By using a common risk-based approach and 
evaluation process for all assets, the District is able 
to prioritize those projects that will provide greatest 
risk reduction at the lowest capital cost. 
Additionally, Donohue has developed a biosolids 
mass and energy balance for both JI and SS. This 
tool is used for evaluating the mitigation 
alternatives using another standard protocol: 
Business Case Evaluations (BCEs). 
The BCEs provide a standard approach for 
evaluating and determining the value of the risk 
mitigation alternatives across all of the asset 
classifications. 
The BCEs provide a summary of the risk description, 
quantify the risk on a standard scale, assess the 
impact of the risk against each service level 
(environmental, financial, etc.), summarize the 
mitigation strategies and their capital and life cycle 
costs, and rank each strategy for its effectiveness at 
mitigating the risk. 
The highest ranked strategies are entered into an 
overall capital plan that schedules the projects in a 
manner that most effectively reduces risks and 
meets the District’s capital budget plans.

Springbrook WRC Facility Plan 
Springbrook Water Reclamation Center, Naperville, Illinois 
Reference: Joe Slevnik, Operations Manager, City of Naperville | 630.420.6125 | slevnikj@naperville.il.us 
Start/End Dates: 6/2020-6/2021 | Eng. Fees: $255,000 | Team Personnel: Eric Cockerill, Nathan Cassity, Natalie Cook, 
Michelle Madrid 
 
Project Highlights 
▪ Comprehensive, site-specific planning for the 
future 
Pr oject Management
Te am
Facility Planning Services 
The City of Naperville is one of the fastest growing 
communities in the Chicago metropolitan area and is 
the fourth largest city in Illinois. Its population is 
approximately 142,000 and it is expected to grow 
to approximately 160,000 in the near future.  
 
The City of Naperville Water Utilities owns and 
operates Springbrook Water Reclamation Center 
(SWRC), treating wastewater from Naperville and 
City of Warrenville, serving a population of 
162,563. SWRC uses a conventional activated 
sludge wastewater treatment process with 
secondary and tertiary treatment at a rated design 
capacity of 26.25 million gallons per day (mgd). 
Donohue has led and performed numerous 
evaluations for the City of Naperville to plan for 
anticipated nutrient removal requirements. Based on 
that background, the City selected Donohue and to 
help develop a Facility Plan for the facility with 
prioritized 5-year, 10-year and 20-year Capital 
Improvement Program recommendations. The effort 
included: 
▪ Comprehensive existing facilities asset evaluation 
▪ Risk assessment and prioritized rehabilitation 
evaluation 
▪ Detailed disinfection alternative evaluation 
▪ Capacity and regulatory improvement needs 
evaluation 
▪ Development of the Facility Plan Report 
▪ Development of 5-year, 10-year, and 20-year 
detailed capital planning cost and implementation 
schedules 
▪ Staffing Evaluation 
In order to meet the specific constraints of the 
SWRC, the facility plan recommended a solution 
that combined proven technologies with innovative 
components, such as S2EBPR, in order to reduce 
operating and capital costs. 
The Facility Plan identified over $125M in projects 
over the next 20 years in order to meet the facilities 
needs for existing asset rehabilitation, capacity 
increases, and regulatory compliance. These 
improvements were laid out in an iterative 
approach that both meet regulatory deadlines and 
spreads costs over the study period.

SOI Topics: Facilities Master Planning, Design of Facility Improvements, Construction Oversight 
Facility-Wide Upgrade at 36-mgd WWTF 
Wausau, Wisconsin 
Reference: Eric Lindman, Director of Public Works | 715-261-6740 | eric.lindman@ci.wausau.wi.us 
Start/End Dates: 3/2017-Present | Eng. Fees: $8.6M | Team Personnel: Jay Bielanski, Jason Edwards,  
Peter Larson, Bart Godziejewicz, Carl Erickson, Josh Rivera, Andrew Dow, Allen Howe, Dave Goecks,  
William Marten, Craig Heisel, Jeff Wills, Nathan Cassity, Wendy Raisbeck, Craig Schuenemann,  
Tom Crouse, Kim Gahagan 
 
Project Highlights 
▪ Led planning and design efforts for 
improvements to Wausau’s aging WWTF 
▪ Provided engineering services during construction 
for project duration 
▪ Activated sludge modifications for enhanced 
biological phosphorus removal and settling 
performance 
▪ New tertiary phosphorus removal system for low 
level phosphorus compliance 
 
Faced with aging equipment and future lower 
effluent permit limits, Donohue led facility planning 
efforts, design improvements, and construction 
oversight to Wausau’s 8-mgd average/36-mgd 
peak wastewater treatment facility. As part of 
comprehensive facilities planning, Donohue 
evaluated the condition and capacity of the plant’s 
existing tertiary sand filters to meet future flows, 
waste load allocated BOD limits, and impending 
phosphorus TMDL limits. 
The evaluation revealed that the existing sand 
filters have inadequate capacity. The goal was to 
develop a cost-effective phosphorus TMDL 
compliance strategy for increasing capacity and 
achieving consistent compliance. The City selected to 
proceed with disc filtration and activated sludge 
improvements that support enhanced biological 
phosphorus removal.  
Donohue designed a tertiary filtration system for 
low level phosphorus removal to treat up to 
20 mgd. The disk filters and upstream rapid-mix, 
coagulation, and flocculation system fit within the 
existing filter building.  
Donohue also developed an innovative activated 
sludge system designed to achieve full nitrification 
while maximizing process performance and 
flexibility, energy efficiency, and production of 
biological alkalinity. The system included a four-
stage anoxic/anaerobic selector basin, high 
efficiency turbo blowers, clarifier upgrades for 
improved flow splitting and settling performance, 
modifications to the aeration basins to form two 
3-pass basins, and dual grid aeration diffuser 
systems for increased aeration flexibility and turn-
down. 
Other improvements include a new administration 
building, raw wastewater screening, raw 
wastewater pumping, grit removal, primary flow 
splitting, primary treatment and solids pumping, 
secondary flow splitting improvements, effluent 
pumping, secondary effluent pumping, UV 
disinfection, primary solids screening, primary 
thickening, WAS thickening, digestion system 
enhancements, dewatering, biosolids drying, and 
biosolids storage improvements.  
Donohue provided contract administration, 
construction oversight, and engineering reviews 
throughout the full construction period. 
The $78M construction project is nearing completion.

◼  Project Management Team – 1  ◼ 
Project Management Team 
 
Project Approach 
Several underlying principles serve as the foundation of our project management approach:  
Capable Project Manager – With any engineering project, strong project management is critical to success. 
The project must be driven by a sound work plan and an experienced Project Manager empowered to 
implement that plan. 
Our Project Manager for these project(s) will be Nathan Cassity, located in our Sheboygan office. Nathan will 
be supported by our Chicago office manager, Eric Cockerill, who has the authority to direct the Donohue 
Team members in an effective manner for project success. He will make assignments and be responsible for 
the delivery of each project task. Nathan will work and communicate directly with the County-assigned 
individuals responsible for managing these project(s).  
Workshops at Key Milestones – We take pride in our “workshop” approach. These workshops facilitate 
information sharing and ideas exchange, solicit LCPW preferences and requirements, and ultimately result in 
decision making in a Team setting. Because our objective on any project is to arm the LCPW staff with the 
information necessary to make sound decisions, we view these workshops as a critical forum to provide this 
information. We have also found these Workshops are an effective means of developing teamwide consensus 
throughout a project as the various parties participate in open round-table discussions and obtain ownership 
of decisions. 
Make Quality Control Integral to the Process – We always aim to produce projects and individual project 
deliverables that meet or exceed expectations. To this end, we develop and then embrace a rigorous Quality 
Assurance & Quality Control Plan. We recognize that the execution of this Plan is essential to success for two 
reasons. First, it insures, at a minimum, that we meet the expectations of our clients. Second, sound quality 
control is always good business. At the onset of the planning and design phases, we will submit our Quality 
Assurance & Quality Control Plan to the Stakeholders for their review and approval. 
Project Personnel 
In 2022, Donohue provided over $28 million in engineering services to Midwest clients. At our current staff 
level, we have 220,000 hours annually of engineering and project management time to respond to our clients’ 
needs. What sets Donohue apart from most consultants is our culture of adhering to the committed schedule. 
Project Managers are held accountable to every project’s schedule, as well as budget. Every other Monday, 
Donohue’s Production Coordinator (Emily Wehmeyer) and a Senior Principal conduct project status-briefing 
sessions with each Project Manager to provide an objective, independent tracking of the project’s progress. 
Emily’s job is to allocate, manage, and balance labor resources for all projects in all offices. This allows us to 
proactively develop a coordinated resource strategy so that each project meets deliverable deadlines. 
Emily’s goal is to assign personnel to a project who are fully capable and qualified to complete the 
assignment and who have the availability to complete the work in a timely manner. 
Based on our review of projects coming to conclusion and other potential projects that have a reasonable 
probability of occurring, we will have adequate resources and availability to complete your project(s) 
successfully with the personnel listed on the organization chart. 
The organization chart and table on the next pages identify the proposed team personnel, their roles, and 
abbreviated qualifications.

◼  Budget – 1  ◼ 
Budget 
Method to Maintain Project Budget 
At Donohue, we know that meeting the budget constraints is integral to the successful delivery of the project. 
Nathan Cassity, our Project Manager, will have primary responsibility for delivering the project on budget. 
The following specific methods are used to manage our projects within the expectations of our clients. 
▪ Plan: Donohue’s QA/QC requires a project work plan at the start of each project. This plan defines the 
scope, budget, schedule, project team, and major deliverables for the project. 
▪ Schedule: We believe that developing a comprehensive project schedule and maintaining that schedule 
influences over 90% of the impact on project budget. The sequence of project tasks will be developed and 
will identify the critical path to maintain schedule. The schedule will be updated throughout the project. 
▪ Communicate: Donohue’s Project Manager conducts weekly project status reviews with each task leader. In 
addition, Craig Brunner, PE, the firm’s President, meets quarterly with all Project Managers to review status 
of project objectives, budget, and schedule. The Project Manager will also keep LCPW staff fully informed 
through monthly written status reports and bi-weekly conference calls, as well as more frequent informal 
calls or emails as issues arise. 
▪ Monitor: Donohue utilizes Deltek Vision (accounting software) and Excel as budget control tools. During the 
project, each task is evaluated based on an “earned value” analysis on a weekly or monthly basis. 
Decision Making and Value Engineering Approach 
During the planning and design process, we strive to provide the most cost-
effective project possible, both in terms of immediate capital cost and long-
term operating costs. Successful project delivery requires well defined 
objectives and a systematic approach to achieve them. We will work closely 
with the LCPW to define specific Project objectives and then strive to meet 
those objectives while maximizing the value of the project. 
With elements and the project objectives in mind, we focus our value 
engineering efforts during the planning phase of the project, when the 
decisions made have the greatest potential impact on project cost and long-
term performance. Our process includes the following steps: 
1. System Needs/Alternatives Brainstorming Workshop: During the initial phase of the project, Donohue will 
work with LCPW to identify “system needs” for the proposed project.  
2. Alternatives Evaluation and Screening: The alternatives carried forward from the System 
Needs/Alternatives Identification will be conceptually developed and evaluated for the purposes of 
comparing and screening alternatives. 
3. Alternatives Development: Alternatives carried forward from the Alternatives Screening will be 
developed in detail to facilitate an evaluation of their cost.  
4. Alternatives Analysis/Selection Workshop: Following our initial evaluation of the developed alternatives, 
we will conduct an Alternatives Analysis/Selection Workshop to review and receive Stakeholder comments, 
particularly from the LCPW staff.  
If desired by the LCPW, Donohue welcomes a Value Engineering process following selection of the 
Recommended Alternative. Value Engineering provides an opportunity for stakeholders and independent 
reviewers to take a step back and evaluate the true benefits and costs of a project approach through an 
unbiased lens.

Quality of Documents 
 
As a firm specializing in wastewater system designs, we have developed a systematic and disciplined 
approach to designing these systems in a “First Things First,” logical manner. We refer to our standardized 
approach as simply our “Design Model.” The Donohue manual outlining the Design Model is followed dutifully 
by our design team and is often found on their desks dog-eared and tattered from heavy use. 
We adhere to this Design Model for two reasons: it maximizes quality, budget, and schedule and achieving 
successful project delivery. Time and again, experience has shown this systematic and disciplined approach 
allows us to consistently deliver large, complex projects that meet our client’s expectations on time, on budget, 
and with minimal “surprises.” A flowchart from our Design Model manual is shown below.

Donhue Sustainability Statement 
Environmental stewardship is inherent to this business and Donohue's philosophy. We have been 
incorporating sustainability/green features into our work environment and our projects since our firm 
was founded in 1997. In fact, Donohue has worked with several wastewater entities that have been 
pioneers in achieving net zero energy consumption (Sheboygan, Wis; St. Cloud, Minn.; Stevens Point, 
Wis.). 
In our offices, we are committed to minimizing waste through digital vs. paper copies, use sustainable 
building materials and energy-efficient equipment, encourage Zoom meetings when practical to 
minimize travel, and encourage other sustainable practices on a daily basis. 
On every project, we work closely with our clients to develop cost-effective and sustainable solutions 
to meet the challenges they face. We have both ENV SP and LEED-certified staff members to assist us. 
Our team is experienced at evaluating and selecting a variety of sustainable elements and will use 
our skills to create a design that meets your needs. Services and design approaches we will consider 
for your projects include the following.
 Stormwater management 
 Minimizing light pollution 
 Minimizing odors 
 Minimizing noise 
 Public engagement and collaboration 
 Energy efficiency and reduction 
 Renewable energy production 
 Resources recovery and reuse 
 Landfill diversion 
 Repurposing and reusing existing 
infrastructure 
 Recycled materials
Donohue consistently produces innovative, award-winning designs with sustainability components 
at the forefront. A Donohue-designed project has won a State’s highest engineering excellence 
award four times in the last 10 years (awarded to only one project in each state): three were 
awarded for innovative approaches to resource recovery and one for an innovative approach to 
low-level phosphorus removal.

STATEMENT OF INTEREST #23173 
STATE OF ILLINOIS 
Professional Architectural and Engineering Services  
for Lake County 
 
 
Notice is hereby given that Statements of Interest (SOI) (one electronic unprotected copy) will be received from qualified, 
professional architectural  and  engineering  firms  interested  in  providing  the  services,  as  described  herein.    Firms  are 
encouraged  to  submit  the  minimum  amount  of  information  as  necessary  to  indicate  their  interest,  experience,  and 
qualifications to perform the work described for any project, service, or combination thereof. In accordance with the 
instructions on the SOI cover page, responses will be accepted electronically until December 1, 2023, 11:00 a.m., local 
time. 
 
CONTACT / QUESTIONS: Please submit questions on our website at http://lakecountypurchasingportal.com by selecting 
the SOI number and addendum link.  Questions may also be submitted via email to purchasing@lakecountyil.gov.  All 
questions shall be submitted no less than seven (7) days prior to the SOI opening date. No interpretation of the meaning 
of the Statement of Interest will be made orally. Failure to request an interpretation constitutes a waiver to later claim 
that  ambiguities  or  misunderstandings  caused  a  firm  to  improperly  submit  a  proposal.    Lake  County  will  review 
submissions and conduct negotiations in accordance with the Local Government Professional Services Selection Act (50 
ILCS 510/). 
 
LAKE COUNTY 
Lake County is in northeast Illinois, between the Chicago and Milwaukee metropolitan areas. Lake County is home to more 
than  700,000  residents.  Lake  County  is  committed  to  open  government  and  transparency,  and  the  County  board’s 
conservative fiscal policies have allowed the County to maintain fiscal stability and achieve AAA bond rating from Standard 
& Poor’s and Moody’s. Lake County is governed by a 19‐member board and managed by a County Administrator. 
 
PROJECT BACKGROUND 
The Lake County Public Works Department (LCPWD) provides essential water and wastewater services to over 40% of 
the County’s residents, and the LCPWD owns and operates three water reclamation facilities, twelve public water 
distribution systems, nearly 800 miles of sewer and water mains, and all associated infrastructure.  Additionally, the 
LCPWD ownership portfolio includes many buildings, including administrative offices, maintenance facilities, and 
operational buildings (pump houses, lift stations, treatment processes housing).  The LCPWD has undertaken a strategic 
prioritization process to identify critical capital improvement projects.  Major capital projects are anticipated for the 
foreseeable future, with an estimated annual value of $10 to $20 million.  Federal and state infrastructure funding 
sometimes contribute additional money to the capital program; including $400,000 in USEPA grant funds for Water 
Reclamation Plant design and $1,000,000 in American Rescue Plan Act (ARPA) funds for Antioch Township Sanitary 
Sewer design efforts.  Most projects are funded through the Enterprise fund based on user rates and connection fee 
charges. 
 
GENERAL QUALIFICATIONS 
The proposing firm should have experience providing engineering services for wastewater reclamation facilities, including, 
but not limited to:  facilities master planning for capital planning, risk assessments, design of facility improvements, 
consulting, oversight of construction, operational optimization studies, operational staffing evaluations.   The statement 
of interest must provide your firm’s qualifications, including ability of professional staff, its past record and experience, 
performance data on file, willingness to meet time and budget requirements, location, workload of the firm, work on 
similar projects, past performance on similar projects and financial stability related to design, bidding, and construction 
inspection for such projects.   
 
INTENT – WASTEWATER RECLAMATION FACILITIES MASTER PLANNING, RISK ASSESSMENTS, CONSULTING, ANALYSIS, 
IMPROVEMENT DESIGN AND CONSTRUCTION OVERSIGHT  

--- PAGE BREAK ---

3 
 
Lake County Public Works (LCPW) owns and operates three water reclamation facilities:  Mill Creek Water Reclamation 
Facility (1 MG daily flow), New Century Town Water Reclamation Facility (NCT WRF) (3.5 MG daily flow) and Des Plaines 
River Water Reclamation Facility (DPR WRF) (11 MG daily flow).  LCPW is seeking the following services:    
 
 
Facility master plans for capital planning 
 
Risk assessments that prioritize repair/replacement by risk of failure; establish a multi‐year replacement 
program utilizing a risk‐based asset ranking approach and provide an estimated schedule to prepare the 
preliminary engineering report 
 
Operational optimization studies and staffing evaluations  
 
Design of facility improvements, including preparing all necessary surveys, preliminary and final engineering 
studies, design documents, and bid documents 
 
Design services include conducting alternatives evaluations and evaluating life cycle costs and operational needs 
 
Secure all permit approvals and provide as‐built record drawings, operational manuals and related documents 
 
Construction oversight, including resident engineer services, evaluating shop drawings, monitoring construction, 
preparing change orders, and reviewing contractor pay requests 
It is expected that all projects will be highly complex and include multifaceted coordination sequencing with 
stakeholders to maintain operational needs throughout the project. All WRFs must remain in operation at all times, 
including the event of high flows.  Engage with stakeholders and the public, as necessary.

SUBMISSION REQUIREMENTS 
The SOI shall include a brief description of why the applicant should be chosen for the project. The SOI should be concise 
and directly relate to the SOI description. The SOI content shall be limited to 14 pages (not including required forms).  A 
minimum of five firms may be selected as the most qualified from their SOI submittal to make a presentation to a 
selection committee. The final selection may result in three consultant agreements, and task orders would be assigned 
to the firm(s) at the discretion of LCPWD. 
 
The County, in accordance with the laws of the State of Illinois, hereby notifies all firms expressing statements of 
interest that it will affirmatively insure that the contract entered into pursuant to this advertisement will be awarded 
without discrimination on the grounds of race, color or national origin. 
 
Where appropriate, the responses provided under this section shall include a brief description of examples of outcome‐
based accomplishments that have measurable success factors to demonstrate a firm’s qualifications and expertise.  All 
examples should include owner name and contact information. 
 
SOI Format 
The proposal itself should adhere to the following format, be provided in the specific order listed, and include: 

--- PAGE BREAK ---

4 
 
 
Section 1.0 – Executive Summary (max. 2 pages, one of which is the firm’s professional licensing documentation) 
Provide a brief summary, which describes and highlights your interest, experience and qualifications for the project. 
Submit documentation to establish the professional licensing necessary to be eligible for the project. 
 
Section 2.0 – Description of Company (max. 1 page) 
Provide a description of the proposing company. 
 
a) Please provide an executive summary of your company. 
b) Provide your company’s full legal name, company mailing address and phone number. Indicate whether your 
company is a sole proprietorship, a partnership or a corporation.  Indicate the number of years in business. 
c) Describe your company’s organizational structure including parent company, affiliates, subsidiaries, strategic 
business alliances, and other related entities.  Include discussion regarding any changes in ownership. 
d) How  many  employees  does  your  company  currently  have  locally,  nationally,  and  globally?  Is  there 
redundancy in staff roles and disciplines? 
e) Describe the structure of your management team.  If available, please provide a copy of your company’s 
organizational chart. 
f) Please provide information regarding any strategic business alliances/partnerships and certifications that 
your company has that may affect how you will do business with the Lake County Public Works Department. 
g) Provide the name, title, email address, and telephone number of the person who will be the principal point 
of contact for your company relative to this SOI, including the person who will be the principal contact related 
to any contractual dialogue and administration of any agreement resulting from this SOI. 
 
Section 3.0 – Relevant Experience (max. 6 pages) 
Relevant Experience shall clearly indicate the firm and which member on the project team participated in all relevant 
experience submitted. All experience submitted for a team member while in the employment of a different firm shall 
include their title and role on the project as well as the firm name that held the contract for all work submitted for relevant 
experience. Provide a description of projects (minimum of three (3), maximum of six (6)) of a similar nature that would 
substantiate the qualifications of the applicant for the work.  Include contactable references for all projects. Quoted 
testimonials are welcomed. 
 
Section 4.0 – Project Management Team (max. 3 pages) 
Describe your approach relative to the delegation of responsibility and assignment of authority and interaction points with 
the County. Include a listing of key personnel and/or subconsultants proposed for the project team. Include a resume 
and/or relevant experience of each key team member and a brief description of the tenure or work history among team 
members.  Identify  how  you  will  address  the  following  disciplines:  site/civil,  structural,  mechanical,  electrical  and 
instrumentation. 
 
Provide an organization chart graphically illustrating how your firm would staff and structure your proposed team to 
perform the project. Include delegation of responsibility and assignment of authority and interaction points with the 
County. 
 
List specific personnel (including subconsultants) proposed for the project team, including the project assignment and role 
or area of responsibility of each individual. Identify the staff team and each members’ availability capacity throughout 
project stages.  Provide a resume for each proposed team member, specifically stating tenure or experience with your 
firm, experience and qualifications of each individual.  The above list represents a minimum submittal for key personnel 
from the Owner’s Representative and Project Manager Team. 
 
Section 5.0 – Budget (max. 1 page) 
Describe your firm’s methods to maximize and maintain quality project management and project image while maintaining 
tight budget constraints.  Cite examples of specific things that you have done on other projects to meet this objective. 
 
Describe your firm's approach to value engineering and what contribution does the firm  typically  provide  during  each 
phase of design and construction. Value engineering should include the ability of the proposer to evaluate cost saving

opportunities in the construction project and add value to the project by applying a creative approach to optimize lifecycle, 
energy consumption and operational costs resulting from the design. 
 
Section 6.0 ‐ Quality of Documents (max. 1 page) 
List the steps and describe the quality control processes we can expect from the firm during each phase of the design and

competent construction observation services. Identify your QA/QC process. 
 
EVALUATION PROCESS 
Lake County will review submissions and conduct negotiations in accordance with the Local Government Professional 
Services Selection Act (50 ILCS 510/). The following qualifications will be considered by the County: 
 
A. Proven  experience  in  design,  construction  observation  services  and  successful  implementation  of  similar 
projects with similar clients. Demonstration of issue resolution and operational optimization. 
B. The ability of the proposer to work with other consultants and contractors to maximize outcomes while 
maintaining budgetary discipline.  
C. Quality and time availability of key project team members including a proven consistent engineer team leader 
that listens to the client and appropriately manages and directs the team’s efforts. Possession of talent and 
redundancy in roles. 
D. Experience and capability of coordinating with internal and external stakeholders.   
 
As part of its evaluation process the County may seek additional information from firms found to have resources and 
methodologies best suited to the project. 
 
TERM OF AWARDED CONTRACTS 
The term of awarded contracts shall be in effect for a two (2) year period from the date of award (initial term).  Lake 
County reserves the right to renew the  contract for three (3) additional one (1) year periods, subject to acceptable 
performance by the Contractor.  At the end of any contract term, Lake County reserves the right to extend the contract 
for a period of up to sixty (60) days for the purpose of getting a new contract in place.  For any year beyond the initial 
year, the contract is contingent on the appropriation of sufficient funds.  No charges shall be assessed for failure of the 
County to appropriate funds in future contract years.

9. Domestic Preference for Procurements 
As appropriate and to the extent consistent with law, Lake County, to the greatest extent practicable under a Federal 
award of American Rescue Plan dollars, provides a preference for the purchase, acquisition, or use of goods, products, or 
materials  produced  in  the  United  States  (including  but  not  limited  to  iron,  aluminum,  steel,  cement,  and  other 
manufactured products) 
a) For purposes of this section:  
(1) “Produced in the United States” means, for iron and steel products, that all manufacturing processes, from the initial 
melting stage through the application of coatings, occurred in the United States.  
(2) “Manufactured products” means items and construction materials composed in whole or in part of non‐ferrous metals 
such as aluminum; plastics and polymer‐based products such as polyvinyl chloride pipe; aggregates such as concrete; glass, 
including optical fiber; and lumber. 
 
10. Prohibition on Certain Telecommunications  
The proposed equipment will not utilize covered telecommunications equipment or services as a substantial or essential 
component of any system, or as critical technology as part of any system. As described in Public Law 115‐232, section 889, 
covered telecommunications equipment is telecommunications equipment produced by Huawei Technologies Company 
or ZTE Corporation (or any subsidiary or affiliate of such entities). 
 
11. Minority and Women Business Enterprises.   
Contractor hereby agrees to comply with the following when applicable: The requirements of Executive Orders 11625 and 
12432 (concerning Minority Business Enterprise), and 12138 (concerning Women's Business Enterprise), when applicable. 
Accordingly, the contractor hereby agrees to take affirmative steps to assure that women and minority businesses are 
utilized when possible as sources [12] of supplies, equipment, construction and services. Affirmative steps shall include 
the following:  
1. Including qualified women’s business enterprises and small and minority businesses on solicitation lists.  
2.  Assuring  that  women’s  enterprises  and  small  and  minority  businesses  are  solicited  whenever  they  are 
potential sources.  
3.  When economically feasible, dividing total requirements into smaller tasks or quantities so as to permit 
maximum participation by small and minority business, and women’s business enterprises.  
4. Where  the  requirement  permits,  establishing  delivery  schedules  which  will  encourage  participation  by 
women’s business enterprises and small and minority business; and 
5. Using the services and assistance of the Small Business Administration, and the U.S. Office of Minority Business 
Development  Agency  of  the  Department  of  Commerce;  and  the  North  Carolina  Office  for  Historically 
Underutilized Businesses.  
 
For the purposes of these requirements, a Minority Business Enterprise (MBE) is defined as an enterprise that is at least 
51 percent owned and controlled in its daily operation by members of the following groups: Black, Hispanic, Asian or 
Pacific Islander, American Indian, or Alaskan Natives. A Women Business Enterprise (WBE) is defined as an enterprise that 
is at least 51 percent owned and controlled in its daily operation by women. Additionally, an MBE or WBE qualifies if it is 
currently certified as a North Carolina “historically underutilized business” under N.C.G.S. §143‐128.4(a) and qualifies as a 
“small business” if it is independently owned and operated and is qualified under the Small Business Administration 
criteria and size standards at 13 C.F.R. Part 21.

15 
 
 
SUSTAINABILITY STATEMENT 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
OCTOBER 2023 
 
 
 
The County of Lake has a responsibility to balance fiscal, environmental, and social considerations into its operational 
decision‐making process.  The County’s commitment to green and sustainable practices and good environmental 
stewardship was memorialized by the Lake County Board in the County’s 2013 Strategic Plan where sustainability is 
listed as a value and a goal.  This was further strengthened in September 2020 by adopting a Net Zero Emissions goal 
through Joint Resolution.  Therefore, we shall promote environmentally preferable purchasing, whenever practical, by 
procuring goods or services that lessen the destructive effects on the environment and the health and well‐being of all 
citizens.   
Consideration of the practices adopted by our contracted firms is key to magnifying the impact of the County’s 
sustainability measures. Proposers are requested to provide a Statement of Sustainability demonstrating the methods 
they have incorporated into their firms.  Sustainability may be one of the scoring criteria included the evaluation rubric 
for the award of this contract.   
INSTRUCTIONS 
Please provide a narrative outlining any policies or practices implemented by your firm to reduce your carbon footprint.  
Your response should include, but need not be limited to: 
 
A copy of your firm’s sustainability policy, awards, and accolades.   
 
Practices such as waste minimization, energy/water efficiency, methods instituted to reduce pollution, green 
products utilized, staff education, community involvement and volunteerism.    
o Specifically include the percentage of your firm’s energy that comes from renewable sources and 
percentage of your fleet that is non‐emitting. 
 
Sustainable approaches your firm may have for this specific project.   
 
Cost variances to incorporate a more sustainable approach to this project and any calculated life cycle costs.

18 
 
     Vendor Certification Definitions 
 
Minority‐owned business (MBE) 
A business concern which is at least 51% owned by one or more minority persons, or in the case of a corporation, at least 51% of 
the stock in which is owned by one or more minority persons; and the management and daily business operations of which are 
controlled by one or more of the minority individuals who own it. 
 
Woman‐owned business (WBE) 
A business which is at least 51% owned by one or more women, or, in the case of a corporation, at least 51% of the stock in which 
is owned by one or more women; and the management and daily business operations of which are controlled by one or more of 
the women who own it. 
 
Veteran‐owned Business Enterprise (VBE)  
A small business (i) that is at least 51 percent owned, controlled and managed by one or more Eligible Veterans or in the case of 
a corporation, at least 51 percent or more of the stock of which is owned, controlled and managed by one or more Eligible 
Veterans.  
 
Eligible Veteran means a person who (i) has been either a member of the armed forces of the United States or, while a citizen 
of the United States, was a member of the armed forces of allies of the United States in time of hostilities with a foreign 
country and (ii) has served under one or more of the following conditions: (a) the veteran served a total of at least 6 months; 
(b)  the  veteran  served  for  the  duration  of  hostilities  regardless  of  the  length  of  the  engagement;  (c)  the  veteran  was 
discharged on the basis of hardship; or (d) the veteran was released from active duty because of a service connected disability 
and was discharged under honorable conditions.  
 
Armed Forces of the United States means the United States Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, Coast Guard, or service in 
active duty as defined under 38 U.S.C. Section 101. Service in the Merchant Marine that constitutes active duty under Section 
401 of federal Public Act 95‐202 shall also be considered service in the armed forces for purposes of this Division.  
 
Persons with Disabilities Owned Business Enterprise (PDBE)  
A small business (i) that is at least 51 percent owned. controlled and managed by one or more Persons with a Disability; or in the 
case of a corporation, at least 51 percent or more of the stock of which is owned, controlled, and managed by one or more Persons 
with a Disability.  
 
Disability or Disabled means, with respect to an individual, a physical or mental impairment that substantially limits one or 
more of the major life activities of the individual, a record of physical or mental impairment that substantially limits one or 
more of the major life activities of the individual, or being regarded as an individual with a physical or mental impairment 
that substantially limits one or more of the major life activities of the individual.  
 
Service‐Disabled Veteran‐owned Business Enterprise (SDVBE)  
A small business (i) that is at least 51 percent owned, controlled, and managed by one or more qualified service‐disabled veterans 
or in the case of a corporation, at least 51 percent or more of the stock of which is owned, controlled and managed by one or 
more Service Disabled Veterans.  
 
Service‐Disabled Veteran means an Eligible Veteran who has been found to have 10 percent or more service‐connected 
disability by the United States Department of Veterans Affairs or the United States Department of Defense.  
 
Service‐connected disability means  a  disability  incurred  in  the  line  of duty  in  the active  military, naval  or  air  service  as 
described in 38 U.S.C. 101(16).  
 
BEP – Business Enterprise Program  
Business Enterprise Program (BEP) BEP assists businesses owned by minorities, women, and people with disabilities gain access 
to the State of Illinois procurement process. BEP certification with the State of Illinois can also open the door to opportunities 
with other public and private entities which are looking for diverse suppliers. 
 
Small Disadvantaged Businesses (SDB) 
A Small Disadvantaged Business (SDB) is a small business owned  and controlled by socially and economically disadvantaged 
individuals as defined by Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) 19.001 
 
Veteran‐Owned Small Business (VOSB) 
A Veteran‐Owned Small Business (VOSB) is a small business that is at least 51 percent owned by one or more veterans; or, if a 
publicly owned business, at least 51 percent of the stock is owned by one or more veterans.  Also, one or more veterans control 
management and daily business operations of the firm. 
 
Local business 
Lake County launched a Buy Local. Build Local. Work Local initiative in 2013 to increase the outreach and procurement 
opportunities for businesses located within Lake County, including women‐owned businesses and minority‐owned business 
enterprises (L/W/MBE). The overarching objective is to maximize participation from these businesses in the County’s procurement 
process, in accordance with applicable law.

UNDERSTANDING LAKE COUNTY NEEDS
Our team understands that the proposed project encompass
Climate Response & Resiliency Considerations in Master Planning
As the world experiences the growing effects of climate change, future-focused 
analytics will be critical to prioritizing human safety and capital investments. 
Resilient Analytics, a Stanley Group Company, has developed proven climate, 
engineering and economic models that help clients understand how climate 
stressors will impact infrastructure and people, determining future fiscal costs 
and risks to communities and organizations. Resilient Analytics provides insight 
on adaption investments that are more cost-effective than reactive approaches 
and have worked in over 50 countries with clients ranging from county 
governments to international banks. Resilient Analytics produces actionable 
insights that have been used to identify millions of dollars in savings for 
clients while reducing the near-term and long-term risks to critical operations.
The key to Resilient Analytics capabilities is to monetize the impacts of climate 
change on communities. Examples include how rising temperatures impact 
both the infrastructure and the workforce. Rising temperatures could require 
additional cooling requirements in electrical rooms or pumping facilities. Another 
example includes how precipitation changes will impact Inflow and Infiltration 
within the collection system. Shorter duration and higher intensity storms will 
impact interceptors differently than longer duration 
lower intensity storms. At the direction of Lake County, 
Stanley Consultants can include climate change 
analytics in your planning and design projects resulting 
in longer-lasting and more resilient infrastructure.
Leveraging Advanced Technology
Stanley Consultants has employed 3D design 
technology and augmented reality for over a decade 
to improve design quality, better communicate 
project details to stakeholders, and incorporate 
asset information. However, this is just the tip of the 
iceberg. Working with our digital partners, Stanley 
Consultants can produce digital twins of collection 
and treatment systems that provides real-time 
operation information and alarms. Our approach 
incorporates smart SCADA systems, collection system hydraulic models, and real-time data from flow metering and wastewater quality 
sensors to monitor and forecast flows, run facility simulations, and train operators. The digital twin changes operations from reactive 
system monitoring to proactive facility operations. Digital twin visualizations can be later enhanced for effective operator training, 
seamless shift operator transfers, and emergency response planning.
Stanley Consultants is rooted in Innovation and 
Community Engagement. We are currently collaborating 
with the City of Wrangell, AK and Hach to integrate new 
Real Time Controller Instrumentation that leverages 
Machine Learning to optimize chemical dosing and reduce 
operational budgets. In addition, we are using 3D design 
software to create to scale 3D animations that have been 
presented to the project team and the City Council.
SCI leveraged our Design Generator to quickly develop several alternative facility plans for a 
new WWTP located outside Pheonix, AZ. The image above illustrated a MBR alternative that 
would allow the new WWTP to be located in an extremely tight footprint.

RELEVANT EXPERIENCE
3.0
Largest Fertilizer Plant in United States 
(WEVER, IOWA)
Stanley Consultants, Inc., designed major elements of the 
largest fertilizer plant in the United States, located in Southeastern 
Iowa. The Iowa Fertilizer Company’s new nitrogen-based fertilizer 
manufacturing facility produces anhydrous ammonia, nitric acid, 
granulated/liquid urea, urea ammonium nitrate (UAN), and diesel 
exhaust fluid (DEF) from natural gas feedstock. The site is located 
near the Mississippi River for barging, with easy access to rail 
network and major highways for trucking.
We provided initial studies, regulatory coordination, preliminary 
design, equipment procurement packages, and final design 
of seven well sites, six miles of 36-inch HDPE offsite water 
transmission main, water treatment, an oily wastewater 
pretreatment and process wastewater treatment plant for the 
facility.
The water treatment plant is a conventional lime sedimentation 
softening process requiring the application of various chemicals 
for softening and pH adjustment. Water used for cooling receives 
no additional treatment. Water used for the processes and boilers 
is filtered with the use of granular bed filters and stored in a 
two-million gallon capacity tank prior to distribution. Lime process 
residuals processing design included plate and frame presses for 
dewatering with bulk cake storage and load out building.
The pretreatment facilities included two gravity oil/water separators operating in parallel, followed by pH adjustment, coagulation, and 
flocculation, and two gravity dissolved air flotation (DAF) units. Process wastewater is treated biologically in a sequencing batch reactor 
(SBR) with two parallel trains that alternate cycles. The biological system is designed to meet effluent standards of less than 10 mg/l 
of total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN), 1 mg/l phosphorous, 20 mg/l biological oxygen demand (BOD), and 30 mg/l total suspended solids 
(TSS). The plant also included an influent/effluent pump station with submersible pumps, an aerobic digester/storage tank to provide 
180 days of thickened sludge storage, a diversion basin and multiple chemical feed systems.
Stanley Consultants also performed design of the site work, buildings, electrical and gas utilities, urea material handling, storage, 
stacker reclaimer, and conveyor systems, and truck and rail load out facilities.

RELEVANT EXPERIENCE
3.0
Camp Ripley Wastewater Treatment Plant 
Renovations (LITTLE FALLS, MINNESOTA)
Stanley Consultants, Inc., In 2014, the Camp Ripley Wastewater 
Treatment Plant was a 28-year old mechanical wastewater 
treatment plant that provided secondary treatment of wastewater 
generated by the highly used National Guard training camp prior 
to discharge to the Mississippi River. The aging plant faced age 
related issues including failing exterior insulation finish system 
(thin stucco over expanded polystyrene insulation), aging and 
deteriorating roof systems, deteriorating building mechanical 
systems, and aging and deteriorating process equipment. 
Functional issues included the entrance/gate configuration 
especially during winter, doors susceptible to freezing shut, 
exterior stairways susceptible to icing hazards, and several site 
and tank access safety concerns.
The State of Minnesota embarked on a wholesale renovation 
project to repair/replace these deficiencies to extend the viability 
of the plant. In addition, the State desired to include energy 
efficiency improvements such as added insulation and LED 
lighting. Stanley Consultants was selected to provide a condition 
assessment, master planning, and design services including pre-
design code analysis, facility, buildings, and site condition assessments (including thermal imaging) and report; design, bidding review, 
and construction phase assistance. After the condition assessment and planning meetings, Stanley Consultants and the State moved 
forward with the improvements described below.
The site access road alignment was shifted to allow better truck turn movements with the State Highway. Also, a new security gate and 
fence modifications facilitate winter operations. The site road was repaved with asphalt; and various sidewalk sections and building 
stoops were replaced to facilitate ADA access to the control building. All site lighting was converted to LED.
In addition to site improvements, the Control Building renovations included a remodeled administration area, lab, and bathroom/shower 
areas, replaced HVAC systems, a new hot water system, LED lighting, new metal roofing and exterior wall cladding, new exterior doors 
and entrance canopies, and vaulted ceiling modifications including additional insulation.
Outside of the Control Building, non-process related improvements included:
	»
Replacing the Clarifier’s aluminum dome stainless doors with new thermal breaks and heat tracing to prevent door freezing.
	»
Enclosing the Trickling Filter north-facing exterior stairs with a new curtain wall and roof system.
	»
A new Anaerobic Digester stairway and CMU wall were used to create new unclassified (non-explosion rated) boiler and electric 
rooms segregated from the explosion proof classified area of the digester building.
	»
A new exterior ships ladder was provided for improved safety when accessing digester roof.
	»
Digester/Garage building and digester tanks were provided with new insulation, metal cladding and exterior doors. The building 
roof included repair of deteriorated wooden rafters, added insulation, and new metal roofing.
The renovated plant has incorporated energy saving features, improved personnel safety and comfort, and greatly extended the life of 
the treatment facility.

Infrastructure Capacity Assessments (ICA) for Fort Carson, Fort 
Bliss, Pinon Canyon and Fort Stewart, as separate task orders 
under a large-scale task order project for Installation Management 
Command. Our team of planners and engineers reviewed and 
assessed each facility’s infrastructure capability to support the 
full execution of the critical missions in the future. The task orders 
were an extension of the master planning process for each 
installation to determine how the utility systems can support the 
long-range vision for the installation, making it more resilient and 
focused on mission execution.
Jeff Frye led the team that reviewed the existing capacity and 
demand for the water, waste-water and storm water systems, then 
developed recommendations on how to improve efficiency and 
resiliency, and estimated the future demands on each system. The 
future demands were calculated based on existing master plan 
documents guiding installation growth to ensure that the systems 
were built to handle increases in demand over time.
The team evaluated and integrated over a dozen Development 
Plans for each of the for facilities, existing condition assessments 
and data from interconnected off site utilities. After these reviews, 
the composite Future Development layer was created, typically 
consisting of several hundreds of known development projects for each site. Our team used the future development picture to forecast 
the anticipated utility system demand levels over the next 20 years for each facility. With this forecasted demand, our team then 
evaluated each localized area of each site and provided an assessment and recommendations for levels of service based on existing 
conditions, capacity, demand, and future conditions. These evaluations considered over 1,000 miles of water, wastewater and storm 
water infrastructure.
The ICA layered an analysis at the strategic (planning), operational (utility ownership and delivery), and tactical (routine maintenance 
program review) levels with unique challenges at each site. This provided a fresh look at the importance of underground utility 
planning and helped successfully bridge the gap between typical master planning exercises and real-world utility system upgrades and 
maintenance programs.
The final product integrated into the full site Infrastructure Master Plan, focused on the long-range growth of the installation and specific 
underground utility projects needed to support the growth. The ICA provided the installation with a clear, comprehensive analysis of 
each utility system with detailed and specific actions, with costs and schedule, to improve resilience and redundancy while achieving 
the long-range development plan for missions.
The assessment resulted in a final Infrastructure Master Plan focused on the long-range growth of the installation and specific utility 
projects needed to support that growth in a resilient manner. In addition, our team included specific policy considerations for each 
system; these were designed to ensure that the systems in question grow in concert with other installation, State, and Federal policies 
while leveraging lessons-learned and best practices.

RELEVANT EXPERIENCE
3.0
Scott Boulevard Trunk Sewer (IOWA CITY, IOWA)
Stanley Consultants, Inc., was retained by the City of Iowa City to 
develop a 30-year Sanitary Sewer Master Plan. The study included 
analysis of the collection system, population projections, land use 
estimates for future development areas, flow metering analysis and 
collection system hydraulic modeling and analysis.
The City of Iowa City hired Stanley Consultants to perform study, 
design, bidding, and limited construction phase services in the 
Spring of 2019 to extend the existing 30-inch Scott Boulevard 
Sanitary Trunk Sewer located on agricultural land extending between 
Highway 6 and American Legion Road. The sewer extension will 
serve future residential development from Iowa Interstate Railroad 
northward along the North Branch of Snyder Creek to American 
Legion Road. At the conclusion of the project the aging upstream 
Windsor Ridge pump station north of American Legion will be 
eliminated and served by this new trunk sewer extension.
The engineering study involved planning for the 6,000 feet of trunk 
sewer routing. Two potential sewer alignments were evaluated 
considering location of temporary and permanent easements, 
private property concerns and features, construction access, 
cultural resources, wetland and environmental impact, current and 
future storm water management, and future trail extension. During 
the study phase topographic survey, soil boring sampling, and cultural resources surveys were performed. A final route was selected 
with design progressing through production of 30, 60, 90, and 100 percent contract documents and cost estimates, permitting, client 
meetings, and public meetings.
Final routing and design resulted in approximately 5,200 feet of 24-inch and 30-inch sewer. The project included one wetland crossing 
and one creek crossing. Bid documents were structured with a base bid of unlined RCP sanitary sewer pipe and manholes plus an 
alternate bid of HDPE lined RCP. The construction cost estimate for the base bid unlined RCP option was $1.66M with the alternate bid 
lined HDPE option was $1.84M. Six responsive bids were received and the City selected the alternate bid to use lined HDPE pipe and 
utility holes option for $1.45M.

BUDGET
5.0
MAINTAINING COSTS
Cost control begins when the scope of the project is defined 
and a budget established. At this point, the scope of the project 
is defined in enough detail to provide confidence the budget 
established will not be exceeded.
Stanley Consultants’ project team controls and maintains 
project costs by allowing no deviation from the scope of the 
project as established. If, after design has begun, a change of 
scope is recommended by Lake County or Stanley Consultants, 
the proposed change will not be implemented until the cost 
implications are defined and the County approves the change in 
scope and budget.
MAINTAINING TIGHT BUDGET CONSTRAINTS
Our Project Manager, Michael Colby and lead discipline 
engineers, all have a vested interest in monitoring your project 
status with our resource planning and project management 
tools and will take corrective action as required. Our project 
management approach integrates the scope of work and the 
respective schedules and costs. This allows for the continuous 
measurement of performance throughout the life cycle of the 
project.
Below is a project that illustrates Stanley Consultants’ method to 
maximize and maintain quality management and project image 
while maintaining tight budget constraints.
Water Supply Transmission Mains & Facilities Design, 
Morton Grove Niles Water Commission (MGNWC)
Project Management - For the Water Supply Transmission 
Mains & Facilities Design project, our team found a majority of 
cost savings in engineering came from open communication 
and knowledge sharing at the project kick-off. We held kick-
off meetings with all stakeholders - management, engineers 
and operators. Our strategy was to understand the existing 
operations, issues and needs from all involved parties and to 
come to a consensus on those items and the final scope of work. 
The kick-off meeting provided an opportunity to start the project 
down the correct path so we could provide the best solution for 
the MGNWC.
Additionally, we requested having all stakeholders at the first 
review meeting so their concerns could be addressed and the 
staff brought on board with the proposed layout and operation 
of the proposed design or with the assessments and proposed 
improvements within a report.
Resource Planning - The project consisted of two pump stations, 
a stand pipe and 12 miles of transmission main and the planning 
and design phases needed to be completed in under 11 months. 
We broke the project into manageable work tasks and utilized 
subconsultants based on their expertise to help us efficiently and 
effectively accomplish this project.
Budget - Stanley Consultants managed the engineering budget 
with six subconsultants. The only engineering change orders 
were for the addition of new scope. To maximize MCNWC’s 
construction budget, Stanley Consultants broke the project into 
ten contacts which created competitive bids.
Project Image - Stanley Consultants actively managed the 
construction of this project. A public relations firm was hired to 
help with outreach material and public meetings. The resident 
engineer and inspectors actively reached out to those impacted 
by the project and kept them informed of the project’s status.
VALUE ENGINEERING (VE)
Our experience has been that the greatest benefit and potential 
for savings is normally achieved when the VE is conducted during 
preliminary engineering, when it is possible to make changes 
without major plan revisions. At this stage, alternative design 
strategies can be employed to preserve function, optimize design, 
reduce costs, and enhance constructability. However, VE can 
be performed at any stage during a project, and construction 
phase reviews can also be conducted to verify the project is being 
completed as cost-effectively as practical to minimize life-cycle, 
energy, and operational costs.
Water Supply Transmission Mains & Facilities Design, 
Morton Grove Niles Water Commission
During the planning phase, Stanley Consultants evaluated 
materials (PVC vs. ductile iron) and methods of construction 
(direct bury vs. directional drilling) of the transmission main to 
determine the most cost effective yet durable design. Options 
were presented to MGNWC with the pros and cons of each 
alternative. Within the booster stations, variable speed drives 
were utilized to lower electrical costs long term. Additionally, 
during the planning phase, it was determine that dividing up 
the contracts would result in a lower construction cost, limiting 
subcontractor mark-up and creating competition within specialized 
areas of construction.
