Objective 
Based on the information available and for purposes of this SOW, Customer’s objectives are listed under “Products” and 
“Scope.” 
The software shall be used to support the organization’s mission by providing efficient and effective methods and technologies 
for managing maintenance work activities, protecting, and maintaining physical assets such as buildings and equipment, and 
providing an information resource, especially in the areas of work management and building maintenance. 
 
Services Approach 
Software is a tool, nothing more.  To gain the greatest benefit from an IWMS solution, the system must be configured based on sound 
business practices.  An organization's biggest challenges in adopting new software are old, antiquated processes and pushback from 
stakeholders who are resistant to change.  
At FacilityForce, we propose executing an implementation plan to facilitate a smooth implementation experience and provide ample 
time for stakeholders to learn the system and train their staff.  
FacilityForce will establish a pre-configured environment to facilitate your implementation.  OPERATE Work Quick Start was 
developed with industry best practices in mind, and standard OPERATE and PERFORM functionality; lean workflows, sample OPERATE 
WorkDesks, sample groups, sample WorkDesk templates, sample standard OPERATE queries, and more. 
The Customer's core team will be provided with a small, simple set of templates for loading institutional records such as organizations, 
shops, employees, properties, etc.  The result is that from Day 1, the Customer will have a working test environment that will provide 
stakeholders with hands-on experience and context as they work through the implementation process, which is detailed in this 
statement of work.

Automation Solutions 
The FacilityForce OPERATE application was designed to be able to be integrated with 3rd party systems.  Virtually all major 
functions in the OPERATE application support inbound and outbound interfaces via Web Services that support find/select, 
insert, and update methods.  The web services are available out of the box, not all are enabled by default, but all can be 
activated with minor metadata edits.  
Budgetary estimates have been included in the quote and are listed in the Project Plan under “Project Services and Timeline.” 
In addition to the design specifications, FacilityForce is providing preliminary estimates for development services. Budgetary 
estimates are only intended to provide the Customer with an estimate of the time and resources required to complete a project. 
Once FacilityForce finalizes design specifications, a formal quote will be issued; actual costs may be higher or lower than the 
budgetary estimates.  
AUTOMATE Data Connectors (DCs)  
Data Connectors (DCs) are a class of Business Automation solutions individually licensed as FacilityForce products. DCs are 
developed to support batch data synchronization or transactional data transfers between OPERATE and multiple types of 
external systems that may be a part of an organization’s technical footprint. DCs are designed for routine batch integrations 
and are not intended as a way to support data migrations associated with system implementation. 
Inbound DCs support automation of file-based importing of external data and require a JSON or CSV file formats to insert or 
update the OPERATE database table records. DCs use a configuration parameter-based workflow in OPERATE to schedule batch 
runs which provide validation, logging, notifications, and searchable run history for an administrator. Inbound DCs are capable 
of handling more than one file during a batch execution cycle but are not intended to support more than 25,000 records per 
file. 
Outbound DCs support automated export file generation for staged data from OPERATE screens or process tables. For example, 
invoice data contained in multiple OPERATE tables would be staged in a single table for export providing a single repository to 
define the data elements to be included in the formatted file export. Staged data can be filtered, grouped, and aggregated 
based on user-defined parameters to create simple export file formats like CSV and fixed length. Outbound DCs can be 
scheduled in a variety of configurations and can support notifications, logging, and searchable run history. 
Customer data transformation will be required for every Inbound DC. As a result, there will always be some custom 
development effort needed to achieve integration with external systems. Customers can elect to handle data transformation 
or request a quote from FacilityForce for this custom effort, but some custom data transformation will always be necessary.

AUTOMATE: ENGINE  
The FacilityForce OPERATE application was designed to be able to be integrated with 3rd party systems.  Virtually all major 
functions in the OPERATE application support inbound and outbound interfaces via Web Services that support find/select, 
insert, and update methods.  The web services are available out of the box, not all are enabled by default, but all can be 
activated with minor meta-data edits.  FacilityForce offers several standard interfaces, custom interface development, and can 
train the Customer’s development team how to utilize the OPERATE Web Services for interface development.  The AUTOMATE: 
Engine is a licensed OPERATE product separately priced with the purchase of a Data Connector (DC) or included with the 
purchase of a DC bundle. FacilityForce offers maintenance on Standard interfaces and custom interfaces.  FacilityForce does 
not provide maintenance on custom interfaces created by parties other than FacilityForce.

Project Services and Timeline 
FacilityForce will deliver services as identified in the following project timeline, where Billable Hours are indicated. Note the 
gray-shaded tasks with 0 Billable Hours. These are Customer tasks, responsibilities the Customer has with respect to this 
project, separate from FacilityForce services, and critical to the project schedule. Refer to Appendix B for details. 
Customer and FacilityForce will determine the actual Project start date after the agreement has been executed.  Once the 
project is initiated, Facility Force will work with the Client and the ERP System Implementer (SI) to align this project plan 
with the SI’s ERP Project Plan. Facility Force will ensure configurations that are required for completion of ERP system testing 
are aligned with the ERP project plan to complete the ERP system testing and Facility Force system testing for common 
functionalities successfully.

Guidelines, Clarifications, and Provisions 
Appendix A describes the implementation process and general services FacilityForce provides to the Customer. This 
section identifies specific guidelines, requirements, and conditions included in this Statement of Work based on the 
unique characteristics of the project plan and Customer requirements (Appendix B) and the FacilityForce 
implementation service plan. FacilityForce has estimated this project based on fair and reasonable parameters and 
has not built-in any contingencies for Customer’s expansion in scope; accordingly, FacilityForce will require changes 
in scope when Customer exceeds those parameters. 
Project Implementation Management 
• 
FacilityForce will provide program management services and collective oversight of all phases of this 
project. Project documentation will be maintained by the FacilityForce Program Manager and shared with 
the Customer Executive Sponsor. The FacilityForce Program Manager and the Customer Executive Sponsor 
will meet periodically to discuss progress, issues, and strategies.  
• 
Implementation management services have been estimated based on a percentage of the overall project 
effort and duration described in this Statement of Work. Services will be delivered commensurate with the 
estimate. Requests for excessive project management activities or schedule overruns due to Customer 
delays may constitute a change in scope; in such event, FacilityForce will provide the Customer with a 
change request and proposal for additional services. 
• 
The Facility Force Program Manager will meet with the Customer and ERP SI Project Management team to 
align the project management responsibilities with the overall project, where applicable.  
Business Process Analysis  
For module implementations that include business process analysis, Customer will provide guidance to FacilityForce 
regarding viable processes that will be incorporated into OPERATE. Customer documentation can be a general 
representation of the processes in use, business process flow diagrams, training documentation, etc. 
• 
FacilityForce is not responsible for developing as-is business process documentation as part of this 
Statement of Work.  
• 
FacilityForce may submit a change request for delays caused by incomplete and delayed communication 
from the Customer.  
• 
To-be Business Processes will consist of one (1) set of business processes implemented by all Customer 
organizations that will implement OPERATE. Customer acknowledges that all organizations will agree and 
adhere to standardized business processes.  While the process may involve more or fewer steps for one 
department or another, the process must not deviate or materially change for each department.  Additional 
processes will require a change request. Additional business processes required to support unique use cases may 
require a change order. 
• 
Customer will designate one member of their Core Team to be the ultimate “decision-maker” should there 
be prolonged debate or disagreement during business process analysis sessions.  Unreasonable delays and 
changes caused by Customer indecision will require a change request. 
• 
Facility Force business process analysis effort with align with the on-going ERP implementation where 
applicable, insofar as the Facility Force functionalities are required to interact and collaborate with the ERP.  
Data Migration 
• 
For each Data Migration, FacilityForce will provide the Customer with pre-formatted data load templates 
to facilitate the import of organizational data into the OPERATE Test Environment.  
• 
For each Data Migration summary task in the project timeline, the Customer will identify one representative 
from its Core Team to collect and organize data into the preformatted spreadsheets provided by 
FacilityForce. Customer will submit consolidated and complete data load templates to FacilityForce once 
with records for all participating organizations.

• 
For each Data Migration summary task in the project timeline, FacilityForce will load revised data into the

Training 
FacilityForce offers both remote and on-site services for all professional services engagements we offer, including 
strategic services consulting, implementation services, training, and more. FacilityForce is proposing a complete 
implementation using remote services, the quote does not include an estimate for travel expenses. This is not an 
issue for the execution of implementation services, as FacilityForce can provide complete remote execution. 
Our remote services approach allows FacilityForce to deliver up to 100% of implementation and other professional 
services remotely. Remote services are a cost-effective and effective way to approach your implementation. 
Sessions/web conferencing are conducted via Microsoft Teams, allowing up to 10,000 attendees, or with other video 
conferencing tools of the Customer’s choosing.  Our Professional Services group conducts every session with video 
cameras turned on to maintain face-to-face contact and a personalized experience for your team. End-user training 
sessions are recorded; your trainer will begin each session with class attendees on mute, share the screen, and 
carefully walk through a training exercise from start to finish without interruption. Then, the interactive training 
session will begin. There is time allotted for Q&A, and attendees may share their screens so that a FacilityForce staff 
member can view the end user's desktop and provide guidance. FacilityForce distributes training session videos to 
Customers free of charge. This approach offers additional value for our customers, as they can reuse training videos 
in their entirety or refer to the initial demonstration at the beginning of each video for quick refresher training. 
• 
FacilityForce will provide training services for Customer Core Team members (Train the Trainer). The Core 
Team Members will then be responsible for training other end users of the OPERATE system. Training 
sessions are limited to 10 students to ensure a high-quality learning experience.  FacilityForce assumes the 
Core Team Members will be reasonably skilled in learning and training on similar systems.  Unsophisticated 
Core Team Members may require a change request.   
• 
FacilityForce has several options related to training materials and presentations.  Customer will collaborate 
with FacilityForce to determine the format of training materials and training delivery consistent with the 
hours allotted on the project timeline. A change request may be required if Customer requests multiple 
format options, additional training documentation, or additional training services.  To control changes in 
scope, Customer communicating with FacilityForce and involving stakeholders is critical. 
Barring any government or Customer travel restrictions, FacilityForce also offers on-site services for all professional 
services engagements. Our team will strictly adhere to your organization's policies and guidelines for on-site 
engagements to ensure a safe environment for your team and ours. The Customer can request services be delivered 
on-site; the number of trips and costs will be defined during contract negotiations and mutually agreed upon by 
Customer and FacilityForce. When on-site services are required, travel and living expenses are billed separately at 
the actual cost. Travel time is billed at 50% of the standard rate for the Project Manager.

Throughout the course of the project, a FacilityForce Project Manager will be responsible for managing the project

meetings and will be jointly developed.

Change Management 
A Change Request Order will be used to propose any additional project activity and to record issues and problems 
identified throughout the implementation process.  Copies of the Change Request Order and a Change Request log 
will be furnished to FacilityForce for use in both “hard-copy” and electronic formats. 
Throughout the project, issues will be raised that require follow-up and resolution.  The Change Request Order and 
log are used for this purpose.  Any modification or deviation from the standard functionality or changes to the time 
or cost of the implementation will be subject to the Change Order process using the Change Request.  The project 
team is responsible for considering the impact of any requests on the project's scope. 
Change Order Process 
The Change Order process may be initiated by any project team member whenever there is a perceived need for a 
change that will affect the overall costs, timeline, or functionality of the project or to raise for discussion any problem 
that occurs during the project. 
All Change Requests will be logged and tracked internally via Customer’s Change Management Form and Change 
Request Log. Customer and FacilityForce will review all Change Requests.  The Customer Project Manager and Project 
Core Team will approve, reject, or defer the Change Request. Scope changes resulting in a requirement for additional 
project funding will require formal approval by the Project Steering Committee. 
 
To Propose a Change or Document a Project Issue 
• 
Complete a Change Request (CR) Order and submit copies to the relevant parties for assessment. 
• 
Record the Change Request in a change control log. 
• 
Investigate the impact of the proposed change or issue. 
• 
Evaluate the impact of not performing the change or resolving the issue. 
• 
Prepare a response to the proposed change or issue. 
• 
File the CR original in the project library. 
• 
Agree on whether the change should be performed or the issue resolved and obtain authorization 
sign-off of the Change Request. 
• 
If a "No Action Required" decision is reached:  The proposed change or issue will be re-examined 
if possible or withdrawn if it is agreed to be non-essential.  In this case, the reasons will be 
documented on the Change Request. 
Monitor Changes 
• 
Work may begin after all necessary approvers have signed the Change Request. 
• 
FacilityForce will modify project plans to incorporate agreed-upon changes. 
• 
Progress on each change will be reported at status meetings.  Both FacilityForce and Customer 
Project Managers must sign the Change Request once the change has been completed. 
• 
The Change Request Log will reside on Customer-provided SharePoint (or similar) site. 
• 
When complete, the Change Request Log will be updated with the date completed. 
• 
The Change Request Log will be reviewed at status meetings and included with the monthly project 
status reports to check on changes that still need to be completed.

equal to the general level of competency for their profession in the industry, and the applications they develop will

•

FacilityForce to partner with Customer’s resources to manage the successful implementation of the system.

will provide a business functional project manager and a technical consultant/lead, who will co-manage 
this project along with FacilityForce’s Project Manager. FacilityForce will 
provide system

of work. Customer will apply needed and appropriate internal resources, including a steering committee,

• 
FacilityForce and Customer will jointly be responsible for maintaining the overall project plan.  
• 
FacilityForce will maintain implementation continuity from phase to phase for the complete project. 
The assigned FacilityForce SME/PM will be the primary single point of contact for all issues. Customer 
will likewise assign primary points of contact for all communications during the project. Continuity by

There are four distinct roles the Process Team fulfills during the implementation process:  
Sponsors. Core Team members who have the power to sanction or legitimize change, e.g., a Director of Facilities.  
Sponsors will:  
1. Consider potential changes facing the organization and assess the organizational impact.  
2. Decide which changes will be instituted, communicate priorities, and provide resources to ensure 
success. 
3. Responsible for creating an environment that enables changes to be made on time and within budget.  
Agents. Individuals such as project managers responsible for institutionalizing all proposed changes.  Success 
depends on their ability to:  
1. Diagnose potential problems.  
2. Develop realistic plans to deal with critical issues and execute changes effectively.  
3. Participate as “Change Agents”   
Targets. Individuals or groups who are directly affected by proposed changes, i.e., Process Owners and/or Data 
Owners. The term target is used because these individuals or groups:  
1. Are the focus of the change effort. 
2. Play a crucial role in the short- and long-term success of the project.

RAID 
A RAID log is a project management tool where the FacilityForce Project Manager tracks the risks, actions, issues, 
and decisions of a project.  Facility Force project team will align the RAID Items with the ERP Project and utilize the 
ERP Project RAID log for visibility of the Facility Force project items with the larger ERP project and that the items 
are mitigated in a timely manner.  
Risk is an exposure of the project to an uncertain future event that may impact the project’s completion. A RAID log 
lets the project manager identify probable risks and the events, situations, or activities that either increase or 
decrease the chance of the risk from happening and becoming an issue. Aside from the likelihood of happening, the 
Project Manager can also assess the severity of the risk, and plan for the resources and actions to minimize it. 
Action is needed to complete a task or to respond to an issue. The action needs to have an owner who will complete 
the action by a certain time or date. The project manager can track and note the date when the action has been 
completed and if it needs a follow-up. 
Issue is a problem that occurred and needs to be clearly identified. It may affect the project in various ways, such as 
prolonging the completion, delaying its progress, adding complexity, requiring additional resources, and others. The 
Project Manager needs to track all issues, their severity and impact, and communicate how they are being managed 
and resolved. 
Decisions that were made during the course of the project will be recorded by the FacilityForce Project Manager. It 
is a reference and should include information such as who made the decision, when it was made or implemented, 
and the justification as to why it was made.

Data Conversion/Migration 
It is planned that data mapping, which is required prior to conversion, will be accomplished for the existing databases 
of property, personnel, equipment, accounts, vendors, and all inventory data repositories.  A decision will be made 
to include/exclude each data item.  It may be that dependence on outside databases will be reduced or become 
unnecessary after the conversion, depending on whether the functionality becomes redundant and Customer 
includes users of all existing systems in the OPERATE family of users.  In most cases, the conversion of legacy data is 
accomplished via the following methods: 
• 
Manual Data Entry. Accomplished within the OPERATE application and generally performed by the 
customer.  The quantity of data entered depends on the amount of legacy data Customer wishes to retain 
or may be dependent strictly on need.  In either case, manual data entry is performed when electronic data 
conversion is unnecessary, i.e., there are too few records to justify electronic data conversion, or electronic 
data conversion is cost prohibitive. 
• 
Electronic Data Conversion. Most often performed when the database must be populated with large 
volumes of institutional data.  In a facility management setting, these records typically include property, 
inventory, and organizational information.  FacilityForce uses a well-defined process for performing legacy 
data conversions; Customers are provided with spreadsheets to facilitate the data loads. 
System Configuration and Codes 
This is a two-step process and consists of the following: 
Business Process Analysis (BPA) 
The purpose of this analysis is for FacilityForce to understand your business processes and business rules. The 
analysis asks the Core Team members to analyze and determine the current workflow processes in conjunction with 
the use of OPERATE within the organization. The FacilityForce Implementation Specialist and Core Team members 
review the functionality of the system for use by all named users and user departments. Ultimately the software 
needs to reflect the way assets are managed and how daily business is carried out. An industry best-practice 
approach is planned for this project. 
FacilityForce and the Process Team work together during Business Process Analysis to determine: 
• 
The types of work and how each moves through the organization 
• 
Identification of each group of people within the organization - their roles and responsibilities, and a listing 
of the functions they perform as they process work or perform adjunct duties, and which software screens 
they would utilize to move the workflow process through the system. 
The business process review can best be accomplished by thinking in terms of inputs and outputs.  A unique business 
process has a defined output, such as an immediate repair, a stock room issue, or documented and regularly 
scheduled activity.  The inputs can vary from one person to many coordinating the internal department resources 
and external vendors. 
When performing the business process review, FacilityForce will identify the business rules you use to support the 
process, such as requiring a signature on all items received in the warehouse. Each task decision point and each 
rule come together to form the basis of the statuses coded to the database to drive the process to completion in 
the system. 
As the workflow analysis is being identified by each process, FacilityForce will identify and document the following: 
• 
Who.  The organizational position - roles and responsibilities, types of work, and how each moves through 
the organization. 
• 
What. The actual task that needs to be done. 
• 
When. The inputs/outputs or preceding/succeeding activities that are dependent upon the task. How – 
the transfer mechanism; paper, computer, phone call, etc. Why – the business rule. 
During the first project phase (Property and Space Management), the Implementation Specialist will work with the 
Customer’s process team to develop one consistent business process to be utilized by all participating organizations 
regardless of their legacy Space system.

Workflow Walkthrough 
To confirm the workflow analysis and the mapping of the workflow to the software, the system is to be set up in a 
test environment using codes loaded into the database based on the discussions and decisions made during the 
Business Process Analysis.  These codes will be used to follow the workflow through the software, mirroring your 
work processes.  Several important things happen as a result; the workflow walkthrough will:  
• 
Illustrate Specific OPERATE Functionality.  Up to this point in the implementation process, the Customer’s 
Process Teams will have gained some experience in OPERATE using Quick Start. During the workflow 
walkthrough, the core team will see how the software works from beginning to end, touching all the 
modules in a process with your configured codes. 
• 
Educate Target Groups. Process team members who attend the walkthrough will see a demonstration of 
the flow (interconnectivity) of the software as it mirrors their work processes.  
• 
Elicit Dialog and Minor Process Revisions. There will usually be a discussion about the work processes and 
how the software has been set up to mirror the business practices.  This is to be encouraged, and changes 
should be noted in the documentation.  
Note: This may also be the first time since the start of the project that any cross-functional teams have re-assembled 
to review the software setup.  Therefore, we may have to deal with organizational issues that have not been 
previously identified.  
Software Setup Procedure 
The OPERATE program uses a Relational Database Management System (RDBMS) to manage all information housed 
within the system.  The OPERATE database as a whole is arranged into tables generally corresponding to each 
function of the OPERATE application.  Tables within the OPERATE database house data that is either input into or 
used by the OPERATE application.  For instance, a work order that a user types into the work order entry screen will 
be stored in the table called ae_p_pro_e.  Groups of tables relating to a single function or purpose are often referred 
to as a database themselves even though they are all interrelated and comprise the OPERATE database. Examples of 
such databases include: 
• 
Cost Standards 
• 
Asset Meter Inventory 
• 
Preventive Maintenance Equipment Records 
• 
Technical Specifications 
• 
Vendor Data 
• 
Account Codes 
In many cases, a table of information in OPERATE is used by several OPERATE application screens to ensure that 
entries typed into a field are limited to choices that appear in a pop-up window.  The table the pop-up windows 
access is often referred to by data processing professionals as lookup or domain tables.  The information housed in 
these tables is often referred to as setup codes.  The information gets the name of setup codes because it is usually 
required to be in place within the system before you can process work in a production environment.  Setup codes 
are required and desirable because they allow you to tailor the OPERATE system to conditions unique to your site, 
and they enforce a discipline to ensure consistent data entries that can be analyzed and summarized in accordance 
with your organization’s requirements.  Examples of setup codes used by the OPERATE application include:  
• 
Asset Meter Types 
• 
Vendor Status Codes 
• 
Work Classification 
Standard Operating Procedures Development 
The Standard Operating Procedure (Standard Operating Procedures) is used for identifying the user's roles and 
responsibilities, the work processes that are mapped to the software, the process (and therefore data) ownership, 
the software setup, and finally, a guide that walks the user through each step of software use. This document is not 
intended to fully describe an organization.  The FacilityForce Implementation Specialist will, of course, provide 
guidance and insight for the assigned resources, and FacilityForce provides electronic user documentation that is 
used as the foundation for Standard Operating Procedures documentation.  Once delivered, this document

Training 
User Training 
End-user training will be accomplished with FacilityForce personnel remotely. The training will be conducted in 2 
parts.  The first will be in a virtual classroom environment where procedures will be taught to groups of users who 
share similar responsibilities.  The second part of the training will be user workstation go-live assistance.  This aims 
to reinforce the classroom training that the user has already received.  This training will be conducted as needed 
remotely at the user’s workstation with real-life records and situations. 
Go-Live Assistance  
Provides “one-on-one” assistance for users immediately after OPERATE “Go-Live” to answer immediate questions, 
resolve issues, and provide additional operator-specific assistance/training.  
General Recommendations 
User training represents the final phase of the documentation and training effort.  FacilityForce instructors with 
extensive knowledge of OPERATE will deliver lecture-based classroom training. End-user training will be conducted 
in a Train-the-Trainer format. 
The many business process changes and new data maintenance demands associated with implementing OPERATE 
necessitate the development of lesson plans.  The lesson plans rely heavily on the Standard Operating Procedures 
(SOP) developed during implementation to ensure that users completely understand OPERATE.  A training schedule 
should also be finalized at least two weeks before training commences.  The training program generally includes the 
following: 
• 
Virtual classroom Introduction to OPERATE 
• 
Virtual classroom instruction for each functional area of the application. 
• 
Hands-on end-user training for each user 
• 
Training for selected users on specific subjects, such as Work Control Center, Materials (Inventory) 
Management, Report Writing, System Administration, etc. 
• 
Development of course material and handouts for classroom training 
• 
Development of end-user data entry instructions for specific tasks 
 
Training Program Fundamentals 
The FacilityForce training program consists of the following fundamental elements: 
• 
Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) Development. The Process Teams accomplish initial process and 
workflow findings during the site visits.  After the implementation team returns from the initial site visit, 
the process and workflow findings are reviewed and entered into a draft Standard Operating Procedures 
(Standard Operating Procedures) document.  A draft copy of the Standard Operating Procedures is sent to 
the Customer for review and input.  After the Customer has adequately reviewed the Standard Operating 
Procedures, the Implementation Specialist will return to gather additional information about the 
Customer’s processes and business rules and map them to the Standard Operating Procedures.  The end 
result is a customs document, tailored to the Customer’s specific work processes and business rules. 
• 
The “Conceptual” or Configuration Course. The purpose of the Configuration Course is to provide data 
owners, process owners and system administrators with an understanding of the capabilities of the 
OPERATE system (transactional logic and general concepts), as well as requirements for entering Customer-
specific data codes. The course addresses the major modules of the product, enabling the Customer to gain 
an understanding of the inter-relatedness and flow of data throughout the system.  Configuration training 
concludes with an introduction to the System Administration module.  
• 
The System Administrator’s Course. System Administrators are critical to ensuring the long-term success 
of the OPERATE system.  It is essential that they play an active role in the installation, configuration, and

User Documentation 
OPERATE documentation includes the following:  
• 
Standard Operating Procedures 
The Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) document originates during the Business Process Analysis and is 
customized based on a customer’s business processes, data setup, and system configuration. During the 
Business Process Analysis, the Implementation Specialist consults with Customer’s Implementation Team 
to define business processes and workflows. This information is used to determine the required data setup 
and system configurations. The total package of business processes and workflows, data setup, and system 
configuration is reviewed, and the draft Standard Operating Procedures document is produced and sent to 
the Customer, giving the Customer time to review the materials before the Implementation Specialist 
returns to the site to conduct systems acceptance testing. Any necessary adjustments to the system 
configuration and the Standard Operating Procedures guide can then be made. The result is a customized 
document that is tailored to Customer’s specific work processes and business rules. The Standard Operating 
Procedures document also forms the foundation of Customer-specific end-user training. 
• 
Training Materials 
Training documents will include step-by-step instructions, with graphics, for all primary use-cases identified 
in the Standard Operating Procedures. 
Training sessions are recorded and will be provided to Customer. 
• 
On-Line Help 
OPERATE is delivered with an embedded HELP file that is module-sensitive. It serves as the latest set of 
reference manuals for the user to access additional information.

Appendix B: Planning for Your Success  
In addition to the services FacilityForce provides, your internal team will also have roles and responsibilities during the 
project. The time investment for organizations implementing an IWMS varies based on several factors, e.g., the 
complexity of business requirements, the legacy system(s) where data resides, the accuracy and completeness of your 
institutional data, scope and complexity of third-party system integrations, internal team experience, and more.  
What You Can Expect 
Implementation Planning 
During the initial site visit, FacilityForce will work with pilot organizations to identify resources and plan for the 
implementation.  
Prior to our first site visit, FacilityForce will provide each organization with sample data load spreadsheets. These 
are simplified spreadsheets that will allow your organization to give FacilityForce sample institutional data. 
FacilityForce will load this data into OPERATE to provide your core team with a relevant OPERATE environment you 
can access for testing while we work through implementation activities. Alternatively, during Conceptual Training, 
the FacilityForce Project Manager may manually key in some institutional records to facilitate training. 
Phase Conceptual Training 
Initial training will consist of conceptual and system administration training. The FacilityForce Sr. Implementation 
Specialist will walk your core team through each module and screen in OPERATE Operations & Maintenance. The 
group will interactively discuss functionality and options, and FacilityForce will demonstrate how the software can 
be used.  
At the conclusion of this training, the core team is encouraged to meet as a group to log in to the OPERATE Quick 
Start to “try out” the system, note questions, etc. The more hands-on time your core team has in OPERATE, the 
more successful your implementation will be.   
Business Process Analysis and Standard Operating Procedures Review 
FacilityForce is primarily responsible for facilitating and documenting business processes and Standard Operating 
Procedures. Sessions with FacilityForce and core team members will be intensive; FacilityForce and core team 
members will make joint decisions about business processes, where each process is subject to group review and 
approval. The time required to complete this step will depend on how quickly we can develop and approve workflow 
processes. 
At the conclusion of the business process analysis, FacilityForce will deliver a Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) 
document. The core team will review the documented business processes and provide feedback. 
Data Conversion 
Activities will be divided between the Project Manager and Core Team members at each site. Depending on the 
quality and completeness of information in your legacy work management system, the data conversion can happen 
quickly or require considerable effort. Examples include inventory parts, locations, shop stock, vendor catalogs, 
assets, employees, organizations, and more.  
 
Manual Data Entry 
In some cases, manual data entry may be required. For example, a site might be responsible for the manual setup of 
preventive maintenance schedules, as the optimized maintenance scheduling system in OPERATE may not correlate 
well with the structure of legacy data.

User Security, Queries & OPERATE WorkDesk  
In addition to the typical setup of users and permissions, the system administrator at each organization must define 
roles and permissions.  
The OPERATE test environment will come pre-configured with standard queries and WorkDesks for four sample 
users: Technician, Shop Supervisor (electric shop), Work Control, and PM Coordinator. The system administrator 
at each organization will be tasked with creating standard queries and WorkDesks for all users in your organization. 
The amount of time this takes will vary based on the size of your organization and the number of OPERATE users. 
As a rule of thumb, if you plan to set up 30 users in OPERATE, the process will take about 40 hours.

FacilityForce. FacilityForce will conduct train-the-trainer training sessions with your core team. Your core team is 
generally responsible for training the end-users of the solution. 
Go-Live 
Your team will spend the go-live week working with end-users to ensure everyone follows procedures.

Appendix C: License, Maintenance and Hosting 
Support & Maintenance 
Today's complex information technology ecosystem demands reliable performance from your enterprise 
applications, including the FacilityForce ENGAGE- OPERATE - PERFORM Integrated Workplace Management System 
(IWMS). Your organization expects facilities management to run smoothly, so you can scarcely afford the frustration 
and inconvenience that arises from system downtime. But the fact is, system-related issues do sometimes occur—
and usually when you can least afford them. 
That is why it is crucial to have the backing of a comprehensive support and maintenance plan – one that rapidly 
resolves your technical issues, provides high-quality customer service, gives you access to the newest versions of 
software, and offers a wealth of valuable benefits. 
FacilityForce's Software Support and Maintenance delivers unparalleled value in a comprehensive portfolio of 
support services that enable your organization to get the most from your software investment. 
Benefits of Support & Maintenance 
 
Access to the Latest Software 
Staying current with your support and maintenance contract ensures access to the newest versions of your licensed 
software, including two scheduled major releases per year and patches as required. These releases ensure you 
always get the new features and functionality that are added to your licensed product(s), performance 
enhancements made to the underlying system, and changes made to keep pace with updated operating systems and 
platforms. 
Superior Customer Service and Technical Support 
FacilityForce is dedicated to providing the highest quality Customer Support and maintaining a strong working 
relationship with each of our customers. We know that each customer is unique and have structured our support 
services to meet the most demanding requirements of our customers. Upon award, the FacilityForce Service Level 
commitment will be defined and agreed upon by both parties in the Service Level Agreement; end-user support 
begins once your implementation process is complete.  
Maintaining your support agreement provides direct access to our Customer Care Team based in San Antonio, Texas. 
This includes unlimited telephone hotline or email technical support with a target 2 - 4-hour response time and 24-
hour access to the online Customer Care Portal, where you can submit and track support tickets, access the 
Knowledge Base and system documentation, submit enhancement requests, and more.  
Standard Customer Care hours are 7:00 AM to 7:00 PM CST / Hosted customers also have an emergency number to 
contact for after-hours issues. 
• 
Toll-free line: 1-833-377-7638 
• 
Customer Support Portal: https://www.FacilityForce.com/support/ 
• 
Email: support@FacilityForce.com 
 
Opportunity to Influence Product Direction 
We are proud to say we are very customer-driven with our product development, and supported customers have a 
variety of ways to influence product direction. This includes the ability to submit and track enhancement requests 
through the Customer Care Portal, participate in a Customer Advisory Board (CAB) to design and enhance an existing 
product, and participate in customer surveys. For an additional cost, supported customers can also be part of a 
Special Interest Group (SIG) to build entirely new capabilities, or engage with us at our annual user conference called 
Elevate. 
Leverage and Provide Leading Technologies 
Built on leading technologies, FacilityForce's software provides a fully integrated facilities management system (also 
known as an Integrated Workplace Management System or IWMS) as opposed to separate systems for real estate &

Payment 
License Fees 
Software as a Service (SaaS): FacilityForce offers a subscription licensing model, which utilizes a concurrent session 
pricing model, where Customer pays an annual fee for a minimum contract term of 3 years, where the fee is inclusive 
of the software use subscription, hosting service subscription and maintenance fees. FacilityForce provides all 
infrastructure, and Customer only requires a standard web browser to access the software. Customer's data is not 
co-located with any other customer's data, so each customer has their own dedicated virtual environment. As each 
customer has their own virtual environment, they are in control of their upgrade schedule and can upgrade at their 
convenience. Customer is provided a complete Pre-Production and Production environment by default. Additional 
environments can be provided at additional cost if desired/required. 
Maintenance 
License/On-Premise: Maintenance commences upon software delivery (contract date) and automatically renews 
every three years. Annual payments for Maintenance will be due before the commencement of the initial one-year 
term of the Maintenance and each anniversary thereafter. FacilityForce reserves the right to change the annual 
Maintenance fee (typically 3%) by providing written notice of the increase at least thirty (30) days prior to any 
scheduled renewal date. 
Custom interfaces are not covered under the standard maintenance agreement. Customers may purchase an annual 
maintenance agreement for custom interfaces, and pricing is based on a percentage of the development cost. 
Services 
Payment for services will be made monthly as invoiced by FacilityForce for reimbursement of actual labor and 
expenses.

Appendix D: ENGAGE Requests 
Overview 
FacilityForce shall work with Customer to implement ENGAGE Requests. ENGAGE Requests is a purpose-driven 
application with a straight-forward goal in mind: To tightly integrate Customer’s constituents, the requestor of 
Customer’s services, into Customer’s work management loop. The menu of services offered by today’s Facilities 
organizations greatly exceed the past and a tech-savvy generation of customers are demanding a simpler, faster  way 
to interact with their service providers. ENGAGE Requests will promote, streamline, and formalize web and mobile 
requests from Customer’s stakeholders allowing Customer to maximize the effectiveness of limited resources by not 
only automating service request intake, but routing and prioritizing those requests as well. 
 
Scope 
ENGAGE Requests (up to 4 templates) 
Includes standard ENGAGE Requests implementation services, as shown in the project timeline. Quoted service 
hours will allow FacilityForce and the Customer to develop approximately 4  ENGAGE Requests templates. If 
additional templates are required, the Customer may configure and deploy new templates independently, or engage 
with FacilityForce for additional services. 
 
Authentication Services 
• 
There are several variables and varying degrees of complexity associated with Authentication Services. 
Authentication services pricing includes services for LDAP, CAS, OKTA SAML, or Shibboleth only; a 
discovery call may be scheduled to allow FacilityForce to gather more information to clearly define 
requirements for other methods. If necessary, FacilityForce will submit a separate quote for additional 
services. 
• 
Services for Shibboleth authentication include configuration of the Service Provider (SP) only. Should 
the Customer require advice or assistance to configure the Identity Provider (IdP), FacilityForce will 
submit a separate quote for consultancy services. 
 
Project Implementation Management 
• 
An FacilityForce project manager will oversee the implementation of this project, schedule, coordinate 
activities and assist as needed to ensure a timely and successful conversion process. Implementation 
management services have been estimated based a percentage of the overall project effort. Services 
will be delivered commensurate with the estimate. Requests for excessive project management 
activities or schedule overruns due to the Customer delays may constitute a change in scope; in such 
event, FacilityForce will provide the Customer with a change request and proposal for additional 
services. 
Deliverables

gray shaded tasks with 0 Billable Hours. These are Customer tasks, responsibilities the Customer has with respect to 
this project, separate from FacilityForce deliverables, and critical to the project schedule. 
 
Please review Project Services and Timeline. Actual Project start date to be determined by Customer and

Business Process Identification and Review 
The purpose of this session is for FacilityForce to understand the Customer’s business processes and business rules. 
In a typical implementation this review asks the Focus Team members to analyze and determine the primary request 
methods, gathered information, funding requirements, approval processes and think about how the introduction of 
ENGAGE Requests will enhance them.  
Interviews will be scheduled by the Customer and conducted with personnel identified as subject matter experts for 
their offered services. Existing processes will be reviewed and opportunities for improvement in both efficiency and 
accuracy will be extracted and documented. It is expected that the Customer will reserve appropriate space in which 
these interviews may be conducted.  
Request Prototype Development  
For each identified Request Form that is implemented by FacilityForce, A request prototype will be developed.   This 
is created in collaboration with the Customer’s Focus Team.  A document defining each service type offered through 
ENGAGE, will be developed and outlined to capture the template requirements.  This document can be used as a 
guide for exactly how the particular request template is operating and implemented within ENGAGE Requests.  
In addition to the working prototype of the Request Template Form and Workflow, a base template will be created.  
This can be used to copy directly or used as an example for the Customer to rebuild in their Production instance of 
ENGAGE.    
In accordance with the FacilityForce deliverable process outlined above, the technical prototypes will be delivered 
to the Customer in the Customer’s ENGAGE test environment. A written acceptance of these deliverables is expected 
within the defined acceptance period. Work will not begin on an ENGAGE Requests template until the working 
prototype for each type of offered service has been created, documented and accepted. Minor adjustments made 
to the request template during testing and training are expected (less than 30 minutes of total effort), but wholesale 
changes and major alterations to the scope or details of the request template will result in the documented change 
order process being initiated to expand the existing agreement.   
For clarity, it is important to define a Standard-Sized Request Template. FacilityForce defines a standard-sized 
request template as one that contains no more than twenty-five (25) unique questions (widgets) and takes no more 
than four (4) hours of work to design within ENGAGE Requests. This time includes constructing the form, building 
logic flow patterns, ensuring proper security, and designing the workflow process.  
If FacilityForce determines that a request template may be headed toward exceeding the standard-sized definition, 
the Customer Focus Team will immediately be alerted before the deliverable is completed. If the Customer wishes

to continue to move forward, FacilityForce will create an estimate of time required to complete the task and initiate

Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) Documentation  
A Standard Operating Procedure (Standard Operating Procedures) document is a contract deliverable and will be 
provided to support and augment the information captured during the Template Requirements process. In addition 
to identifying processes and their fit within ENGAGE Requests, the Standard Operating Procedures is used to identify 
user roles and responsibilities, the work processes that are mapped to the software, the process (and therefore data) 
ownership, the software setup, and finally it will act as a user guide to document each step of software use.  
The goal of any Standard Operating Procedures document is to provide a complete picture of the relationship 
between the software and the organization, but this document is not intended to fully describe the Customer’s 
organization, nor is it a training document designed to step a user through each step of the Customer’s request 
templates. There may be additional external processes that are leveraged by the organization and, more specifically, 
users of ENGAGE Requests, in order to complete a request process. It is not the goal of this document to describe all 
of those processes and/or map those subroutines. 
It is understood that once Customer accepts the Standard Operating Procedures, any changes to the Standard 
Operating Procedures due to changes to organization, processes, or procedures will be the sole responsibility of 
Customer. 
Verification of Request Templates (Workflow Walkthrough) 
Once FacilityForce has completed the creation of templates within ENGAGE Requests, the templates will be tested 
and validated by the Customer Focus Team. This activity is one of the most critical for ensuring a successful 
implementation and it is imperative that all key stakeholders actively participate. Any required modifications to the 
system setup will be documented in the Standard Operating Procedures. At this time FacilityForce will provide a draft 
of the Standard Operating Procedures. Any required modifications to the system setup will also be documented. 
The purpose of this exercise:  
• 
Show the Focus Team members the specific functionality of the software with respect to their business 
practices and rules. Other than conceptual training, up to this point in the engagement the Customer will 
not have seen the software functioning in its entirety. This is usually the first time that the Focus Team will 
see the software function from beginning to end, “touching” all of the features in a work process using 
“real” data.  
• 
Educate the group on the use of the software by showing their work processes. At this point, we will 
demonstrate the flow (interconnectivity) of the software as it mirrors their work processes.   
The goals of this exercise:  
• 
The Focus Team understands the end state software setup. 
• 
The request template documentation is validated. 
• 
The Standard Operating Procedures and system setup is modified with recommended changes/alterations. 
• 
Move forward on enterprise-wide deployment of the software. 
• 
Communicate the progress and decisions. 
Training 
FacilityForce will utilize a “train-the-trainer” approach where key Customer resources will be trained to deliver end-
user training. To ensure an effective train-the-trainer program class sizes are capped at 10 students.  Unless otherwise 
state this SOW includes remote  training, delivered in 3 sessions. The dates for training delivery will be finalized at 
least two weeks prior the actual delivery of training.  Training consists of:

Change Management 
A Change Request Order will be used to propose any additional project activity, and to record issues and problems 
identified throughout the implementation process.  Copies of the Change Request Order and a Change Request log 
will be furnished to FacilityForce for use both in “hard-copy” and electronic format. 
Throughout the project, issues will be raised that require follow-up and resolution.  The Change Request Order and 
log are used for this purpose.  Any modification or deviation from the standard functionality or changes to the time 
or cost of the implementation will be subject to the Change Order process, using the Change Request.  The project 
team is responsible for considering the impact of any requests on the scope of the project. 
 
Change Order Process 
The Change Order process may be initiated by any project team member whenever there is a perceived need for a 
change that will affect the overall costs, timeline, or functionality of the project, or in order to raise for discussion 
any problem that occurs during the course of the project. 
All Change Requests will be logged and tracked internally via Customer’s Change Management Form and Change 
Request Log. All Change Requests will be reviewed by Customer and FacilityForce.  The Customer Project Manager 
and project Core Team will either: approve, reject, or defer the Change Request. Scope changes resulting in a 
requirement for additional project funding will require formal approval by the Project Steering Committee. 
 
To Propose a Change or Document a Project Issue 
• 
Complete a Change Request (CR) Order and submit copies to the relevant parties for assessment 
• 
Record the Change Request in a change control log. 
• 
Investigate the impact of the proposed change or issue. 
• 
Evaluate the impact of not performing the change or resolving the issue. 
• 
Prepare a response to the proposed change or issue. 
• 
File the CR original in the project library. 
• 
Agree whether the change should be performed, or the issue resolved and obtain authorization sign-off of 
the Change Request. 
• 
If a "No Action Required" decision is reached:  The proposed change or issue will be re-examined if possible 
or withdrawn if it is agreed to be non-essential.  In this case, the reasons will be documented on the Change 
Request. 
  
Monitor Changes 
• 
Work may begin after all necessary approvers have signed the Change Request. 
• 
FacilityForce will modify project plans to incorporate agreed-upon changes. 
• 
Progress on each change will be reported at status meetings.  Both FacilityForce and Customer Project 
Managers must sign the Change Request once the change has been completed. 
• 
The Change Request Log will reside on Customer-provided SharePoint (or similar) site. 
• 
When complete, the Change Request Log will be updated with date completed. 
• 
The Change Request Log will be reviewed at status meetings and included with the monthly project status 
reports to check on changes that have not been completed. 
 
Project Responsibilities and Standards 
The implementation process is interactive and intensive, and success requires close teamwork between the Customer 
and FacilityForce. FacilityForce will assist in the development of the implementation plan and will play a key role in 
determining informational needs. Full support by the Customer and FacilityForce is essential to the engagement’s 
success. This Scope of Work is based on the assumptions listed below. Any changes to the assumptions listed here or 
elsewhere or in the overall engagement scope of work will be handled through the Change Order process.

VIA EMAIL 
 
 
Yvette Albarran, Lake County, IL

Re: Strada Transition 
 
Dear Ms. Albarran, 
 
In a strategic transaction that recently closed (the “Transaction”), Alight Solutions LLC(“Alight”) sold its Payroll 
and Professional Services business, which includes Enterprise Resource Planning and Workday Implementation 
Services, to an affiliate of H.I.G. Capital. The Payroll and Professional Services Business will now operate as a new 
company called Strada U.S. Professional Services, LLC (“Strada”). As part of the Purchase Agreement, all Alight 
Payroll and Professional Service contracts, proposals, staff, intellectual property, and other resources conveyed 
as part of the Transaction. Alight core clients continue to purchase employee benefits administration, specifically 
health insurance and retirement administration.   
 
As the above scenario would be considered a divesture pursuant to RFP General Terms and Conditions Section 
1.7.30, Strada wishes to assure Lake County that we are committed to and capable of providing the Services that 
were detailed in our RFP response and the orals presentation that we delivered. The Transaction has not had 
any impact on our response, effectively, beyond the name of the company that will be providing the Services to 
the County.  Strada has retained over 8,000 employees with over 100 residing in Lake County.  The existing 
service delivery team providing professional services, including Workday deployment services, will continue to 
service clients going forward. Ultimately, our proposed services, staff, and methods remain unchanged, and you 
will continue to receive the same value you have expected all along. We are excited about the next stage of 
growth of Strada’s payroll and professional services business being accelerated by the investment and resources 
of H.I.G. Capital. 
 
Since its founding in 1993, H.I.G. Capital has invested in and managed more than 400 companies worldwide 
including a significant number of human capital management and tech-enabled services businesses. H.I.G. 
Capital also has a strong track record of corporate divestiture and carve-out transactions and has completed 
over 20 such transactions in the last several years. The firm's current portfolio includes more than 100 
companies with combined sales more than $53 billion. 
   
We look forward to continuing productive discussions around contracting and the Statement of Work.  Please 
don’t hesitate to contact me should you have any questions about this recent transition.   
Sincerely,

8. Contractor Work Hours and Safety Standards Act. No contractor or subcontractor contracting for any part of 
the contract work which may require or involve the employment of laborers or mechanics shall require or 
permit any such laborer or mechanic in any workweek in which he or she is employed on such work to work 
in excess of forty hours in such workweek unless such laborer or mechanic receives compensation at a rate 
not less than one-half times the basic rate of pay for all hours worked in excess of forty hours in such 
workweek. 
 
In the event of any violation of the above clause the contractor or any subcontractor responsible therefor 
shall be liable for the unpaid wages.  In addition, such contractor and subcontractor shall be liable to the 
United States for liquidated damages. Such liquidated damages shall be computed with respect to each 
individual laborer or mechanic, including watchmen and guards, employed in violation of the above clause, in 
the sum of $27 for each calendar day on which such individual was required or permitted to work in excess of 
the standard workweek of forty hours without payment of the overtime wages required by the clause above. 
Lake County shall upon its own action or upon written request of an authorized representative of the 
Department of Labor withhold or cause to be withheld, from any moneys payable on account of work 
performed by the contractor or subcontractor under any such contract or any other Federal contract with the 
same prime contractor, or any other federally-assisted contract subject to the Contract Work Hours and Safety 
Standards Act, which is held by the same prime contractor, such sums as may be determined to be necessary 
to satisfy any liabilities of such contractor or subcontractor for unpaid wages and liquidated damages.

clause requiring the subcontractors to include these clauses in any lower tier subcontracts.  The prime

11. Minority and Women Business Enterprises.   
Contractor hereby agrees to comply with the following when applicable: The requirements of Executive 
Orders 11625 and 12432 (concerning Minority Business Enterprise), and 12138 (concerning Women's 
Business Enterprise), when applicable. Accordingly, the contractor hereby agrees to take affirmative steps to 
assure that women and minority businesses are utilized when possible as sources [12] of supplies, 
equipment, construction and services. Affirmative steps shall include the following:  
1. Including qualified women’s business enterprises and small and minority businesses on solicitation 
lists.  
2.  Assuring that women’s enterprises and small and minority businesses are solicited whenever they 
are potential sources.  
3.  When economically feasible, dividing total requirements into smaller tasks or quantities so as to 
permit maximum participation by small and minority business, and women’s business enterprises.  
4. Where the requirement permits, establishing delivery schedules which will encourage participation 
by women’s business enterprises and small and minority business; and 
5. Using the services and assistance of the Small Business Administration, and the U.S. Office of 
Minority Business Development Agency of the Department of Commerce; and the North Carolina 
Office for Historically Underutilized Businesses.  
 
For the purposes of these requirements, a Minority Business Enterprise (MBE) is defined as an enterprise 
that is at least 51 percent owned and controlled in its daily operation by members of the following groups: 
Black, Hispanic, Asian or Pacific Islander, American Indian, or Alaskan Natives. A Women Business Enterprise 
(WBE) is defined as an enterprise that is at least 51 percent owned and controlled in its daily operation by 
women. Additionally, an MBE or WBE qualifies if it is currently certified as a North Carolina “historically 
underutilized business” under N.C.G.S. §143-128.4(a) and qualifies as a “small business” if it is 
independently owned and operated and is qualified under the Small Business Administration criteria and 
size standards at 13 C.F.R. Part 21. 
12. Assurances of Compliance with Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 
Contractor and any subcontractor, or the successor, transferee, or assignee of contractor or any 
subcontractor, shall comply with Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which prohibits recipients of federal 
financial assistance from excluding from a program or activity, denying benefits of, or otherwise 
discriminating against a person on the basis of race, color, or national origin (42 U.S.C. §§ 2000d et seq.), as 
implemented by the Department of the Treasury’s Title VI regulations, 31 C.F.R. Part 22, which are herein 
incorporated by reference and made a part of this contract. Title VI also provides protection to persons with 
“Limited English Proficiency” in any program or activity receiving federal financial assistance, 42 U.S.C. §§ 
2000d et seq., as implemented by Treasury’s Title VI regulations, 31 C.F.R. Part 22, and herein incorporated 
by reference and made a part of this contract.14

DATA PROTECTION AGREEMENT 
This Data Protection Agreement (this “DPA”), effective as of September 20, 2024 (the “Effective Date”), is by and between 
County of Lake (“Client”), and Strada U.S. Professional Services, LLC (“Strada” and, together with Client, each a “Party” and 
collectively the “Parties”).  This DPA is entered into in connection with services to be provided by Strada to Client (“Services”) 
under that certain Framework Services Agreement, effective as of September 20, 2024, by and between the Parties (the 
“Services Agreement”), pursuant to which Strada may be required to process Personal Data (as defined below).  In 
consideration of the mutual covenants contained herein, and other valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of 
which are hereby acknowledged, the Parties agree as follows: 
Section 1. 
Definitions.  Capitalized terms used but not otherwise defined herein shall have the meanings ascribed thereto 
in the Services Agreement.  Unless the express context otherwise requires, any reference to the Services Agreement includes 
any Statement of Work, statement of work or other ordering document entered into thereunder. 
1.1. 
“Data Protection Laws” means all applicable laws and regulations regarding privacy, security or data 
protection, including, as applicable, the California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (“CCPA”), the California Privacy Rights Act of 
2020 (“CPRA”), the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (“HIPAA”),  the EU General Data Protection 
Regulation 2016/679 (“GDPR”), the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (“GLBA”), the UK Data Protection Act of 2018 or the New York 
Department of Financial Services Regulations, as any are amended, repealed or replaced. 
1.2. 
“Data Subject” means, with respect to any Personal Data, the subject of such Personal Data. 
1.3. 
“Personal Data” means any information processed by or on behalf of Strada for Client in connection with 
the Services Agreement that (a) relates to an identified or identifiable natural person, where an identifiable natural person is 
one who can be identified, directly or indirectly, from such information alone or in combination with other information 
processed by or on behalf of Strada,  in particular by reference to an identifier such as a name, an identification number, 
location data, an online identifier or to one or more factors specific to the physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, 
cultural or social identity of that natural person or (b) is otherwise protected under the Data Protection Laws. 
1.4. 
“Processing” or “Process(es)” means (a) any operation or set of operations that are performed on Personal 
Data or on sets of Personal Data, whether or not by automated means, including collection, storage, adaptation or alteration, 
retrieval, use, disclosure, erasure or destruction, and (b) any other activity involving Personal Data included in the definition 
of “processing” under Data Protections Laws. 
1.5. 
“Standard Contractual Clauses” or “SCCs” means the standard contractual clauses for the transfer of 
personal data from the European Union (“EU”) or the European Economic Area (“EEA”) to third countries annexed to the 
Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2021/914 of 4 June 2021, or any successor documents or transfer mechanisms. 
1.6. 
“Subprocessor” means any person (other than an employee), including Strada’s affiliates, appointed by or 
on behalf of Strada to Process Personal Data on behalf of Client. 
1.7. 
“UK IDTA” means the International Data Transfer Addendum to the SCCs, version B1.0, approved by the 
United Kingdom (“UK”) parliament on 21 March 2022, issued under Section 119A of the Data Protection Act 2018 to comply 
with Article 46 of the UK GDPR when making restricted transfers, or any successor documents or transfer mechanisms. 
Section 2. 
Processing Personal Data. 
2.1. 
Instructions; Limits on Use.  Client hereby appoints and instructs Strada to Process Personal Data for the 
purpose of performing the Services and otherwise complying with any of its obligations or asserting any of its rights under 
the Services Agreement and this DPA, complying with applicable law and complying with any other instruction provided by 
or on behalf of Client (the “Purpose”).  Strada shall only retain, use, disclose or otherwise Process Personal Data for the 
Purpose.  Client and Strada will at all times work together to ensure that they are complying with Data Protection Laws.  
Strada shall immediately notify Client if, in its opinion, an instruction provided by or on behalf of Client is in conflict with any 
Data Protection Law; provided that Strada shall have no responsibility to seek out or discover such conflicts or to otherwise 
ensure that such conflicts do not exist.  In the event Strada notifies Client of any such conflict, Strada may suspend the 
Docusign Envelope ID: BC83E8A5-C7AA-423F-B578-30A4CF289B4B

--- PAGE BREAK ---

 
17 
execution of the applicable instruction to the extent necessary to avoid such conflict while the Parties cooperate in good faith 
to resolve such matter in a timely manner. 
2.2. 
Limits on Disclosure.  Strada shall not disclose any Personal Data to any third party except as necessary to 
fulfill the Purpose and otherwise in accordance with this DPA.  If Strada or any of its representatives is requested or required 
to disclose or otherwise Process any Personal Data by law or legal process, then Strada shall (if permitted by law) promptly 
notify Client and reasonably cooperate (at Client’s expense) in Client’s efforts to obtain an appropriate protective order or 
other remedy.  Client shall only disclose Personal Data to Strada to the extent requested by Strada or as otherwise necessary 
for the Purpose. 
2.3. 
Compliance with Data Protection Laws.  Each Party shall comply with its obligations under Data Protection 
Laws.  Strada shall provide reasonable assistance to Client with meeting its obligations under Data Protection Laws in relation 
to the Processing of Personal Data, taking into account the nature of Strada’s Processing and the information available to 
Strada. 
2.4. 
Supervisory Authority Requests.  If Client receives a request for information from a competent supervisory 
authority in relation to Processing of Personal Data by Strada (including details regarding the Purpose), Strada shall provide 
reasonable assistance to Client in responding to such request to the extent Client does not otherwise have access to such 
information, and taking into account the nature of the Processing and information available to Strada. 
 
2.5. 
Data Protection Impact Assessment and Prior Consultation.  Strada shall provide reasonable assistance to 
Client with any data protection impact assessments, and prior consultations with supervising authorities or other competent 
data privacy authorities, which Client reasonably considers to be required by the Data Protection Laws, in each case solely in 
relation to Processing of Personal Data by, and taking into account the nature of the Processing and information available to, 
Strada. 
2.6. 
Data Subject Rights.  Strada shall promptly notify Client if it receives any request from a Data Subject 
asserting rights under Data Protection Laws with respect to their Personal Data.  Strada will not respond to any such request 
except on the written instructions (including email) of Client or as required by Data Protection Laws, in which case Strada 
shall, to the extent permitted by such Data Protection Laws, inform Client of such requirement prior to such response.  Strada 
will provide Client with reasonable assistance in its efforts to fulfill its obligations to respond to such requests, including by 
providing access to or information about, deleting or modifying the relevant Personal Data, in each case, to the extent 
required under and in accordance with Data Protection Laws.  If Strada is unable to provide any such assistance for reasons 
permitted under Data Protection Laws, Strada shall promptly notify Client of such fact and shall provide such assistance 
promptly after the reasons for not doing so have expired. 
2.7. 
Return and Destruction.  Upon written request of Client following termination or expiration of the Services 
Agreement, Strada shall, and shall require its Subprocessors to, (a) return a complete copy of all Personal Data to Client by 
secure file transfer in Strada’s customary format and (b) delete or render permanently anonymous all other copies of Personal 
Data.  Strada shall comply with any such written request within 20 business days.  Strada and its Subprocessors may retain 
Personal Data as necessary to fulfill the Purpose and comply with applicable law, in which case the terms of this DPA shall 
continue to apply to such Personal Data for so long as it is retained. 
2.8. 
Recordkeeping.  Strada shall keep accurate and up-to-date records regarding any Processing of Personal 
Data, including (a) records regarding access to and security of the Personal Data, the purposes and categories of Processing 
the Personal Data and its Subprocessors and (b) any other records as required by Data Protection Laws.  This DPA serves as 
record of processing activities as required under art. 30(2) GDPR. 
2.9. 
Employees.  Personal Data shall only be accessed by Strada employees who require such access to assist 
Strada in connection with the Purpose.  Unless otherwise restricted by applicable local laws, Strada requires all new 
employees be subjected to a comprehensive pre-employment background check in accordance with local laws and customs.  
Strada requires that agreements that include non-disclosure / confidentiality provisions be signed by all new employees.  
Strada provides employees with periodic data security and privacy training.
