2. The Contractor agrees to report each violation to the LCHD and understands and agrees that the LCHD 
will,  in  turn,  report  each  violation  as  required  to  assure  notification  to  the  Federal  Emergency 
Management Agency, and the appropriate Environmental Protection Agency Regional Office. 
3. The Contractor agrees to include these requirements in each subcontract exceeding $150,000 financed 
in whole or in part with Federal assistance. 
 
7. Procurement of Recovered Materials. In the performance of this Agreement, Contractor shall make maximum 
use of products containing recovered  materials that are EPA‐designated items unless the product  cannot be 
acquired –  
1. Competitively within a timeframe providing for compliance with the contract performance schedule; 
2. Meeting contract performance requirements; or 
3. At a reasonable price. 
 
Information about this requirement, along with the list of EPA designated items, is available at EPA’s Comprehensive 
Procurement Guidelines web site, https://www.epa.gov/smm/comprehensive‐procurement‐guideline‐cpg‐program.  
 
Contractor also agrees to comply with all other applicable requirements of Section 6002 of the Solid Waste Disposal Act. 
 
8. Contractor Work Hours and Safety Standards Act. No contractor or subcontractor contracting for any part of the 
contract work which may require or involve the employment of laborers or mechanics shall require or permit any 
such laborer or mechanic in any workweek in which he or she is employed on such work to work in excess of forty 
hours in such workweek unless such laborer or mechanic receives compensation at a rate not less than one‐half 
times the basic rate of pay for all hours worked in excess of forty hours in such workweek. 
 
In the event of any violation of the above clause the contractor or any subcontractor responsible therefor shall be liable 
for the unpaid wages.  In addition, such contractor and subcontractor shall be liable to the United States for liquidated 
damages. Such liquidated damages shall be computed with respect to each individual laborer or mechanic, including 
watchmen and guards, employed in violation of the above clause, in the sum of $27 for each calendar day on which such 
individual was required or permitted to work in excess of the standard workweek of forty hours without payment of the 
overtime wages required by the clause above. 
 
Lake County shall upon its own action or upon written request of an authorized representative of the Department of Labor 
withhold  or  cause  to  be  withheld,  from  any  moneys  payable  on  account  of  work  performed  by  the  contractor  or 
subcontractor  under  any  such  contract  or  any  other  Federal  contract  with  the  same  prime  contractor,  or  any  other 
federally‐assisted contract subject to the Contract Work Hours and Safety Standards Act, which is held by the same prime 
contractor, such sums as may be determined to be necessary to satisfy any liabilities of such contractor or subcontractor 
for unpaid wages and liquidated damages. 
 
The contractor or subcontractor shall insert any subcontracts the clauses set forth in this section and also a clause requiring

8 
 
the subcontractors to include these clauses in any lower tier subcontracts.  The prime contractor shall be responsible for 
compliance by any subcontractor or lower tier contractor. 
 
9. Domestic Preference for Procurements 
As appropriate and to the extent consistent with law, Lake County, to the greatest extent practicable under a Federal 
award of American Rescue Plan dollars, provides a preference for the purchase, acquisition, or use of goods, products, or 
materials  produced  in  the  United  States  (including  but  not  limited  to  iron,  aluminum,  steel,  cement,  and  other 
manufactured products) 
a) For purposes of this section:  
(1) “Produced in the United States” means, for iron and steel products, that all manufacturing processes, from the initial 
melting stage through the application of coatings, occurred in the United States.  
(2) “Manufactured products” means items and construction materials composed in whole or in part of non‐ferrous metals 
such as aluminum; plastics and polymer‐based products such as polyvinyl chloride pipe; aggregates such as concrete; glass, 
including optical fiber; and lumber. 
 
10. Prohibition on Certain Telecommunications  
The proposed equipment will not utilize covered telecommunications equipment or services as a substantial or essential 
component of any system, or as critical technology as part of any system. As described in Public Law 115‐232, section 889, 
covered telecommunications equipment is telecommunications equipment produced by Huawei Technologies Company 
or ZTE Corporation (or any subsidiary or affiliate of such entities). 
 
11. Minority and Women Business Enterprises.   
Contractor hereby agrees to comply with the following when applicable: The requirements of Executive Orders 11625 and 
12432 (concerning Minority Business Enterprise), and 12138 (concerning Women's Business Enterprise), when applicable. 
Accordingly, the contractor hereby agrees to take affirmative steps to assure that women and minority businesses are 
utilized when possible as sources [12] of supplies, equipment, construction and services. Affirmative steps shall include 
the following:  
1. Including qualified women’s business enterprises and small and minority businesses on solicitation lists.  
2.  Assuring  that  women’s  enterprises  and  small  and  minority  businesses  are  solicited  whenever  they  are 
potential sources.  
3.  When economically feasible, dividing total requirements into smaller tasks or quantities so as to permit 
maximum participation by small and minority business, and women’s business enterprises.  
4. Where  the  requirement  permits,  establishing  delivery  schedules  which  will  encourage  participation  by 
women’s business enterprises and small and minority business; and 
5. Using the services and assistance of the Small Business Administration, and the U.S. Office of Minority Business 
Development  Agency  of  the  Department  of  Commerce;  and  the  North  Carolina  Office  for  Historically 
Underutilized Businesses.  
 
For the purposes of these requirements, a Minority Business Enterprise (MBE) is defined as an enterprise that is at least 
51 percent owned and controlled in its daily operation by members of the following groups: Black, Hispanic, Asian or 
Pacific Islander, American Indian, or Alaskan Natives. A Women Business Enterprise (WBE) is defined as an enterprise that 
is at least 51 percent owned and controlled in its daily operation by women. Additionally, an MBE or WBE qualifies if it is 
currently certified as a North Carolina “historically underutilized business” under N.C.G.S. §143‐128.4(a) and qualifies as a 
“small business” if it is independently owned and operated and is qualified under the Small Business Administration 
criteria and size standards at 13 C.F.R. Part 21. 
 
12. Assurances of Compliance with Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 
Contractor and any subcontractor, or the successor, transferee, or assignee of contractor or any subcontractor, shall 
comply with Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which prohibits recipients of federal financial assistance from excluding 
from a program or activity, denying benefits of, or otherwise discriminating against a person on the basis of race, color, 
or national origin (42 U.S.C. §§ 2000d et seq.), as implemented by the Department of the Treasury’s Title VI regulations, 
31 C.F.R. Part 22, which are herein incorporated by reference and made a part of this contract. Title VI also provides 
protection to persons with “Limited English Proficiency” in any program or activity receiving federal financial assistance,

42 U.S.C. §§ 2000d et seq., as implemented by Treasury’s Title VI regulations, 31 C.F.R. Part 22, and herein incorporated 
by reference and made a part of this contract.9 
 
13. Publications 
Any publications produced with funds from this award must display the following language: “This project [is being] [was] 
supported, in whole or in part, by federal award number [enter project FAIN] awarded to [name of Recipient] by the U.S.

14. Increasing Seat Belt Use in the United States.  
Pursuant to Executive Order 13043, 62 Fed. Reg.19216 (Apr. 18, 1997), contractor is encouraged to adopt and enforce on‐
the‐job seat belt policies and programs for your employees when operating company‐owned, rented or personally owned 
vehicles.  
 
15. Reducing Text Messaging While Driving.  
Pursuant to Executive Order 13513, 74 Fed. Reg. 51225 (Oct. 6, 2009), contractor is encouraged to adopt and enforce 
policies that ban text messaging while driving and establish workplace safety policies to decrease accidents caused by 
distracted drivers.  
 
16. Conflicts and Interpretation.  
To the extent that any portion of this Addendum conflicts with any term or condition of this contract expressed outside 
of this Addendum, the terms of this Addendum shall govern.

SUSTAINABILITY STATEMENT 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
NOVEMBER 2023 
 
 
 
The County of Lake has a responsibility to balance fiscal, environmental, and social considerations into its operational 
decision‐making  process.    The  County’s  commitment  to  green  and  sustainable  practices  and  good  environmental 
stewardship was memorialized by the Lake County Board in the County’s 2013 Strategic Plan where sustainability is listed 
as a value and a goal.  This was further strengthened in September 2020 by adopting a Net Zero Emissions goal through 
Joint Resolution.  Therefore, we shall promote environmentally preferable purchasing, whenever practical, by procuring 
goods or services that lessen the destructive effects on the environment and the health and well‐being of all citizens.   
Consideration  of  the  practices  adopted  by  our  contracted  firms  is  key  to  magnifying  the  impact  of  the  County’s 
sustainability measures. Proposers are requested to provide a Statement of Sustainability demonstrating the methods 
they have incorporated into their firms.  Sustainability may be one of the scoring criteria included the evaluation rubric 
for the award of this contract.   
INSTRUCTIONS 
Please provide a narrative outlining any policies or practices implemented by your firm to reduce your carbon footprint.  
Your response should include, but need not be limited to: 
 
A copy of your firm’s sustainability policy, awards, and accolades.   
 
Practices such as waste minimization, energy/water efficiency, methods instituted to reduce pollution, green 
products utilized, staff education, community involvement and volunteerism.    
o Specifically  include  the  percentage  of  your  firm’s  energy  that  comes  from  renewable  sources  and 
percentage of your fleet that is non‐emitting. 
 
Sustainable approaches your firm may have for this specific project.   
 
Cost variances to incorporate a more sustainable approach to this project and any calculated life cycle costs.

     Vendor Certification Definitions 
 
Minority‐owned business (MBE) 
A business concern which is at least 51% owned by one or more minority persons, or in the case of a corporation, at least 51% of 
the stock in which is owned by one or more minority persons; and the management and daily business operations of which are 
controlled by one or more of the minority individuals who own it. 
 
Woman‐owned business (WBE) 
A business which is at least 51% owned by one or more women, or, in the case of a corporation, at least 51% of the stock in which 
is owned by one or more women; and the management and daily business operations of which are controlled by one or more of 
the women who own it. 
 
Veteran‐owned Business Enterprise (VBE)  
A small business (i) that is at least 51 percent owned, controlled and managed by one or more Eligible Veterans or in the case of 
a corporation, at least 51 percent or more of the stock of which is owned, controlled and managed by one or more Eligible 
Veterans.  
 
Eligible Veteran means a person who (i) has been either a member of the armed forces of the United States or, while a citizen 
of the United States, was a member of the armed forces of allies of the United States in time of hostilities with a foreign 
country and (ii) has served under one or more of the following conditions: (a) the veteran served a total of at least 6 months; 
(b)  the  veteran  served  for  the  duration  of  hostilities  regardless  of  the  length  of  the  engagement;  (c)  the  veteran  was 
discharged on the basis of hardship; or (d) the veteran was released from active duty because of a service connected disability 
and was discharged under honorable conditions.  
 
Armed Forces of the United States means the United States Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, Coast Guard, or service in 
active duty as defined under 38 U.S.C. Section 101. Service in the Merchant Marine that constitutes active duty under Section 
401 of federal Public Act 95‐202 shall also be considered service in the armed forces for purposes of this Division.  
 
Persons with Disabilities Owned Business Enterprise (PDBE)  
A small business (i) that is at least 51 percent owned. controlled and managed by one or more Persons with a Disability; or in the 
case of a corporation, at least 51 percent or more of the stock of which is owned, controlled, and managed by one or more Persons 
with a Disability.  
 
Disability or Disabled means, with respect to an individual, a physical or mental impairment that substantially limits one or 
more of the major life activities of the individual, a record of physical or mental impairment that substantially limits one or 
more of the major life activities of the individual, or being regarded as an individual with a physical or mental impairment 
that substantially limits one or more of the major life activities of the individual.  
 
Service‐Disabled Veteran‐owned Business Enterprise (SDVBE)  
A small business (i) that is at least 51 percent owned, controlled, and managed by one or more qualified service‐disabled veterans 
or in the case of a corporation, at least 51 percent or more of the stock of which is owned, controlled and managed by one or 
more Service Disabled Veterans.  
 
Service‐Disabled Veteran means an Eligible Veteran who has been found to have 10 percent or more service‐connected 
disability by the United States Department of Veterans Affairs or the United States Department of Defense.  
 
Service‐connected disability means  a  disability  incurred  in  the  line  of duty  in  the active  military, naval  or  air  service  as 
described in 38 U.S.C. 101(16).  
 
BEP – Business Enterprise Program  
Business Enterprise Program (BEP) BEP assists businesses owned by minorities, women, and people with disabilities gain access 
to the State of Illinois procurement process. BEP certification with the State of Illinois can also open the door to opportunities 
with other public and private entities which are looking for diverse suppliers. 
 
Small Disadvantaged Businesses (SDB) 
A Small Disadvantaged Business (SDB) is a small business owned  and controlled by socially and economically disadvantaged 
individuals as defined by Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) 19.001 
 
Veteran‐Owned Small Business (VOSB) 
A Veteran‐Owned Small Business (VOSB) is a small business that is at least 51 percent owned by one or more veterans; or, if a 
publicly owned business, at least 51 percent of the stock is owned by one or more veterans.  Also, one or more veterans control 
management and daily business operations of the firm. 
 
Local business 
Lake County launched a Buy Local. Build Local. Work Local initiative in 2013 to increase the outreach and procurement 
opportunities for businesses located within Lake County, including women‐owned businesses and minority‐owned business 
enterprises (L/W/MBE). The overarching objective is to maximize participation from these businesses in the County’s procurement 
process, in accordance with applicable law.

Section 1 – Introduction
This Statement of Work (“SOW”) effective as of the date of last signature below sets forth the 
framework to implement three one-way outbound interfaces which include: Warrant Interface 
(Outbound), Quash Warrant Interface (Outbound), and Notice of Court Date 
Interface(Outbound) for Lake County, IL Circuit Court (herein referred to as “Client”), by 
Journal Technologies, Inc. (hereinafter referred to as “JTI”) the SOW # 24-2494 dated August 
15, 2024 (“SOW”) between Lake County, IL Circuit Court (“Client”) and Journal Technologies, 
Inc. (“JTI”).
This SOW describes the project's scope, its phases, assignment of responsibilities, and required 
deliverables by the Client and JTI.
Section 2 – Project Scope and Definition 
2.1 Project Scope
The project scope includes requirements, development, testing and delivery of Warrant Interface 
(Outbound), Quash Warrant Interface (Outbound), and Notice of Court Date Interface 
(Outbound) for the Client.  These three interfaces will be established for the new Lake County 
Sheriff’s Office RMS system.  
Warrant Interface
•
One-way outbound 
•
eCourt will make Warrant information for criminal and civil warrants available to 
the Sheriff’s Office RMS upon the signature of any warrant by a judge in Lake 
County, IL 
•
There will be one warrant sent for each charge on the case and there can be one or 
more warrants per case.
•
Each warrant will be made available to the Sheriff’s Office RMS through XML in a file 
share location to be determined.  
•
Credentials will be required for the web service access (e.g. base URL, username, 
password, etc.), which will be provided by JTI.  The file will contain data for one 
or more warrant records. There will always be one warrant per case offense 
•
The XML will contain a link to the Warrant document image.  The interface will 
download the PDF and attach the image to the Warrant record in the Sheriff’s RMS 
system.  
•
The Sheriff’s Office RMS will determine a configurable scheduled interval to retrieve only 
new Warrants and Warrant images from JTI.  
•
There will be no updates to existing Warrants through this interface.  
•
If an error occurs within the Warrant interface preventing the adding of a warrant, the 
Sheriff’s Office RMS will handle all error messaging.  

--- PAGE BREAK ---

3
Quash Warrant Interface
•
One-way outbound 
•
eCourt will allow quash warrants, both criminal and civil, to be updated with the 
Sheriff’s Office RMS upon the update status of any quash warrant by a judge in 
Lake County, IL. 
•
There will be one warrant quash update sent for each charge on the case and there 
can be one or more warrant quashes per case.
•
Each warrant quash will be made available to the Sheriff’s Office RMS through XML in a 
file share location to be determined.  
•
Credentials will be required for the web service access (e.g. base URL, username, 
password, etc.), which will be provided by JTI.  The file will contain data for one 
or more warrant records. There will always be one warrant per case charge or 
offense.
•
The Sheriff’s Office RMS will determine a configurable scheduled interval to retrieve 
warrant quash information from JTI.  
•
Warrant quash will be the only warrant status the warrant quash will look for.  
•
If an error occurs within the quash warrant interface preventing the update status of 
warrant quash, the Sheriff’s Office RMS will handle all error messaging.  
Notice of Court Date
•
One-way outbound 
•
eCourt will provide notice of court date to the Sheriff’s Office RMS for individuals 
detained by the County Sheriff’s Office.  
•
There will be one court date per event. eCourt will provide the next scheduled 
event, upon the scheduling of that event. All activity related to the event will be 
provided to the Sheriff’s Office RMS, including canceled events and continued 
events.  
•
Each notice of court date will be made available to the Sheriff’s Office RMS through XML 
in a file share location to be determined.  
•
The XML will contain the date, time, and location, including courtroom of the next 
court date. 
•
Credentials will be required for the web service access (e.g. base URL, username, 
password, etc.), which will be provided by JTI.  The file will contain data for the 
next court date. The data provided will always be for only the next court date.
  
•
The Sheriff’s Office RMS will determine a configurable scheduled interval to retrieve 
notice of court date information from JTI.  
•
If an error occurs within the notice of court date after it is picked up by the Sheriff’s 
Office RMS, the Sheriff’s Office RMS will handle all error messaging.

Implementation of the Warrant Interface (Outbound), Quash Warrant (Outbound), and Notice of

requirements.

• All communication and data transfer to/from JTI systems will occur via HTTPS or other
encrypted channels. No cleartext transfer will be permitted.

Appendix
JTI Warrant Import Lake County, IL
Requirements Document
Version Tracking: 
Last Modified May 21 2024 by Jeff Shefke
1. Purpose
The purpose of this interface is to provide a one-way import of warrant information from 
the county’s District and Municipal Courts using JTI software. This interface will allow 
quash warrants to be updated. All warrants will be imported to a single ORI.
2. Overview
The process for importing warrants to Enterprise Public Safety’s Wants & Warrants 
module from JTI will be as follows:
1. Warrants (civil and criminal) are entered and updated in the JTI software
2. At a configurable interval (every 15 minutes) the interface will request any new 
warrant records from JTI’s web service
3. The interface will read the data and create or quash warrants in the Enterprise 
RMS (RMS) Wants & Warrants module
a. Quashes to warrants will occur only when certain conditions are met 
(detailed below) and will simply update the warrant status for further 
review.  Nothing else on the record will be updated.
3. Requirements
The interface must download warrant records from JTI
1. At a specific interval (every 15 minutes), Public Safety will call the JTI web service 
for warrant records to import 
a. Interval will be configurable in the interface settings
b. Public Safety will need appropriate permissions to access the web services 
http://ijapp1.lakeco.org:80
i. Interface will be configurable to specify the credentials required for 
the web service access (e.g. base URL, username, password, etc.), 
which will be provided by either the customer or JTI
c.
Public Safety will call the web service method WarrantIssued with no 
parameters
i. There will be one warrant sent for each charge on the case and there 
can be one or more warrants per case.
2. Only 1 ORI is needed to import warrants, and this will be configured within the 
IOE settings
3. Data received from the web service will be in XML format and will be saved to a 
network share as an XML file
a. File will contain data for one or more warrant records
i. There will always be one warrant per case offense 
4. After processing the warrant files will be moved to an archive folder

--- PAGE BREAK ---

9
b. If there is an error importing, the file will be copied to an error folder for 
further triage or reprocessing
5. If an error occurs connecting to the web service or downloading the data, the 
following will occur:
a. The error will be written to the error log
b. An email will be sent to a configurable email address containing the error 
details
6. Attempt at matching the defendant will be done using the CID
a. If a jacket is matched by CID
i. Blank Public Safety fields or fields currently populated will be 
updated from the XML file
ii. Non-blank data in Public Safety will NOT be updated with blank data 
from the XML file
b. If a jacket cannot be matched using the CID provided
iii. All persons listed in the data will follow global subject matching logic 
as described in section 3.3
1.1
The interface will import the warrant data returned from JTI into the Public Safety 
Wants and Warrants module
1. The interface will import warrant data from the XML data downloaded from the 
JTI web service
2. All warrants will be imported as new warrants
a. Warrant updates will not be processed
b. Fields to be imported are detailed in Section 4.1 below
3. All quash warrants will be imported as updates to warrants
a. Quashed warrants will use a different schema in Section 4.2 below
4. Interface will only import new warrants
a. If a warrant (Warrant Number) already exists for the ORI 
(OriginatingAgencyOri), the interface will log the details and move on to the 
next record
b. Warrants will be unique per ORI.  
5. New warrants will be assigned a default status, which will be configurable in the 
interface settings.  Status date/time will be set to the current date/time 
5. A PDF copy of the warrant will be attached to the warrant if sent from JTI
a. XML will contain a link <WarrantDocumentImage> to the path and 
filename of a PDF copy of the warrant
b. Interface will download the PDF and attach it to the warrant record as a new 
document
i. Interface will require appropriate permissions to access and download 
the file
c.
The document description in Public Safety will be set using the filename sent 
from JTI
6. All persons listed in the data will follow the global subject matching logic as 
described in section 3.5
a. If a jacket match is not found using configured criteria, a new jacket record 
will be created
7. Any error adding warrants will be processed as detailed in Section 3.7 below

--- PAGE BREAK ---

10
1.2
Addresses
1. The interface will attempt to verify an address against the Public Safety GIS Data
2. The process for parsing and verifying address data is the same logic the base 
product uses to verify addresses
3. If an address cannot be verified an overridden address will be created in the 
database
4. To be considered verified a single match must be made
5. A setting will be added to enable mapping city to a venue
a. By default, the interface will pass the address as presented to GIS for 
attempted verification
b. If the setting is enabled, a list of city-to-venue mappings can be created
i. If city is not in list, address will override 
ii. If city is in list, look to see if has been mapped to a venue and use 
mapped value.  If not, use the city as the venue.
Statute Matching
1. In order to import a warrant charge a statute is required.  In Public Safety, a statute 
is unique by the following fields: Statute Number, ORI, Crime Code, and Reference 
Number.  
2. Lake County will be responsible for providing Public Safety with the correct field 
mappings and/or possible values in order to match statutes between RMS and the 
court systems
3. If an exact field to field mapping is not possible Lake County will be responsible for 
creating alternate values behind statutes in RMS so the incoming values from the 
courts can properly reference a statute in RMS.
a. A specific usage type will be defined to allow alternates to be added for 
importing the warrant statutes
4. If a warrant contains a charge and the statute cannot be resolved the warrant 
import will fail and an error will be processed as detailed in Section 3.7 below
The interface must attempt to match data to existing global subjects
1. Global subject matching rules will be defined in Enterprise RMS (ERMS) Agency 
Settings
a. The following fields are required for global subject matching:
i. ORI
ii. Jacket Type (Adult/Juvenile/Business)
iii. First Name
iv. Last Name
v. Date of Birth (optional if SSN is selected below)
b. At least one of the following fields are also required (multiple can be selected)
i. Social Security Number (SSN)
ii. Driver’s License Number + Driver’s License State
c.
Any of the other following fields may also be used for matching:
i. Middle Name
ii. Name Suffix
iii. Sex
iv. Race
v. Local ID Number

--- PAGE BREAK ---

11
2. The interface will be configurable to specify which rules apply for matching in the 
interface from the ERMS Agency Settings page
3. If using Master Name Index, the interface will always use its associated agency when 
attempting to match jackets
4. When a match is not found, the interface will be configurable to either allow:
a. Do nothing (move on to the next jacket)
b. Create a new global subject record
5. When a single match is found, the existing global subject record identified will be 
associated with the ticket
6. When multiple matches are found, the interface will be configurable to either allow:
a. Do nothing (move on to the next jacket)
b. Selecting the first single match for the global subject record
c.
Selecting the best overall match based on the rules criteria from the ERMS 
Agency Settings for the global subject record
7. When a global subject is found, the interface will take one of the following actions 
based on a configuration value:
a. Do nothing and skip this global subject record (move on to the next jacket)
b. Update the Public Safety global subject record with non-blank data from JTI
c.
Overwrite the Public Safety global subject record with data from JTI which 
could cause some fields to be removed if the field is not sent in the data
d. Create a new global subject record
8. The interface message log will record all global subject records selected and/or 
created
1.3
The interface must attempt to match data to existing global vehicles
1. Global vehicle matching rules will be defined in Enterprise RMS (ERMS) Agency 
Settings
2. The interface will be configurable to specify which rules apply for matching in the 
interface from ERMS Agency Settings page
3. Available criteria for matching include:
a. VIN
b. Make
c.
Model
d. Color
e.
Model Year
f.
License Plate
g. License State
4. When a match is not found, the interface will be configurable to either allow:
a. Do nothing (move on to the next vehicle jacket)
b. Create a new global vehicle record
5. When a single match is found, the existing global vehicle record identified will be 
associated with the warrant
6. When multiple matches are found, the interface will be configurable to either allow:
a. Do nothing and skip this global vehicle record (move on to the next vehicle 
jacket)
b. Selecting the first single match for the global vehicle record

--- PAGE BREAK ---

12
c.
Selecting the best overall match based on the rules criteria from the ERMS 
Agency Settings for the global vehicle record
7. When a global vehicle is found, the interface will take one of the following actions 
based on a configuration value:
a. Do nothing (move on to the next vehicle jacket)
b. Update the Public Safety global vehicle record with non-blank data from JTI
c.
Overwrite the Public Safety global vehicle record with data from JTI which 
could cause some fields to be removed if the field is empty in the data 
provided
d. Create a new global vehicle record
8. The interface message log will record all global vehicle records selected and/or 
created 
Users can be notified if errors occur during the import
1. If an error occurs preventing the adding or updating of a warrant, the following will 
occur:
a. The error will be written to the interface error log
b. An email will be sent to a configurable email address(es) containing the 
following:
i. ORI
ii. Warrant number
iii. Error details

will be added for this interface.
3. Changes to field mapping may be made during implementation, pending Public

5. Assumptions and Limitations

--- PAGE BREAK ---

24
1. Interface will not delete warrants as part of the interface.  If this is necessary, the 
process will be managed manually by Lake County staff.
2. Fields passed from 3rd parties as part of an import might be longer than the 
corresponding data fields in Public Safety.  In these scenarios we will truncate the 
left side of the value as needed.  
3. Whitespace in data passed from 3rd parties as part of an import (preceding or 
trailing spaces) will be truncated as fields are imported to Public Safety
4. If validation rules for incoming data are violated, the value will be ignored, when 
possible.
5. The values of many fields in Public Safety are limited to a set of values as defined by 
validation sets or master files (ORI, statutes, personnel, etc).  These fields are 
identified in the tables below with ‘VS’
a. For these fields, the court field values must either:
i. Exactly match the values in the Public Safety dropdown
ii. Or match against alternate values entered by the customer
b. If a value sent from JTI is not an acceptable value, the value will be ignored 
and a message will be written to the log file.
6. Customer Responsibilities
The customer is responsible for providing the following items.
1. Provide all hardware and software necessary to complete the interface or 
enhancement.
2. Provide access to network resources necessary to complete the interface or 
enhancement.
3. Configure and verify connectivity between the devices, workstations, servers, and 
external networks that are required for the interface or enhancement to function 
properly.
4. Respond to requests for documents, approval, and feedback made prior to 
development within a mutually agreeable timeframe. This includes documents related 
to requirements, design, and interface functionality, such as field mappings and 
data schemas. An alternate timeframe should be discussed if additional time is 
needed to provide an adequate response. The project schedule may be delayed if 
documents required to complete development have not been provided or approved.
5. Respond to technical and business questions related to the interface or enhancement 
that arise during development within a mutually agreeable timeframe. The project 
timeline should be discussed prior to the start of development to ensure customer 
availability and awareness. An alternate timeframe should be discussed if additional 
time is needed to provide an adequate response. The project schedule may be 
delayed if questions critical to development go unanswered.
6. Act as a liaison between Tyler Technologies and third-party software vendors. The 
project schedule may be delayed if a third party is unresponsive or uncooperative.
7. Test and verify the functionality of the interface or enhancement once deployed 
within an agreed upon timeframe. The timeframe should be decided when 
deployment plans and testing strategies are discussed.

AGREEMENT #24313 
FOR PROFESSIONAL SERVICES 
For LAKE COUNTY 
This Agreement for Professional Services (“Agreement”) is between the County of Lake 
(“County”) and Journal Technologies, Inc. (“Consultant”), whose principal business address is 
915 E. 1st Street, Los Angeles, CA 90012. 
 
RECITALS 
 
1. Lake County Justice Agencies (“LCJA,” comprised of the Circuit Clerk, 19th Judicial Circuit 
Court, Public Defender, and State’s Attorney) recently implemented an Integrated Case 
Management System (“ICMS”) with Consultant. 
2. Lake County Sheriff’s Office (“LCSO”) recently implemented a new Records Management 
System (“RMS”) with another vendor. 
3. Integrating the new systems to transmit information will provide greater operational 
efficiency and improve the overall administration of justice in Lake County. 
4. Since the ICMS system was developed and is supported by Consultant, they possess the 
necessary knowledge and experience to complete the required work effectively and 
efficiently. 
5. The County and Consultant have negotiated terms under which Consultant will perform 
the Services. 
6. To memorialize the terms and conditions under which Consultant will perform the 
Services, the parties have drafted this Agreement. 
In light of the foregoing, Lake County and Consultant agree as follows: 
SECTION 1. AGREEMENT DOCUMENTS 
 
This Agreement, which includes an Exhibit A (Scope of Work), constitutes the entire agreement 
of the Parties and supersedes all previous agreements between them; except for the Software 
License, Maintenance and Support Agreement executed as of May 15, 2019. 
SECTION 2. SCOPE OF WORK 
 
Consultant will perform the scope of work set forth in Exhibit A. 
 
SECTION 3. EFFECTIVE DATE; TERM 
 
This Agreement shall be effective upon execution and shall be in effect until the work is complete, 
unless terminated under the provisions for doing so further below. The work is complete upon a 
determination of completion by Lake County, as measured against any statements of work or 
other documents or contractual terms that the parties have memorialized. A determination of 
completion shall not constitute a waiver of any rights or claims that Lake County may have or 
thereafter acquire with respect to any provision of this Agreement.

Effective Date. Unless a different effective date is provided above, this Agreement will become 
effective when all of the parties have signed it, and the date this Agreement is signed by the last 
party to sign it (as indicated by the date associated with that party’s signature) will be deemed 
the “Effective Date” of this Agreement. If a party signs but fails to date a signature, the date that 
the other party receives the signing party’s signature will be deemed to be the date that the 
signing party signed this agreement, and the other party may inscribe that date as the date 
associated with the signing party’s signature. 
 
SECTION 4. AGREEMENT PRICE 
The County will pay Consultant a fee of $20,000 for each of the three interfaces identified in 
Exhibit A, for a total not to exceed amount of $60,000. The Consultant will invoice the County 
pursuant to the milestone payment schedule listed in Section 3.2 of Exhibit A. 
SECTION 5. INVOICES & PAYMENT 
A. At the start of this Agreement, the County will issue a purchase order for the work and 
Consultant shall submit invoices detailing the products and services provided and identify 
the purchase order number on all invoices. 
 
B. Consultant shall maintain records showing the actual time its employees and agents 
devoted to the project, and the costs incurred. Consultant shall permit a representative

SECTION 6. CONTRACT MODIFICATIONS 
In the event changes to the scope of the project or additional work become necessary or desired 
(a “Modification”), the parties shall follow the procedures set forth in this Section to memorialize 
the modification (a “Contract Modification”). A Contract Modification shall be effective only if 
documented in writing, dated and signed by both parties, and expressly referencing this 
Agreement. The Contract Modification shall set forth in detail: (i) the Modification requested, (ii) 
the reason for the proposed Modification; (iii) the cost of the Modification; and (iv) the 
Modification’s impact on the time for completing the project. 
 
In the event either party desires a Modification, the Project Manager for such party shall submit 
to the other party’s Project Manager a proposed Contract Modification. If the receiving party 
does not accept the Contract Modification in writing within 10 business days, the receiving party 
shall be deemed to have rejected the Contract Modification. If the parties cannot reach 
agreement on a proposed Modification, Contractor shall nevertheless continue to render 
performance under this Agreement in accordance with its (unmodified) terms and conditions. 
Modifications that involve or increase in the amounts payable by the County may require 
execution by the County Purchasing Agent. Some increases may also require approval by the 
County Board. In cases where the Purchasing Agent’s signature is required, or where County 
Board approval is needed, the Contract Modification shall not be deemed rejected by County 
after 10 days if the County’s Project Manager has indicated in writing within the 10-day period 
an intent to present the Contract Modification for appropriate signature or approval.

SECTION 16. TERMINATION 
Lake County reserves the right to terminate this Agreement as set forth below. 
 
a. Termination for Convenience: 
Lake County reserves the right to terminate this Agreement, or any part of this 
Agreement, with or without cause, upon 30 days’ written notice. In case of such 
termination, Consultant shall be entitled to receive payment from Lake County for work 
completed to the date of termination in accordance with the terms and conditions of this 
Agreement. 
 
b. Termination Due to Material Breach: 
In the event that this Agreement is terminated due to the Consultant’s material breach, 
Lake County shall be entitled to purchase substitute items or services elsewhere at 
County’s sole expense. 
c. Termination Due to Lack of Appropriations: 
If sufficient funds are not appropriated by the Lake County Board to continue the 
services under this Agreement, then Lake County may terminate this Agreement. Lake 
County agrees to give written notice of termination to Consultant at least 30 days prior 
to the end of the last fiscal year for which appropriations were made. Lake County shall 
remit payment for all work completed and approved or accepted by the County, to the 
date of termination. Termination under this subsection shall not entitle the Consultant 
to contractual damages of any kind. 
d. Termination Due to Force Majeure Events: 
 
(i) If a Force Majeure Event prevents a party from complying with any one or more 
obligations under this agreement, that inability to comply will not constitute breach if (1) 
that party uses reasonable efforts to perform those obligations, (2) that party’s inability 
to perform those obligations is not due to its failure to (A) take reasonable measures to 
protect itself against events or circumstances of the same type as that Force Majeure 
Event or (B) develop and maintain a reasonable contingency plan to respond to events or 
circumstances of the same type as that Force Majeure Event, and (3) that party complies 
with its obligations under section 16(d)(iii), below.
